Full many a demi-score, I ween,
of Troians: but, to aid them,
From many a friendly city, men

div.note: Hermeas (Mercury) slew the hundred-eyed Argus.

Pelops came from Lydia to Greece, into "the land of Apis" (see v. 269 above), which was from him called (later than Homer) Peloponnesus, or island (peninsula) of Pelops. Previously the race of Perseus reigned in Mycenæ: Pelops was the founder of a new dynasty of which Homer here intends to record the succession.
Argos, here perhaps means Argolis, a district of Peloponnesus.

l.seg: Nine circling times of lofty Jove
Sapp'd are the timbers of our ships,
Meanwhile, I trow, our consorts dear,
Sit in the halls expecting us;
but, as ye see, the purpose
For us is unfulfill'd, for which
But come! as I the word shall speak,
who had not heard the Council.
And as, when on a cornfield deep
Dust as a cloud; and each to each
To cleanse the channels of the ships;
to lend the hand, and drag them
Down to the briny flood divine:
As home they hurried. Forth they pull'd
But that the danger Juno saw,
Say! to their native land belov'd,
Then would they for a glory leave
The Argive Helen; whom to win,
Far from their native land belov'd,
But now proceed amid the host
Nor let them launch upon the brine
Ulysses there she met, to Jove
Standing; nor forward did he stretch
His hand; sith as a pang of grief
his heart and soul had enter'd.
Say! shall ye thus, intent on home
Then would ye for a glory leave
The Argive Helen; whom to win,
Far from their native land belov'd,
But now amid the Achaian host
proceed, and from them start not,
Nor let them launch upon the brine
the goddess' voice which call'd him.
To run preparing, off his cloak
he cast; but it the herald
And with it went along the ships
Him he with gesture of respect
But tranquil sit thyself, and cause
For not yet clearly knowest thou
Nor did we all in council hear,
And troth! of such Jove-nurtur'd king
From Jove his honour is; and Jove,
But whomsoe'er of common folk he
Him with the sceptre he chastis'd,
and hear the word of others, 200,

div.note: An equipoise; for "equal." This is more than quaint. I would not defend it as literal, if I did not believe that the Greek phrase also sounded odd to an Athenian.

l.seg: Who are thy betters far; but thou
I trow, to play the monarch.
Let one be king, to whom the child
But they, from galleys and from tents,
With murmurings; as when a wave
Then did the others take their seats,
And of the host which came to Troy
none was as he so ugly.
One of his eyes was fix'd awry;
one foot of his was crippled;
His shoulders round and bunching were,
Sharp was his head; and downy hair,
For chiefly them revil'd he: now
Of copper, lo! thy tents are full;
are found, whom we the Achaians
Or gold dost need beside, which some
Whom I in bonds may lead away,
Whom thou detainest at thy will,
Home with our galleys let us go;
a man, than him far better,一
Achilles: for his prize he holds
For of the army which in Troy
I well believe, is found, than thee,
And spit reproachful words on them,
Nor yet distinctly know we all,
Many good gifts on him bestow;
But roundly will I say, and this
If e'er again I light on thee
May then the head no longer stay
But I on thee will seize, and strip
The cloak and tunic, and whate'er
And from the assembly driving thee
Send thee to champ thy grief beside
Thus spake he, and with sceptre stout
Smote him: Thersites writh'd; and quick
fell from his eyes a teardrop.
Rose on his back with stains of blood:
and down he sat, and trembled.

div.note: Troy, is often said by Homer for the district Troas; while Ilium is specially the city of Troy.
Are said to the multitude, not to Thersites.

l.seg: Tortur'd with pain, he wip'd the tear,
And thus spake one, with eye that glanc'd
Who doth in canny counsel lead,
And now hath he this best of deeds
His haughty spirit will not soon
Against the kings his spite to vent
Thus spake the crowd: but rising up,
The sceptre held; and at his side
Unto a herald like in form,
That all Achaia's sons who stood
Might hear at once the speaker's voice,
Who thus with kindly soul harangu'd,
and spake his word among them:
the Achaians seek to make thee;
Nor longer care they to fulfil
the vow, which erst they promis'd
For now, in guise as children weak,
For one, who but a single Moon
from his own wife is parted,
But nine times over, now to us
While here we stay, is passing; hence
I blame not, that the Achaians
Is it, in all case, long to stay,
Endure, my friends, and wait awhile;
Whether in truth the word divine,
For this in all our minds is fix'd-
and all of you may witness,
Who stand from fates of death exempt,
And we, beneath a planetree fair,
There did a mighty sign appear:
Crouching to hide beneath the leaves
Eight was their number; ninth was she,
There he devour'd them one by one,
The mother flitted round and round,
But by the wing, with rapid coil,
When thus the sparrow and her young
The god, who sent him forth to light,
And we in wonderment stood by,
to see the deeds which follow'd,
When thus dire monsters of the gods
Why dumb in silence are ye held,
To us hath Jove the Counsellor
Late seen, and in fulfilment late,
As did the snake the sparrow's self
In number eight, but ninth was she,
So here, by like account, shall we
But in the tenth shall we o'erpow'r
Here on the spot; until we storm
The Argives, when he thus harangu'd,

div.note: Skirl (Scotch), to sing shriekingly; related to our word Shrill.

l.seg: Whither are gone the oaths for us?
In fire should our advice be cast,
The pure libations, and the pledg'd
right hands in which we trusted?
For vainly wrangle we with words
But still, as erst, Atrides! thou,
Over the Argives play the chief
And if some one or two consult
Whether the word be true or false
Leave these to pine and vanish; sith
High on the right hand lightening,
Upon the day, when Argives erst
Therefore, let none be willing here
Before that every one have seiz'd
On sake of Helen to avenge
Or if among you one for home
Let him his hand put out to touch
That he before the others may
But prince! wise counsel take thyself,
The word, which I will speak to thee,
By tribes and brotherhoods thy men
and tribe from tribe, have succour.
If such be thy command, and if
The coward then shalt thou discern, -
And also who is brave; for each
"Troth! still thou in debate, old friend!
Would that I had, - O father
Ten counsellors who might with thee

div.note: Equal, for Adequate; a word inadmissible in this style.

l.seg: Then quickly should king Priam's city
But griefs on me are come from Jove,
Sith for a damsel's sake have I
and I in rage was foremost.
But if in counsel we again
Shall Troy postponement earn of woe,
But now to banquet come, that next
Let each man sharpen well his spear,
That all of us the livelong day
For no cessation will we give,
Now over many a breast the strap
Shall sweat, and clinging to the spear
shall many a hand be weary;
And many a courser too shall sweat,
But whomsoe'er my eye shall see
And loiter by the crested ships,
for him it were not easy
Against the dogs and ravining birds
At this harangue the Argive host
When by the rushing South impell'd,
Tho' veer the winds to every side,
And rais'd a smoke from every tent,
To diverse gods of birth eternal
But a fat bull, five years in age,
Nestor, of all the first: but prince
The two Ajaces next were call'd,
and then the son of Tydeus;

div.note: Pan-Achaia, i. e. all Achaia. He wants a word for "Greece."

l.seg: Ulysses was the sixth, to Jove
For well he knew his brother's soul,
Around the bull these stationed them,
Then amid all, the general vow
Let not the sun go sooner down
and yield the world to darkness,
And round the breast of Hector's self
By griding brass; and many a man
Into the dust thrown headlong, seize
So spake he; nor did Saturn's child
Then after pray'r was made, and they
The victims' necks they backward drew,
And parted out the thighs; but these
in double fold they cover'd them,
With fragrant fat, and over them
And these they roasted with the flame
But pierc'd the vitals, and above
the might of Vulcan held them.
Now when the joints were roasted well,
The rest they slash'd in smaller bits,
And broil'd them all right cleverly,
After the toil had found its end,
and all the feast was ready,
They banqueted, nor did their soul
of food and drink were ended,
No more debate admit we here,
Lose we the action, which the God
But come ye! to the general throng
And for ourselves, patrol we through
Thus in a troop, that quicker

div.note: Good at need: strictly, good at (the crisis of) an alarum.

l.seg: He spake; nor found the prince of men
To summon to the ranks of war
These gave the word of summoning:
kings around the son of Atreus
With costly ægis on her arm,
A hundred fringes, all of gold,
With this she glancing flash'd, and
Exciting them to tramp of war;
to toil in war and combat.
As balefully a fire may blaze
So then, as march'd the companies,
In fashion as of feather'd birds
Of geese, or cranes, or long-neck'd
Or with loud screams alight in front,
and all the mead is noisy;
So then of these the many tribes
from out the tents and galleys
From feet of horses and of men
Thus stood they on the meadow-banks
By tens of thousands, as in spring
bud forth the leaves and blossoms.
Like as the many races are
when pails with milk are wetted;
So many then upon the plain

div.note: Asia, primitively meant the delta of the river Cäyster; next, the kingdom of Croesus, bounded eastward by the Halys, or Kizil Irmak; thirdly, the kingdom of Persia was called Upper Asia; lastly, Asia was extended to include the entire eastern continent.

l.seg: Stood forth the Troians to confront,
And as, amid vast herds of goats,
when mix'd are they in pasture;
So these the leaders, parting well,
For mell of war; and mid them mov'd
in eyes and head, the semblance,
In fashion as amid the herd
stands out by far the foremost
Utter for me, ye Muses, now,
For ye in truth are goddesses,
But we a rumour only hear,
nor know we aught for certain;-
Who mid the host of Danaï
But for the multitude, not I
might tell their tale, or name them,
Not, if I had a tenfold mouth
And tho' my chest were made of brass,
and naught my voice could shatter,
Now will I all the ships rehearse,
and all their chiefs, in order.
All who in Hyria had lot,
And all who dwelt at Eleon,

div.note: In antique medallions, great breadth of shoulders is assigned to Neptune. Ares, or Mars, ought to be small in girth, as seems to be here intended.

l.seg: Or who in bright Onchestus dwelt,
And all who held Mideia's soil
All who in heav'nly Nisa dwelt
By these were fifty galleys mann'd;
and youths, six score in number,
Were by two sons of Ares rul'd,
whom in the house of Actor
To Ares fierce produc'd; but he
Sprung from the son of Naubolus,
From these a company was made
In bulk and stature not so large
But greatly smaller: slight was he,
But with the spear excell'd mid all
In Bessa and in Scarphe and
On adverse coast, with him in train
Who dwelt at Dium's fortress steep
or held their lot at Styra,
And in his train the Abantes mov'd,
Eager to burst the corslet through
With him there came a company
But she in Athens planted him
Where, as the circling years are full,
by gift of rams and bullocks
O'er these the son of Peteos,
To him not yet upon the earth
With him there came a company
And where the bands of Athens stood,
And all Achaia's youth who dwelt
Of these there came a company
But those who in Mycenae dwelt,
Or in Pellene dwelt, or held

div.note: Erechtheus, i. e. indigenous; a name denoting that the Athenian nation sprang out of the soil. "Athens" is the city of Athene.

l.seg: From these a hundred ships were led
Follow'd; and mid them shone himself
Who had their lots round Oetylus
In sixty galleys; but his bands
Urging to battle; and his heart
On sake of Helen to avenge
and stopt him from his warblings,
But they in anger marr'd his eyes
The bands of these the charioteer
Lofty and steep, beside the tomb
Hand to hand combating, are found;
and all who dwelt at Ripe,
All who at Tegea had lot
O'er these Anchaius' son bare rule,
In sixty galleys: and on board
The welldeck'd galleys gave to them,
From the tall rock of Olenus
Also to these were chieftains four;
By ten swift ships, on board of which
To this man Cteatus, to that
The third did strong Diores lead,
Over the fourth, command was held
The Echinæ, come, across the sea,
Meges the captain was of these,
Son of a chief by Jove belov'd,
And who round Samos held their lot,
All these Ulysses led, to Jove
With him there went in company
Who held their lot at Olenus,
And Chalkis, hard upon the sea,
For neither did the sons survive
Nor Oeneus'self; but death had seiz'd
To Thoas then was princely rule
And with him came a company
With them there came a company
Whom out of Ephyra he brought,
he many a town had ravag'd.
But in the palace tightly built
Scarcely was nurtur'd, when he slew
A branch of Ares, now decay'd,
Went o'er the deep a fugitive:
By other sons, and sons of sons,
Drifting at random, fill'd with pangs,
on Rhodes at length he landed:
There, tribe by tribe, three-fold they
dwelt, and greatly were they favour'd
The man of fairest form, except
But easy of despoil was he,
and scant his train of people.
But all who at Nisurus dwelt

div.note: Charopus means Blue-eyed; Aglaia, brilliancy. Such names given to the parents of Nireus are evidently mythical, if not the poet's invention. Nireus is not mentioned again in the poem.

l.seg: Two sons of Thessalus, a son
Now, as for those who habited
or had their lot in Trachis;
Of fifty galleys mann'd from these
But them, of harshly yelling war
For none stood forward in their ranks,
For at his galleys lay, divine
Swelling at heart to lose the maid,
Whom, by much toil, select he won,
When of Lyrnessus spoil he made,
and storm'd the walls of Thebe,
And cast to earth those spearmen keen
Whose sire Euenus was, and prince
So, grieving for the maid, he lay;
but soon to rise was destin'd.
But those who dwelt at Phylake
And Antron, hard upon the sea,
the earth's dark bosom held him.
His spouse with mangled cheeks remain'd
Slew, when from off his ship he leapt,
Nor yet, e'en so, unled were they, -
But them in ranks of war array'd
But of more tender age; but he
nor did, in sooth, the people
yet long'd they for their hero.
With him there came a company

div.note: Hellas, which afterwards became the name of all Greece, in Homer denotes a limited district of Thessaly.

l.seg: O'er seven galleys bare he rule,
But he upon an island lay,
There lay he anguish-struck; but soon
Nor yet, e'en so, unled were they,
But Medon did their ranks array,
All who in Tricca dwelt, and in
And with him came a company
But those who at Gyrtone dwelt
Bare on the day, on which the Beasts
Which he thrust out from Pelium
and to the Aethîkes drove them:)-
The offspring he of Caeneus' son,

div.note: Hydrus, Hydra, water-snake. The arrow that wounded Philoctetes was infected with the snake's poison.

l.seg: Of these there came a company
But two and twenty galleys swift
Him the Peraebi, firm in war,
Who fix'd their dwellings round about
For-that it streameth from the Styx,
An eager hero: these were spread
Or held their homes where Pelion
These of the Danaï were nam'd
But Muse! do thou declare for me
Both men and steeds, which companied
and vied with birds in fleetness:
Like were their manes, and like their
age; their height of equal measure.
These erst were in Pieria rear'd
Both of the gentler sex; yet they
spread fear and flight before them.
Might vie, nor with the steeds that
drew the spotless child of Peleus.
And all his people, straying loose
Took their amusement at the quoit
And arrows; while by every car

div.note: Styx, i. e. disgust: apparently a naphtha stream; supposed to flow out of hell. The gods were believed to swear by this river.
Pelion, a mountain of Thessaly, crowned with forests.
Parsley, or celery perhaps. But we are used to render it parsley, as the prize of the Nemean games.

l.seg: Tranquil: and in the princes' tents
Stroll'd and patroll'd along the camp,
But it, as fire along the fields
So then beneath the feet of these
As on they went; and speedily
the breadth of plain travérs'd they.
But Iris swift with feet of wind
And they in general mote were met
The younger and the elder men,
Then Iris swift of foot drew near,
and stood, and spake her message.
In voice she did the semblance take
sat as watcher for the Troians
To espy, if e'er the Achaian host
rush'd forth to leave their galleys.
As formerly in peace; but war
For verily I oft have gone
through many a fight of heroes,
But never people yet saw I
For troth! like to the forest-leaves
or to the sands in number,
Now move they through the plain, to fight,
and make the prize our city.
Thee, Hector! chiefly would I charge,
But men from diverse race deriv'd
Let every chieftain give the word
to those who know his guidance,
Thus spake she; nor did Hector doubt
the goddess' voice which call'd him;
But quickly he the mote dismiss'd,
and to their arms they hurried.
Then open all the gates were flung,
and out the folk came rushing,
The footmen and the horsemen both,
In front a steep and lofty knoll
Which truly is by earthly men
But by the gods, the monument
There were the Troians and allies
Hector with helm of motley work
Offspring of Priam; tribes of men
Were after him array'd for war,
Nor lonely stood he; with him stood
But those who in Zeleia dwelt
Troians of wealthy race, who drank
On whom Apollo's self of yore
These did two sons of Merops lead,
but they his word obey'd not;
For why? the Fates to gloomy death
But those who occupied their lots
a chief of men, was leader;

div.note: Myrine, an Amazon. See Book III, 189.

Motley. I hold the old-fashioned belief that the Greek aiolos is equivalent to the Latin varius, and that the verb in Odyssey, 20, 27, means variat. I see no indication that "rapidity" or "flexibility" are ever intended; qualities not found in Hector's helmet or in Ajax's shield, Iliad, 7, 222. Since even Buttmann interprets the aiola nyx of Sophocles "nox (astris) varium," i. e. spangled night, the rendering " spangled-helmed" seems admissible here, and may be adopted through the poem if any prefer it. See Note on 4, 490.

l.seg: As for the tribes of warriours,
Who dwelt upon the massy clods
All whom within its noble stream
Jove, claim'd Keas for his father.
From Axius, whose stream is shed
Where a wild breed of mules among
Men who in Sesamus had lot,
Or who on banks of Parthenus
The Mysi Chromis own'd as chief,
Nor he by arts of augury
But him Pelides, fleet of foot
Within the river, where his might
The Phryges next, with Phorcys and
born at the foot of Tmolus.
Who at Miletus dwelt, and held

div.note: The Pelasgians in Homer are never confounded with Achaians, Athenians, Arcadians, or any of those afterwards known as "Hellenes," Greeks. Argos in Thessaly was called Pelasgian (v. 681 above) in contrast to Achaian Argos, or Argolis, ix, 141.

l.seg: Nastes, who enter'd war, with gold
O simpleton! nor this at all
But him amid the river's flood

div.note: Æacides, son (or grandson) of Æacus. Achilles was son of Peleus son of Æacus.
Xanthus, in Homer, is not only this Lycian river, but is also the sacred name of Scamander, the Trojan river.

div.head: BOOK III

l.seg: The Troian ranks, like flocks of fowl,
mov'd on with noise and clatter;
As verily the scream of cranes
and by the scowl of winter,
Unto the men of Pygmy breed
And at the early morning, set
march'd all of them in silence,
some feat of arms to venture.
And as, along a mountain's tops,
mist from the South wind gathers,
To shepherds hateful, but to thief
than shades of night more friendly,
And so far as one casts a stone,
may each man see before him;

div.note: Ineffable. The Greek word literally means: "inexpressible even to gods."
Pygmy, in Greek, means "as large as one's fist." There is little doubt that the monkeys in Africa are the origin of the notion of "pygmy" men, whom the cranes attacked.

l.seg: Sothen the dustwave wreath'd in storm
As on they went: and speedily
the breadth of plain travers'd they.
When both to shorter distance came,
Foremost among the ranks of Troy
With panther skin and bending bow
And cutlass; but in double hand
made to all the Argive bravest,
With long and lofty stride, before
His joy was as a lion's joy,
For he in hungry mood, I ween,
Though hard upon his traces press
Beneath his eyes; for he, in sooth,
leapt to the ground in armour.
And back he to his comrade-troop,
As when a startled man, within
Hath sudden met a serpent's eye,
his limbs are seiz'd with tremor,
and o'er his cheek is paleness;
So then, in fear of Atreus' son,
with words of scorn address'd him:
Oh that thou never birth hadst known,
For this would I prefer for thee,
and far more gainful were it,
Than thus to be a contumely
and mark of hate for others.
Troth! loud and scornfully do laugh
Who say, that with a face so fair

div.note: Alexander (Paris) was accoutred in light armour, and quite unprepared to meet Menelaus. The poet is often obscure on the whole question, and seems to forget: thus in v. 339, Menelaus arms, as if not already full-armed.

l.seg: Must needs excel: but thou in soul
Didst thou, with such a heart as that,
And o'er the briny waters sail
And, mix'd with men of foreign soil,
from out the land of Apis;
A mighty torment to thy sire,
the town, and all the country;
In sooth 'twas wiser, not to wait
Or put the might of him to proof
For not thy harp would succour thee,
Nor tresses nor fair features, when
thou wouldst in dust be mingled.
But very cravens are the folk;
or thou, long since, in guerdon
For all the mischief thou hast wrought,
"With right, and not beyond the right,
Ever thy heart unworn doth last,
Which by the arm of him, whose skill
Grides through a plank, and mightily
Blame not in me the lovely gifts
For not to be rejected are
Which, at their pleasure, gods bestow,
and none at will may seize them.
But now, if still thou wishest me
Cause all the rest to sit at ease,
For Helen and for all her gear
in feats of war to bargain.
Let him the woman have at home
and all her gear around her.
And ye the rest, well reconcil'd
Dwell o'er the loamy fields of Troy;
He spake: and great was Hector's joy,
Along the Troian front he mov'd,
Grasping his spear midway; but all
Then many an arrowshot at him
Sent eagerly, and many a stone
For Hector, of the motley helm,
Thus when he spake, they paus'd from
fight, and quick were hush'd in silence,
To listen: then mid both the hosts
"Hear me, ye Troians! hear me too,
for whom this strife hath risen.
He biddeth that the rest of us,
But twixt the armies he himself
For Helen and for all her gear,
Let him the woman have at home
And us the rest be reconcil'd
He ended: and on either side
they all were dumb in silence.
thus spake his word among them.
"To me too listen, all of you!
for sense of wrong and outrage
Me chiefly reacheth: now at length
I trust to part the combat
sith many a woe hath pierc'd you
Out of my quarrel, and on score
But of us two whichever be
Die let him; so to you the rest
Twin lambs of adverse sex bring ye-
For Earth and heavenly Sun: but we
And fetch ye noble Priam's self,
Lest Jove's high treaties damag'd be
But if an old man interpose,
Glanceth his thought, how either side
He spake; and gladden'd by his
So drew they up their steeds in line,
And from their sides the armour stript
and on the earth reclin'd it,
Each army to the other near,
Then to the city heralds twain
Forward dispatch, two lambs to fetch,
and bear the call to Priam.
One lamb; nor he unfaithful was
Helen within her hall she found;
Which for her sake they still endur'd
Then Iris swift of foot drew near,
and stood, and spake her message:
Each upon other bare the rage
But lo! in silence sit they now,
Leaning upon their shields; and near,
In single combat shall for thee
And whoso winneth, him shalt thou
Thus spake the goddess, and within
Sweet longings for her ancient lord,
Forth from the bower hurried she,
Quickly above the gates of Troy,
Where, at the royal Priam's side,
Over the Skæan gates of Troy,
Who verily through age were slack

div.note: Skæan gates, i. e. left-hand.

l.seg: Such then upon the tower sat
And when they Helen now beheld
" For such a woman, none can
Against the Troians many a year
Still let her, e'en though such she is,
Nor here be left a pestilence
for us and for our children.
While thus they whisper'd, Priam spake,
and call'd the name of Helen:
and take thy seat before me,
To see thy former lord amid
Not upon thee the blame I cast:
the gods it is, that grieve me,
Who from the Achaians visit me
But come; declare to me by name
But nowhere yet so noble form
To him with words responsive spake
Would that I had contented been
Companion to thy son I came,
My kinsfolk and my darling girl,
But such a fate befell me not;
so now I pine with weeping.
But as for that thou askest me,
that shortly will I tell thee:
This is the elder son of Atreus,
To me the vixen-fac'd he once,
As thus she spake, the aged man
How many a bold Achaian youth
Where men with motley steeds I saw,
And Mygdon, who with gods might cope:
For I, to their alliance join'd,
When Amazons, a match for men,
Nor yet were these so many, as
Next, the old man Ulysses saw,
Shorter is he in stature, than
But broader to the sight, across
Amid the flashing rows of war,
like some tame pet, he bustles.
Unto a ram with solid wool
Who through a vast and dazzling flock
For long ago, on sake of thee,
Them did I entertain as guests
And learn'd the features of them both,
Now when amid the gather'd crowd
But of the twain, when down they sat,
Few were his words, but sweet and clear:
though he in birth was younger.
He stood, and downward cast his eyes,
His sceptre he nor forward stretch'd,
nor backward did he wield it,
Some wild fanatic he might seem,
But when at length from out his chest
No longer then might other mortal
A third time then the aged man
is that, so grand and goodly,
With lofty head and shoulders broad
Like to some god; and round him, lo!
Now all the rest distinguish I
and tell their names and titles.
Own brothers both to me were they,
Did they perchance not join the host
Or hither did they voyage take
But now in fight of warriours
So fancied she: but them already
There, in their own beloved land,
But, for the treaties of the gods,
Fruit of the earth, in skin of goat;
And standing by the aged prince,
To join them on the plain below,
In single combat for their spouse,
And whoso winneth, him the wife
and all her gear shall follow.
Then too may we, well reconcil'd
Dwell o'er the loamy fields of Troy;
So spake he: but the aged man
Then on the seat did Priam mount,
and drew to him the bridles,
And on the car so beautiful
Thus thro' the Skæans to the plain
And up the wise Ulysses rose.
wine in the bowl they mingled,
And on the hands of adverse kings
But Atreus' son with ready hand
Which by his sword's great scabbard hung,
And shear'd from head of every lamb
Parted and bare among the chiefs
And River gods, and mother Earth,
The men whose work is done,-whoe'er
Be all of you our witnesses,
Then let him keep his foreign wife
and all her gear beside her;
And we the rest will homeward go
Then let the Troians straight restore
And let them pay to us beside
And which to folk as yet unborn
and pay to me the forfeit,
Then I, abiding here, will still,
I reach the end of battle."
He spake, and thro' the victims'throats
Relax'd and helpless; for the knife
The chiefs then dipping in the bowl
took up the wine in goblets,
And to the gods of birth eternal
And thus did one or other speak
As thus the wine on earth is spilt,
so may their brains be sprinkled,
Theirs and their children's; but their wives
So prayed they; but Saturn's child
thus spake his word among them:
"Hear me, ye Troians; hear me too,
Could father's eyes endure to see
For which of these two combatants
the end of death is destin'd."
So spake the godlike man; and plac'd
Then mounted he the seat himself,
and drew to him the bridles,
And on the car so beautiful
Thus back to Ilium the twain
First measur'd out the ground for fight;
A lot for either chief, within
Shuffled and shook to see which first
Then all the peoples made their pray'r,
And thus did one or other speak
hath put this work betwixt us,
Grant that his life its refuge make
And we the rest be reconcil'd
So spake they; but with eyes revers'd
Toss'd up the morion; and swift
out leapt the lot of Paris.
Then all in either host did take
Lord of the bright-hair'd Helen, plac'd
First on his shins the dapper greaves,
He fasten'd; but, to guard his chest,
Lent him a corslet of his own;
About his shoulders next he slung
Brazen of edge; and after it
And on his gallant head he put
Last, pick'd he out a sturdy spear
When thus apart from either host
In measur'd step their posts they
There, at short distance, stood they both,
And hit the shield of Atreus' son
which equal was on all sides;
Nor might the metal force its way,
but first the point was broken,
By the bluff shield resisted. Next,
to me, and neath my hands lay prostrate
Mischief to work against a host
who op'd his doors in friendship."
He spake; and poising, forward threw
And hit the shield of Priam's son
which equal was on all sides.
Close to his side from front to back
Harmless; for quick the hero flinch'd
Thereat, the son of Atreus drew
And rising smote the helmet's ridge:
Out of his hand all aidless dropt,
Then gazing up to heaven high,
"Oh father Jove, of all the gods
For all his baseness, troth! I thought
But now within my hands the sword
Was vainly darted from my arm,
nor have I hit my foeman."
He spake, and wildly rushing, seiz'd
And twisting haul'd his foe toward
But him, around his tender neck,
Then surely had he dragged him off
And burst in twain the leathern thong
And empty in his broad hand left
Toss'd it with whirl indignant: but
Then back again with brazen lance
He rush'd, all eagerness to slay:
Helen to call: and found her
Upon a tower high; and round
The goddess seizing with her hand
the robe that breath'd of nectar,
Took Shook it, beneath the semblance hid
And greatly Helen lov'd, when she
at home he claims thy presence.
There in his bower tarries he,
and not from fight of heroes
Wouldst thou pronounce him fresh arriv'd,
Or, haply! in the dance fatigu'd,
Sne spake, and strongly did bestir
Nor yet was Helen slow to mark
And eke the neck so glorious,
She knew, and marvell'd at the sight,
If, there too, thou some darling hast
Me, hateful wight; dost therefore now
Go then, and sit thee by his side;
the path of gods forsake thou,
Nor let thy feet their courses bend
But toil for ever, him to serve;
Until he take thee for his wife,
But I, not thither wend my way, -
for troth! it were a scandal, -
lest I in wrath forsake thee,
And such be then my enmity,
as now my love is wondrous.
And lest against thee I devise,
Wrapt in a veil of dazzling white,
for why? the goddess led her.
But she, divine of women, sought
For her, the heavenly Queen of Smiles,
Carried and plac'd a double chair
Sit, with her two eyes turn'd away,
"Art come from battle? on the field
Oft was in former days thy boast
with sword and spear and prowess.
But, front to front, do thou again
But no! in sooth I counsel
"Oh lady! wound not thou my soul

div.note: Imp, i. e. graft, scion; hence in high style, offspring; as with the Greek tragedians ernos.

l.seg: Now, by Athene's aid, for once
Again shall I o'er him prevail:
For never yet so much did love
Not even, when in early day
I snatch'd thee as my prize, and sail'd
And on the isle of Cranäe
in bed and fondness held thee, -
As now the sweet desire of thee
Thus speaking, to the couch he
led, and with him went his consort;
But Atreus' son, amid the crowd,
like some wild beast was stalking,
If here he might, or there, espy
But no one of the Troians,
nor of the allies so famous,
Tho' not from tenderness, I ween,
had any spar'd to show him;
abhorr'd was he by all men.
thus spake his word among them:
Do ye then Argive Helen yield
and all her gear around her,
And which to folk as yet unborn
Atrides spake: and at his word

div.head: BOOK IV

l.seg: Now by the side of Jove the gods
Gave welcome each to each, toward
Yet they afar from him do sit
and cast but glance upon him,
The queen of Smiles, is sentinel,
And now anew hath rescued him,
Our part it is to ponder, how
and the grim cry of battle,
But if, to all of us aloft,
Let folk still throng within the walls
He spake: thereat they mutter'd deep,
Close sat they, side by side, and woes
But Juno hid not in her breast
her wrath, but thus address'd him:
"O son of Saturn, grim and dire,
How meanest thou my work to make
And all my moil and all my sweat?
Do so: but we, the other gods,
not all shall praise thy doing."
So many mischiefs work on thee,
If through the gates thou mightest pass,
And all the Troians, then mayhap
Work thou thy will against them, lest
This also will I say, and thou
am bent some town to ravage,
A town, wherein are born and rear'd
Then seek not thou to dissipate
my rage, but yield it freedom:
For I too this to thee concede,
For verily, of all the towns
Beneath the beams of yonder sun,
And Priam's self, and all the folk
For never hath my altar wanted
And fragrant fat, and streams of wine;
in all the world are dearest, -
they to thy soul are hateful:
These to defend I stand not forth
For if thy angry will I thwart,
and seek to stay their ruin,
I by the struggle nothing win;
Yet some regard should I too meet,
For I am, e'en as thou, a god:-
from the same source our being;-
And eldest born I claim to be
Eldest by birth, and rank also;
for that I hold the title
Thy wedded queen to be; and thou
I unto thee, thou unto me;
Our primacy will own. But thou
Nor did the Sire of men and gods
He by such charge Athene spurr'd,
And, speedy darting, down she came
In such aspect, as when the son
Or to some army's ample ranks;
and trails of light it flasheth;-
In such appearance, down to earth
And thus spake one, with eye that
glanc'd upon some other near him:
and the grim cry of battle
Of war and peace, will amity
So then did one or other speak
But she, in semblance as a man,
if here or there she find him.
And soon she found the warriour,
Standing: and all around were pour'd
Of men, who from Aisepus' streams
There, near before his face, she stood
Art brave enough, a speedy shaft
This would, with all the Troians,
If he might Atreus' son behold,
Apollo is not god of the sun,

div.note: Lycia-born. Some think the word means born of light. In the Iliad, Apollo is not god of the sun, nor is connected with Delos.

l.seg: When safe unto his home restor'd,
Quick he uncas'd the polish'd bow,
Which as from out a rock it came,
Hit on the chest, and back it fell
The horns that from its forehead grew
Low resting this against the ground,
Strung it; and his companions brave
Lest, ere he reach the warlike lord,
start sudden up to shield him.
But he the quiver's lid uprais'd,
Quickly did he upon the string
When safe unto thy home restor'd,
Then seiz'd the arrow at the notch
And brought the string against his breast,
But when the mighty bow was strain'd
The arch recoil'd, loud shriek'd the string,
She from the flesh the dart beat off
so far, as may a mother
Beat from her boy a fly, when he
Where golden buckles join'd, and where
There, on the girdle fitly set,
And thro' the corslet's crafty work

div.note: I venture to treat herma as equivalent to Lat. germen and gemma. In Æsch. Suppl. I think it means germ, where it is ridiculously rendered ballast. In the plural the word confessedly means jewels. Compare ernos.

l.seg: The baldric, which, for fence of darts,
yet thro' even this it pierced.
Spent in its force, the arrow-shot
And from the gash the blood straightwayin
As when some dame of Maeonis
Within her chamber stor'd it is,
Prayeth to bear it; there it waits,
Alike, a beauty to the steed,
When he from out the gash beheld
But when he saw the leathern strap
Speak with deep moan; and after him
So have the Troians wounded thee,
But verily not vain are oaths
And blood of lambs and right hands pledg'd,
have not at once fulfill'd them,
Yet will he, late of time, fulfil:
By their own heads, and by their wives
For this, in heart and soul, full sure
And Priam's self and all the folk
shall flout flaunt his gloomy ægis
Against them all. Not then, in sooth!
But anguish gaim on me shall fall
For memory of their native land
Then should we for a glory leave
The Argive Helen; while thy bones
And thus, I reckon, then shall speak
on all so wreak his vengeance,
And lo! he to his home is gone,
may the earth yawn to gulp me."
"Be of good cheer! 'tis yet too soon
Not in a mortal part is fix'd
and eke the sash and baldric,
"Oh that it so may really be,
Spread in, which haply may afford
Straight must he visit Atreus' son,
At whom some skilful archer hand
—Glory to him, but woe to us,
But sped to go along the host
Standing: and all around were pour'd
Of men, who with him companied
There, near before his face, he stood,
Quick must thou visit Atreus' son,
—Glory to him, but woe to us,
He spake, and strongly did bestir
Amid the crowd. But when they
Was wounded, and in circle thick
Were gathered, and midst of them
First would Machaon pull the shaft
But that the pointed barbs were snapt
and tangled, as he drew it.
Then from his waist unfasten'd he
But when he saw the wound, wherein
He suck'd from it the blood, and spread
The others too their armour donn'd,
Then not a-slumber hadst thou seen
Nor like a coward skulking low
For he his car with brass inlaid
Snorting and puffing, them his squire,
To him he many charges gave
at hand to have them alway,
When weariness might seize his limbs,
But he himself, on foot proceeding,
Beside them standing, thus he spake
But they who wilfully have wrought
The vultures on their tender flesh
And in our galleys we, whene'er
Shall bear their darling wives away
and doth no shame possess you?
in guise of fawns, abide ye?
As these, when they, by lengthen'd race
Stand still, nor in their empty hearts
So ye, like fawns, stand stupified,
What? wait ye, till the Troians
With ample poops aloft are haul'd
That so ye try, if Saturn's child
will stretch his hand to save you?"
Moving along the troop of men,
then came he to the Cretans,
Their chief was in the foremost ranks,
like to a boar in prowess,
While, to exhort the rearmost bands,
at sight of them was joyous,
Honour I give, alike in war,
And at the pleasant banquet, where
Mix in the bowl the sparkling wine
Drink by the portion, yet for thee,
But such as formerly thy boast,
such rouse thee now to battle."
Be, even as in olden time
But others rather stir thou up
The oaths have voided; but on them
So answer'd he; and Atreus' son
Moving along the troop of men,
Both were full-arm'd, and after them
As when a goatherd may a cloud
To him, as he apart doth stand,
and a thick squall it carries;
He sees and shudders, and his flock
In a broad shade of blue confus'd,
at sight of him was joyous,
To you no charge (for need is none)
— make I to urge your peoples;
Would that I found (O father Jove,
In all my chiefs such enterprize
Then quickly should king Priam's city
So saying, them he left behind,
Next met he Nestor, clear of voice,
Bias, and Haemon wide of sway,
In front his charioteers he plac'd,
But, for the rearguard, posted he
A bulwark of the war to be:
Full in the midst, where each perforce,
First to the charioteers he gave
Bade he to hold their steeds, nor dare
in mell of war to justle.
Let him with lengthy lance reach out:
For thus the men of former days
So did the aged man exhort,
at sight of them was joyous,
now the heart in thy dear bosom,
So did thy knees bear company,
and so thy force were steady!
leans hard. I would some other
Of men might barter it, and thou
But not to mortal men do gods
If then a stalwart youth I was,
and now doth Eld o'ertake me,
Yet with the charioteers do I
which is the old man's duty.
In fight of spear the spear to wield,
So answer'd he; and Atreus' son
He found the son of Peteos,
had heard the yell of onset;
For newly were the columns deep
And of Achaians, stirr'd to war,
so they at ease were waiting
And thou, with fox's puny heart,
Why stand ye crouching in the rear,
and wait the lead of others?
For you it seemly were to stand,
Right in the van, and bluffly meet
For of a banquet both of you
When for the counsellors a feast
There to regale on roasted flesh,
Honey-sweet wine from out the cup,
Now would ye willingly look on,
Of Argives, far in front of you,
To him with frowning glance replied
What negligence of war dost find
Then shalt thou, if thou wilt, behold,
and if such things concern thee,
Mix'd in their foremost ranks: but thou
Needless to thee my banter is,
For surely do I know, the heart
for such thy aims, as mine are.
But come, if aught amiss hath now
Will we adjust: and may the gods
So saying, them he left behind,
The son of Tydeus next he found,
Why crouchest thou and peerest far
But not to Tydeus thus, I trow,
But far in front, against the foe,
Such was their tale, who watch'd his work;
but as for me, I never
Met him nor saw him; but they say,
Once truly came he, not with war,
Who in those days would march against
And for choice helpers of the fray
Willing were we the men to grant,
Now when they were set forth and
gone far onward in their journey,
all deep in grass and rushes:
Whence Tydeus by the league was
sent to speak their common message.
He on the ambassy went forth,
Then, tho' a stranger in their walls,
No terror knew, when lonely left
But he to combats challeng'd them,
Right easily: such aid to him
and twain to them were leaders,
And Mæon, Hæmon's son, who bare
He slaughter'd all, and left but one
was once; but leaves an offspring
Worser than him in fight of men,
When thus he chided, no reply
But quick to him replied the son
For us, our boast it is to be
Us, who have storm'd the fast abode
Yet trusting to the aid of Jove
Then never place our sires with us
in the same rank of honour."
No blame have I for Agamemnon,
Who now to feats of war doth urge
For to his throne will glory great
On him too mighty grief will light,
But come, let us too bend a thought
He spake, and from his chariot
leapt to the ground in armour:
And dreadful was the clang of brass
As down he plung'd: e'en hardy souls
it might have fill'd with terror.
As when the surges of the sea,
First, in the deep it heaveth high;
With screech and roar it steepeneth,
So then the bands of Danaï,
to his own troops each leader
His orders spake, but dumbly went
So vast a train of people held
Meantime, as sheep, within the yard
While the white milk is drain'd from them,
And, grieved by their lambs' complaint,
So then along their ample host
For not to all the language was
the same, nor like their accent,
But mingled was the tongue of men
By Ares these were urged on,
By Fear, by Panic and by Strife
Who truly doth at first her crest
Planting upon the ground her feet,
She then, proceeding through the bands,
When, to encounter hasting, they were
Hides clash'd on hides, and spear on
spear, and might with might of heroes
Each upon each leant hard, and rous'd
Then rose there, all around, of men
and reek'd the earth with carnage.
Mix in the bottom of a dell
And far along the cliffs their brawl
So, when in conflict these were mix'd,

div.note: Immeasurably. I accept the strange word amoton as an older form of ametron, from a lost verb meto, Latin metor.

l.seg: The spear upon his helmet's ridge,
Reach'd to the forehead, pierc'd the
In the tough fight sheer down he fell,
Him, by the foot, Chalcodon's son,
For, watching as he dragg'd the corpse,
Beneath the buckler, as he stoop'd;
So fled his spirit. Over him,
Rose noisome onset, as of wolves,
and man by man was slaughter'd.
Then did a blooming youth fall slain
to watch the sheep, and bare him
Nor paid he to his parents dear
His life was ravish'd by the spear
On his right breast the brazen point
hit him, and through the shoulder
Pass'd cruelly; and in the dust,
there fell he, like a poplar,
Which in a marshy mead grows smooth,
For some fair car a rounded wheel;
Beside the river: so fell he
At Ajax, mid the throng;-and miss'd:

div.note: Hend, to catch (and overpower).
If any reader object to the old plural eyen, (in Thomson, eyne) he may substitute for the half line, "and night his eyes enshrouded."

Antiphus was distinguished by his corslet, as Oresbius, 5, 707, by his baldric, so as to receive an epithet from it; but its flexibility would not be visible and striking. In 215 above, the girdle of Menelaus was called aiolos, and in 135 it was daidaleos: nearly the same thing must have been meant, viz. various in hue, and complicated in work. The same word is in 3,185; 5, 295; 7,222; 12, 167 and 208; 19, 404; 22, 509.

l.seg: The gallant Leucus, in the groin,
He dropt the dead man from his hands,
and fell as stone upon him.
He thro' the foremost ranks advanc'd,
There at short distance did he stand,
and turn'd his gaze on all sides,
Then darted: back before his rush
the Troians shrank; nor aim'd he
Who from Abydus came to him,
Thro' both his temples pierc'd the brass,
So with a loud crash down he dropt,
and o'er him clang'd his armour.
Their van, and Hector brave, retir'd.
Drew up the dead, and onward rush'd:
yield not in glee of battle
To Argives: for in sooth their flesh
To bear the gashing brass: nor now
Achilles, fights; but at his ships
So from the citadel the god
Them did the glorious imp of
His ankle by a rugged stone
was maul'd: a chieftain hurl'd it,
With scrape and smash all merciless
And bone assail: back in the dust
Yet to his comrades stretch'd his hands.
And with the lance his navel pierc'd:
But at the Thracian in turn
And in his chest the weapon fix'd;
Pluck'd out the spear, and took his life,
Yet might not he the armour strip;
Who held at bay the Ætolian,

div.note: Bastard; i. e. born of an inferior wife. So 5, 69 &c. See in contrast, 6, 25. Our language has no specific terms for these distinctions.
Tritogeneia, i. e. Athene, born at the lake Tritonis.

l.seg: The chief receded: and the twain,
there in the dust were lying,
As brethren, side by side; but this,
Had any view'd those deeds at ease,
If maid Athene seiz'd his hand,
and thro' the hurly led him,
Warding the darts and thrusts of spear:
for on that day full many
Were in the dust flung side by side,

div.head: BOOK V

l.seg: that he, mid all the Argives,
About his helmet and his shield
Such fire around his head she then
And urg'd him to the midmost ranks,
Among the Troians liv'd a man,
Dares, who priest of Vulcan was,
They from the lofty car to fight,
but he on foot assail'd them.
When they to shorter distance came,
Missing him on the left: then he
But thro' the bosom pierc'd the foe,
and dash'd him from the horses.
Down, from the car so beautiful,
Nor dar'd to overstride the limbs
Nor e'en, I trow, had he himself
But Vulcan saw and rescued him,
seiz'd on the car and horses,
And to the hollow galleys bade
One skulking from the foe, and one
The soul of all held grim debate.
Seiz'd by the hand and thus with words
Were it not well for us to leave
To prove the will of Jupiter
While we, to shun the Father's wrath,
And on the high banks seated him
Then sank the heart and might of Troy:
And every leader hent his man.
Cast stately Hodius from his car,
Turning to flight; but he the spear
Right in the back, and thrust it thro'
So, with a loud crash, down he dropt,
and o'er him clang'd his armour.
From loamy Tarne come: but him,
Reach'd on right shoulder with the pike,
when he would mount his horses.
From the car's edge sheer down he fell,
stript off the dead man's armour.
For, as he fled in front of him,
Spear-famous, aiming justly, fix'd the spear
Right in the back, and thrust it thro'
So, headlong dropping, down he came,
and o'er him clang'd his armour.
Right daintily; for, him the maid
Source of annoy; which carried home
And to himself; so knew he not
Him, in the right haunch, Merion,
when by pursuit he caught him,
Had wounded; and the brazen point
So, groaning, on his knees he dropt,
Her lord to please: but Phyleus' son
Pierc'd thro' his head from back to front,
Down in the dust he dropt: his teeth
A priest was made, and by the folk,
e'en as a god, was honor'd.
Into the dust the gory limb
There crimson Death his eyen press'd,
So they of either army toil'd
But Tydeus' son might puzzle thee,
Not in the Troian ranks fought hé,
For o'er the breadth of plain he rag'd,
as when a stream is flooded,
The dams and causeys hold it not,
if rains from Jove be heavy;
And by its fury wasted lie
So by the swoop of Tydeus' son
the bands were maul'd and justled
Sweeping in rage across the plain,
Quickly he drew his bending bow,
and blood was on the corslet sprinkled.
Will long endure my stubborn shaft,
The son of Jove, from Lycia
but him the pointed shaft subdued not.
He stood, and call'd on Sthenelus,
haste! from the car dismount thee;
Thou from my shoulder now must aid
Stood by his side, and pull'd outright
Then thro' the tunic's twisted work
If ever at my father's side
In foeman's war, to me too now
Grant unto me spear-reach of him,
who hath from ambush hit me,
And glorieth that I not long
So utter'd he the word of prayer,
His limbs, both feet and hands above,
Stood by his side, and whispering,
For in thy bosom, lo! I breathe
The mist, which heretofore hath veil'd
thy eyes, — I now withdraw it,
within the throng to tempt thee,
Direct; but if, in mell of men,
But Tydeus' son again went forth
And, eager tho' before he was
Then threefold rage upon him came,
Who, to devour the woolly flock,
In the broad field: — the shepherd's dart
grazeth and doth not tame him,
But wakeneth his might the man
But plungeth mid the stalls of sheep,
Till from the deep yard leapeth he,
To one with mighty sword he smote
On collarbone; so the whole limb
from back and neck he sever'd.
Leaving them there, he straight pursued
Yet never to their sire came they,
But slaughter'd there they lay and stript
Xanthus and Thoön next he chas'd,
And rear'd no other child beside,
There did the hero lay them low
Of both; and to their father left
Sith, never did he welcome them
the neck of cow or bulkin;
So both of them did Tydeus' son
But to his comrades gave the steeds,
to drive them to the galleys.
But when Æneias saw the chief
He sped him o'er the battlefield,
if here or there he find him:
And soon he found the warriour,
And there in face of him stood forth,
and spake his word before him:
may claim to be thy better?
But raise to Jupiter thy hand,
To this strange man, who swayeth
wide with noyance to the Troians,
And who of many a gallant soul
Unless some god it haply be
To him in words responsive spake
All his outside to me is like
Whom by the shield I recognize
And by his steeds: yet be he man or god,
But, should he be the man I deem,
Not without aid divine, I trow,
Standeth by him some deathless one,
Who turn'd my pointed shaft aside,
which duly reach'd and hit him.
For I already aim'd at him;
Troth! thought I, he was headlong flung
to be a guest of Pluto,
But, ne'ertheless, I tam'd him not:
Nor now stand ready for my feet
Yet in Lycaon's halls, I weet,
Twain for each car, stand duly train'd,
when I for Troy would leave him.
He bade me mount a chariot,
and bring with me the horses,
And play the chief to Troïans
But I his counsel follow'd not,
In mercy to my gallant steeds,
So left I him, and came on foot
On archery; but fate, it seems,
meant not my shafts to aid me.
Yea, and my shot hath reached them both,
And drawn true blood from out their veins,
Down from the peg, upon that day,
But if I haply scape the war,
See with my eyes my sire and wife
my head from off me sever,
If with my hands I do not snap
And cast them in the shining fire:
for vainly do I bear them."
To him in turn spake opposite
no change of things may happen,
Ere we, against this hero match'd
But come, this car of mine ascend,
What virtue hath the breed of Tros,
Hither and thither fleet to scour
These to the city us will save,
Come, take the scourge and glossy reins,
and I will mount to brandish
The spear: else thou the spear shalt hold,
and I will tend the horses."
To him again in turn replied
Liefer will they the rounded car
flee from the son of Tydeus.
nor bear us from the battle,
Slay both of us and drive away
But thou thyself thy proper car
And him, if he invade us, I

div.note: Overhend, i. e. overtake and catch. See above, on 4, 457.

l.seg: No measure may their sinew tell:
But come, withdraw we now and mount
Rush in the van, and waste a life
To him with frowning glance replied
"Counsel not fear to me: for I
deem not thou canst persuade me.
Nor truly in my breed is it
I loathe upon the car to mount:
thus, as I am, I face them,
Not both of them, by our attack
By the swift horses off the field,
This also will I say; and thou
To slay both one and other, then
And from the Troians drive unto
For know! they are a progeny
Wherefore beneath the Dawn and Sun
From those, Anchises prince of men
Six of this race were foal'd to him
The four, he kept at home himself,
But these he to Æneias gave,
But nearer now the twain were come,
My pointed shaft subdued thee not,
'Tis well; but with the spear I now
will try, if I can hit thee."
He spake, and poising, forward threw
And hit the shield of Tydeus' son:
"Right thro' the body hath my spear
Long wilt thou last: so thou to me
But, naught dismay'd, to him replied
"It was a miss, and not a hit:
No pause of eagerness will make,
Glut with his gore the warriour-
Thus, saying, forth he threw the dart;
past the white teeth it glided;
Thro' the tongue's root it cut, until
So dropt he from the chariot,
and o'ed him clang'd his armour,
Swerv'd in alarm: but he by life
But quick with shield and lengthy lance
He, like a lion, round it strode,
And forward held his spear, and shield,
which equal was on all sides,
Full resolute to slay the man,
And horribly he yell'd. But next
which not two men might carry,
but he alone with ease did swing it.
With this he struck Æneias' hip,
just where, as on a pivot,
The thigh within the hip is hing'd;
and men the socket call it:
The rugged stone the socket crush'd,
Tearing the hero's hide: but he,
Sank to his knees; and o'er his eyes
Now would Æneias, prince of men,
But for his gentle mother's care,
On slopes of Ida bare him.
Around her bonny child she spread
Her two white arms, and o'er him
Folds of her brilliant veil stretch'd out,
a screen to bristling battle, lest

div.note: The wound seems at first impossible; but it implies that Pandarus had bowed his head suddenly forward to escape the spear, but did not stoop sufficiently.

From out the Troian ranks, unto
Beyond his other friends of youth,
to drive them: then the hero
The queen of Cyprus. Well he knew,
Troth! nor Athene is she, nor
But when he had her overhent,
So, leaping from the ground aloft,
with a slight scratch he wounded
Her hand, at bottom of the palm;
nor might her flesh resist it.
Thro' her ambrosial veil it pass'd,
And drew immortal blood; such juice,
For they no earthly viands eat,
And therefore bloodless are they all,
dropt from her hands their burden;
But bright Apollo caught him up,
If thou with battle's grim array
wilt deal, in sooth I fancy
Hereafter e'en the talk of war
He spake; and she, with sore annoy
Her, Iris swift as wind receiv'd,
and drew her from the tumult,
Fretted with throbbing pains; and all
Soon, to the left of toilsome fight,
Sitting; and on a cloud his spear
Then, sinking on her knees, did she,
and grant to me thy horses,
That to Olympus I may go,
Sorely I suffer from a stab,
She spake: and Ares gave to her
Into the car she mounted, still
And Iris mounted by her side,
And flogg'd the coursers, nothing loth
Where Iris, swift with feet of wind,
And loos'd them from the yoke, and cast
Her mother: she within her arms
And soothing her with hand and voice,
such ill hath wrought upon thee,
All wantonly, as tho' thou wert
"The son of Tydeus me hath stabb'd,
My child Æneias, who to me
far dearest is of all men.
The deadly warcry now is not
For, many a woe from mortals we,
Have suffer'd, either race on each
Once Ares learnt to suffer, when
So he for thirteen moons was kept,
And there would Ares perish now,
But that their father's dainty bride,
She by the stalwart offspring of
In the right bosom: whence she knew
What time the selfsame man, the son
Before the portal of the dead
Fretted in heart and pierc'd all thro'
Was in his sturdy shoulder fix'd,
But soon did Pæon, Heaven's leech,
And heal his wound; for not, in sooth,
Worker of guilt and hard of soul,
Is he, who grieveth by his darts
And now this son of Tydeus, whom
Hath urg'd against thee, knoweth not,
That he not long endureth, who
Nor, when he cometh from the war
Shall children, climbing on his knees,
Wherefore let Tydeus' son, tho' now

Missing her lord of early love,

div.note: Heracles, ostensibly son of Amphitryon, is also son of Jupiter, as in v. 396.

l.seg: The queen of Cyprus (as I trow)
While stroking with her slender hand
Hath scratch'd her wrist so delicate
She spake. The Sire of gods and men
"Not unto thee, my child, we give
that held his hand above him.
Nor, even so, the mighty god
Thrice did he rush against the foe,
But when a fourth time he would rush,
A peer unto the gods to be:
Of men that move upon the ground,
Thus spake the god: and Tydeus' son
But, from the crowd apart, the god
A fane was builded: there within
rais'd him to health and splendour.
and like in all his armour:
And round this form the Troïans
Then thus did bright Apollo speak
Wilt thou not chase across the field
The queen of Cyprus, on the wrist,
first with his weapon stabb'd he,
And then upon myself he rush'd,
But, mid them moving, ghastly Ares

div.note: These lines are so inconsistent with 467, 514, as to seem like an interpolation.

l.seg: How long abandon ye the folk
Till haply round the wellmade gates
But come ye! let us from the brawl
He, by such words, in every breast
Thy boast it was that thou alone,
But none of these I here, or there,
And all the fight on us doth fall,
For I myself am thy ally,
and I from far have journey'd:
For far from hence is Lycia,
Where I my dearest consort left,
and for their wives do battle.
Beware, lest thou, and Priam's self,
To men of adverse spirit be
Thee it behooveth, night and day,
And to the princes of the allies
So spake Sarpedon; and his word
deep stung the heart of Hector,
leapt to the ground in armour.
thro' all the army went he,
Then did they gather to a ball,
But eke the Achaians clos'd their ranks,
chaff by the wind is carried,
When auburn Ceres by her sieve
the crop and chaff doth sunder;
And all the ground of winnowing
Were then above with tides of dust
all white, which feet of horses
Onward and back: for round and round
But straight ahead, and hand to hand,
the battle rush'd; and round it
The ranks in all parts visiting:
To rouse the Troian heart, sithence
found none like Her to aid them.
When they beheld him thus, alive
And full of noble vigour: yet
no question did they ask him;
By Ares, pest of man, and strife
The Danaï to war were urg'd;
Nor at the force of Troïans
But firm abode they, like to clouds,
Of mountains, Saturn's child hath plac'd,
so, mid the war, the Danaï
But thro' the crowd Atrides mov'd,
And each to other bashful be
are safe, and few are slaughtered;
He spake and keenly hurl'd his spear,
And son of Pergasus: but him,
The Troians held; for keen was he
Upon his shield came down the spear
Nor might the hide resist it; but
thro' all the folds it hurried,
deep in the vitals pierc'd him.
So with a loud crash down he dropt,
and o'er him clang'd his armour.
Then of the Danaï in turn
Whose father held a wide domain
Wealthy of substance; and his birth
When these at man's estate arriv'd,
To earn renown for Atreus' sons:
and there did Death enshroud them.
But they, like to two lion-cubs,
And thro' the stables ruin spread,
Till, by the hands of men, themselves
with the sharp brass are slaughter'd:
Such did these princes fall, subdued
Lofty they fell, and prostrate lay,
And thro' the foremost ranks advanc'd,
And brandishing his spear. In sooth,
With this intent, that he, beneath
Beheld, and thro' the van advanc'd:
Vastly he fear'd, lest aught befall,
When side by side two braves he saw
So did the twain draw up the dead
On whom the dart of Atreus' son,
there, as he stood, subdued him.
just as he wheel'd his horses,
With massy stone his elbow hitting;
smote with the sword his temple.
Out of the wellwrought car he fell,
And lighting wherethe sand was deep,
stood long on crown and shoulders,
Till the two horses, moving on,
When Hector mid the ranks descried
the twain, he rush'd against them
With piercing cry; and after him
Stubborn; for now, to lead them, march'd
With Her was brawl of ruthless fray,
with Him was might of weapons.
So then did Tydeus' son recoil,
"Not without cause, O friends, do we
What sort of spearman he is prov'd,
But alway standeth near to him
And now yon Ares at his side,
But with your faces onward turn'd,
Retire ye, nor against the gods
At distance short he took his stand,
And struck the son of Selagus,
Dwelt, rich in cattle, rich in corn;
For service of ally to march
And fix'd within his tender lap
So with a loud crash down he dropt:
To strip his armour; but their darts
But he with heel advancing trod,
Out of the corpse; yet might not he
From shoulders of the dead; for sore
And of the haughty Troians he
Who scar'd and drave him to retreat,
So they of either army toil'd
a man both tall and goodly.
When they to shorter distance came,
to come and skulk in Troas?
False is their tale, who trace thy birth
For twixt those warriours and thee
Who were, in days of former men,
Such as of yore the ancient folk
Who, when, with galleys only six,
Unto these shores had drawn him, —
Deem I that thou at all wilt be,
But thro' the gates of Aïdes,
Who benefits from him had reap'd,
Nor paid the coursers as his meed,
for which from far he journey'd.
But upon thee, I promise, here
From me alight; and thou, subdued
To me a glory, and a life
The ashen shaft: of both the chiefs
Sped from their hands: Sarpedon's point
Right in the middle: thro' and thro',
With his long spear: with fury fill'd,
Deep cutting to the bone; but still,
death by his Sire was warded.
his comrades strove, to bear him
Out of the battlefield: but him
Sorely distress'd; but none took thought,
The ashen shaft from out his thigh
to pull, and aid his climbing
Into the car aloft: for care
He thereupon with mind and soul
Or from the troop of Lycians
to doom more lives to slaughter.
By the sharp brass to send below
Then Cœranus was slain by him
yet more of them had fallen,
But that it quickly drew the eye
He thro' the foremost ranks advanc'd,
Jove's son, at his approach rejoic'd,
"O son of Priam, leave me not
Here prostrate, but avenge my fall:
and then, let life desert me
Delight to my dear wife to give
He spake: but no reply came back
Past him he rush'd, all eagerness
to rout and slay the Argives.
But round Sarpedon, match for gods,
his comrades strove, and plac'd him
Beneath a fair and mighty beech
Drew from the wound the ashen shaft,
which in his thigh was planted.
His life was gone in swoon, and mist
But soon his breath came back again;
The Argives, press'd by Ares and
Then who was first, and who was last,
a sport of death and plunder
Mark'd by his baldric's motley hues;
Who close to lake Kephisis held
in their fat soil of plenty.
But when, from heaven looking down,
Then verily in vain we pledg'd
But come, let us too bend a thought
And Hebe quickly to the car
All were of brass, each had eight spokes; —
The fitted tires were all of brass,
The boxes which on either side
ran round, were both of silver.
On golden and on silver straps
the seat was swung: around it
its pole was form'd of silver.
Upon its end she bound the yoke,
golden and fair; and thro' it
Pass'd the fair golden traces; then
Whose tissue she herself had wrought
Then, in a martial tunic clad,
So on her shoulders' breadth she slung
Dreadful; which, on the rim around,
And in it Strife, and in it Might,
In it the frightful sever'd head
And on her head a four-plum'd casque
then with her feet she mounted,
And grasp'd her spear, — vast, weighty, stout;
Of heroes, whom for wrath she dooms,
But Juno keenly with the scourge
The gates of Heaven boom'd aloud,
The Hours, who hold beneath their trust
Betwixt these gates they guided clear
And found, apart from other gods,
How many and how noble men
to me a grief! but Cypris,
These have set on this frantic one,
O father, wouldest thou with me
Who beyond others aye is wont
in bitter pains to catch him."
She flogg'd the coursers, nothing loth
in flying race to speed them
In the mid regions, over Earth,
Far as across to outmost haze
So far the gods' high-neighing steeds
But when in Troas they arriv'd
And loos'd them from the car, and spread
But they, the goddesses, with track
to aid the men of Argos.
But when they were arriv'd, where men
Were closely held at bay, in guise
Shouted in tones that pierc'd as far
went to and fro in battle,
did then the steps of Troians
But now, far from their walls they fight,
She, by such words, in every breast
And in the midst she found the prince
The breadth of strap, whereon was hung
Where heat and sweat and gore were mix'd;
and all his arm was weary:
But he the clouded blood beneath
Then holding by the horses' yoke,
I see the child of Tydeus!
Tydeus was short of stature; yet,
Even when I his ardour check'd,
Or sport of martial sallies; when
Him I commanded in the halls
But he, with that stout heart of his,
such as of old he carried,
Challeng'd the youths of Cadmus' fort,
Right easily: such force to him
Also with thee, behold! I stand
close to thy side, and guard thee;
then never could I hold thee
The offspring true of Oeneus' son
To thee then promptly will I speak
my word, nor will conceal it.
But what thyself did charge on me,
Direct; but if, in mell of men,
Neither do thou that Ares fear,
In fight direct: such force to thee
But come, and guide on Ares first
And strike him hand to hand, nor feel
Who in discourse, short space ago,
But now with Troians joineth he,
Down to the ground; but Diomed,
Eager she mounted: with the load,
For dreadful was the god it bare,
Just had he slain a man, of all
Put on the casque of Aïdes,
When Ares, pest of mortals, saw
He turn'd himself away, and left
In the same spot, whereon he fell,
to lie; and, fill'd with fury,
When they to shorter distance came,
First Ares stretch'd beyond the yoke,
to win the meed of battle:
Within her hand, and thrust it off
did next to fight apply him
With brazen spear; and maid Athene
To strike the foe in lowest lap,
There did it hit; and thro' and thro'
But back she drew the spear again.
Groan'd loud as thousands nine or ten
of men who shout in battle,
From fear: so loud did Ares groan,
Such as a mist of Tartarus
Such and so huge to Tydeus' son
And pointed to the heav'nly blood,
which from the gash was streaming,
Which, to the joy of mortal men,
But thee we all attack; for thou
and each of us is subject:
But upon her, by word or act,
But givest rein; sith from thyself
Who now hath Tydeus' son impell'd, —
The queen of Cyprus, on the wrist,
first with his weapon stabb'd he;
And then upon myself he rush'd,
But me my speedy feet bare off;
There on the spot, for length of time,
Of gods, who hold Olympus, none,
as thou, to me is hateful;
For alway feud to thee is dear,
In thee thy mother's spirit lives, —
Thy mother Juno, whom by words
And by her promptings, troth! I count
But still, much longer cannot I
For-that of me a birth thou art,
— to me thy mother bare thee.
But if, from other of the gods,
Long since had I degraded thee
He spake, and unto Heaven's leech
To heal his wound; for not, in sooth,
As the white milk by curdling juice
So swiftly did the heav'nly skill
Him Hebe tended at the bath,
When thus the goddesses had stopt

l.seg: So the grim battlecry was left
This way and that, across the plain,
While either host against the foe
For he a hero struck, who mid
The spear upon his helmet's ridge
Reach'd to the forehead, pierc'd the bone;
Then did Diomedes, good at need,
The son of Teuthras, who his dwelling
and dear was he to all men;
For-that he dwelt beside the road,
Yet none of those who lov'd him well
then stood in front, and warded
The ruesome fate; but two at once
fell by the son of Tydeus,
he chas'd; whom erst a Naïd,
Who boasted in a lordly sire
His eldest child he was, but base;
by stealth his mother bare him.
Tending his sheep he met the nymph,
and won her for his bridal,
And she from his embrace bestow'd
With brazen weapon pierc'd to death
Who, by the side of Satnious,
Alive; for o'er the plain his steeds
Till in the boughs of tamarisk
Then snapping short the pole, themselves
Back to the city, where the rest
For he, their lord, beside the wheel
from out the car was tossed,
For in my wealthy father's home
Many and costly; brass and gold
From these my sire would speedily
Soon as he learns of me alive
When thus he spake, his argument
Shortly had he the captive sent,
and why this care so fruitless
For lives of men? troth! thou at home
From Troians. Then, let none of them
He spake; and by well-reason'd words
Who thrust Adrastus off from him:
By a side-stab the foe o'erthrew.
Stepping with heel upon his breast,
Then Nestor rais'd his voice aloft,
Think not of spoil, nor stay behind,
But slay the men; then tranquilly
He, by such words, in every breast
The wisest, came and spake a word
Patrol the army, form the lines,
Ere in their wives' embrace they fall,
and yield the foe a triumph.
When ye have strengthen'd all the bands,
But Hector, thou the city seek;
and with her key to open
Her sacred closet's door, and choose
the robe, which in her palace
This let her place upon the knees
And pledge the vow to consecrate
may ward the son of Tydeus,
Whom I in sooth affirm to be
and none his might may equal."
He spake; and Hector willing show'd
And from his chariot straightway leapt
thro' all the army went he,
Then did they gather to a ball,
And back the Argives drew themselves,
For some immortal seem'd to them
to drop; so quick they rallied.
Then Hector rais'd his voice aloft,
Be men, my friends! nor negligent
Whilst Ilium I seek, and urge
Then Hector of the motley helm
His bossy shield (so vast its orb)
with its black rim of leather
Then in the midst, between the hosts,
When they to shorter distance came,
"Bravest of men, what name is thine
For never have I look'd on thee
On former days; but now by far
And troth! unhappy are the sires,
But if from heaven thou art come,
Lasted not long, who strove against
For he of yore the nurses chas'd
Shed on the ground their sacred gear;
for dread from the man's raving seiz'd him.
Therefore the gods who live at ease
And Saturn's child his sight destroy'd:
fain would I shun to battle.
But if a mortal man thou art,
To him the gallant offspring of
As leaves upon the trees are born,
such is the birth of mortals.
Of leaves one brood before the wind
on earth is shed; but others
Soon from the budding forest rise,
So too the courses of mankind
But if it please thee this to learn,
then straight will I inform thee,
Who are our parents:——and with this
A city dwelleth in a nook
And Glaucus in his turn begat
On whom the heav'nly gods bestow'd
and drave him from the people.
Yet might not win the righteous heart
Then she with false and wicked words
Who tried, in service of his lust,
When from his spouse such tale he heard,
the prince was seiz'd with anger,
To slay his rival he forbore
But sent him forth to Lycia,
To show unto his consort's sire
So went he forth to Lycia
But when to Lycia he came
Nine days he entertain'd the guest,
a tenth time dawn'd upon him,
Then did he question put to him
and ask to see the token,
his daughter's lord might send him.
But when the evil marks he saw,
and knew the mind of Proetus,
First sent he him with charge to kill
Who came not from the world of men,
With lion's front and serpent's tail,
From whom the direful might of fire
And deem'd, that never fight of men
Then Amazons, a match for men,
But for his backward path the king
From the broad land of Lycia
And laid an ambush;——yet of these
For all of them the spotless man
But when the king at length in him
Of heaven, he detain'd him there,
And eke, imparted half to him
Who bare to him that match for gods,
But, when at length the spotless chief
by all the gods was hated,
Devouring his own heart and soul,—
and him I claim as parent.
He into Troas sent me forth,
and play the chief to others,
Nor to misgrace my fathers' breed,
Such is the parentage, and such
the blood, in which I glory."
Oeneus a girdle gave to him
And I, departing for the war,
for still was I an infant
At home remaining, when at Thebes
Therefore to thee a friendly host
And thou to me in Lycia,
But let us, each amid the throng,
Whom God to me may grant, and whom
Thou too enough of Argives hast
to slay, if thou be able.
But let us, each with other, change
our arms; that all who see us
May know, that, to be stranger-friends
Each of the other seiz'd the hand
And gave his gold for common brass,
the Skæan gates and beech tree,
And all around him flock'd the wives
To make inquiry for their sons,
And for their husbands. He, in turn,
All to entreat the gods: but grief
was held in store for many.
But when at Priam's gorgeous house,
He had arriv'd, wherein were rang'd
All side by side; where Priam's sons
Under the roof, all side by side,
Where slept, beside their wives august,
And closely did she press his hand,
"And why, my child, thus comest thou,
Sorely, I guess, outwear the folk;
To come and raise thy hands to Jove
But stay, and let me bring thee wine.
Shalt thou libations make to Jove
shalt by the draught be strengthen'd.
As wearièd art thou, my son,
Great Hector of the motley helm
Lest thou my limbs unnerve, and steal
It shameth me, the sparkling wine

div.note: Ephyra; here, for Corinth. In 2, 659, is another Ephyra.
Engraving,—scratching or painting. It is still disputed, whether picture-writing like the Mexican, is intended, or rather, under poetical phrases, alphabetic writing.
Blameless seems here opposed to the forbidden arts of sorcery, magic, &c. So 9, 128.
Chimæra, in Greek simply means a She-goat, and is so used in v. 181.
Sudden deaths, especially of women, are attributed to the arrows of Artemis. See 428, and 19, 59.

Oeneus,—father of Tydeus father of Diomedes. Meleager (2, 642) was successor to his father Oeneus in Aetolia: his brother Tydeus married a daughter of Adrastus, king of Argos (and of Sicyon, 2, 572) son of Taläus (2, 566). Hence Diomedes succeeded to the principality of Argos, though his father was an Aetolian (4, 399).

to slay, if thou be able.

our arms; that all who see us
May know, that, to be stranger-friends
Each of the other seiz'd the hand
And gave his gold for common brass,
the Skæan gates and beech tree,
And all around him flock'd the wives
To make inquiry for their sons,
And for their husbands. He, in turn,
All to entreat the gods: but grief
was held in store for many.
But when at Priam's gorgeous house,
He had arriv'd, wherein were rang'd
All side by side; where Priam's sons
Under the roof, all side by side,
Where slept, beside their wives august,
And closely did she press his hand,
"And why, my child, thus comest thou,
Sorely, I guess, outwear the folk;
To come and raise thy hands to Jove
But stay, and let me bring thee wine.
Shalt thou libations make to Jove
shalt by the draught be strengthen'd.
As wearièd art thou, my son,
Great Hector of the motley helm
Lest thou my limbs unnerve, and steal
It shameth me, the sparkling wine
To Jupiter; nor may a man
But thou with gifts of incense seek
Out of thy sacred closet choose
the robe, which in thy palace
This do thou place upon the knees
And pledge thy vow to consecrate
if that she deign to pity
may ward the son of Tydeus,
Do thou, within her fane, approach
He will to feel reproaches. Oh!
that Earth might yawn to gulp him!
And all his sons and all his folk
If to the house of Aïdes
Seemeth, my mind would then be rid
He spake. Then she, unto her halls
Himself from Sidon brought to her,
In that emprize of voyage, which
Of these did Hecuba take one,
Like to a star its brilliance was;
Then forth she hied; and after her
But when Athene's fane they reach'd
To them the doors were opened by
Whose sire was Kisseus, but her lord
For-that the Troians her had set
Then all, with trilling cry, their hands
And to the imp of mighty Jove
And cast before the Skæan gates
So do we vow to consecrate
if that thou deign to pity
So utter'd she the vow; nor gain'd
Thus to the child of mighty Jove
But Hector, he meanwhile had reach'd
by men, who then were counted
Of all in loamy Troas' land
Who built for him a spacious hall,
To Priam's self and Hector near,
and in his hand he wielded
A lengthy spear, which measur'd ells
Glitter'd its brazen head, whose neck
But he his brother found, employ'd
Giving to every hand its task
with words of blame address'd him.
The people, round the lofty wall
Perish. On score of thee, behold!
Abroad on every side; and thou
If any thou didst haply see
But up and act; lest soon the town
"With right, and not beyond the right,
To thee then will I speak: but thou,
Within my bower here sat I,
Nor scorn of Troians; but, I wish'd
But now by force of gentle words
Into the war to hurry: yea,
But wait thou at my side, while I
Or go, and I will follow thee,
and think I shall o'ertake thee."
He spake, but no reply came back
To whom with soothing accents spake
"O, brother thou of me, who am
First gave me light, an evil squall
of rushing wind had borne me
Into the dells of mountain beasts,
There would the wave have swept me off,
ere deeds of woe were compass'd.
But tho' it pleas'd the gods above
I might have chosen me at least
But as for this one, neither now
his mind is firm, nor ever
But enter now, and in this chair
take thou a seat beside me,
My brother! since on thee in chief
For me, the vixen of my sex,
On both of whom hath Jupiter
To men of aftertime shall yield
Great Hector of the motley helm
My heart already o'er the field
Rather, do thou thy husband rouse,
That me he shortly may rejoin,
For to my home must I too go,
My loved wife and infant boy:
for none can know the future,
I yet may see their faces,
the gods may lay me prostrate."
Then Hector of the motley helm
But to a tower gone was she,
Bearing her infant: there she stood,
But Hector, soon as he within
thence, he call'd to the domestics:
went forth from out the palace.
Doth she the mansion haply seek
Or went she to Athene's fane,
To him the keeper of his house,
the very truth to tell thee,
Neither doth she the mansion seek
Nor went she to Athene's fane,
But to a tower forth she went
The Troians worsted were, and great
So she unto the outmost wall
to see the war hath hurried,
In frantic fashion; and the nurse
So spake the chief domestic; then
forth from his house did Hector
Haste on the selfsame path again,
When at the gates he was arriv'd,
The Skæan gates;——for there would he
pass out to join the army;——
His daughter was in wedlock held
And met him now: along with her
Bearing within her arms the child,
Like to a brightly twinkling star,

div.note: Glorious is explained by 3, 126-8.
Warmed is a bitter joke for Burned: so elsewhere.
Chair, a double-chair, as 3, 425.

Hypo-Placan, i. e. under Placus. On Thebe see 1, 366. It must not be confounded with Bœotian Thebes, which Diomedes and his confederacy destroyed, 4, 406; and of which Hypothebe, 2, 505, was an outpost.
Astyanax, prince of the city.

l.seg: The father cast upon his boy
a glance, and smil'd in silence:
And closely did she press his hand,
thy very might will kill thee;
Who soon shall be despoil'd of thee:
Soon shall the Achaians slay thee:
but, for me, of thee bereaved,
Better were it, below the earth
None will remain, if thou by fate
But only griefs: for, father none
For Achileus, a match for gods,
but when he slew their chieftain,
