The bloody corpse despoil'd he not
But burn'd it, even as it was,
and round it elms were planted
But those who in my father's halls
They in a single day went all
down to the house of Pluto;
For all of them divine Achilles
When they the snowy sheep would guard
And for my mother; queen was she,
And her he brought away with him,
But Hector, thou my father art
Thou art to me for mother's son,
But come now, pity me; and here
Nor doom thy child to orphanhood,
and make thy wife a widow.
By the wild figtree post the bands
and where the wall was mounted.
For thrice have here the bravest foes
Whether-that one gave hint to them,
Great Hector of the motley helm
I shunn'd the war: nor doth my heart
allow it; but hath taught me
And guard my father's great renown
For this, in heart and soul, full sure
I know; a day is coming,
And Priam's self, and all the folk
But not so much for Troïans,
Or for my many brothers brave,
who, by the hands of foemen,
none of these doth anguish rend me,
So, as for thee: whom haply one
Despite thy tears, shall lead away
spoil'd of thy day of freedom:
Then for a foreign mistress thou
Bear water; sore against thy will:
but stern constraint will force thee.
And haply one may say, who sees
a tear from thee to trickle ;——
'This is the wife of Hector, who,
When rag'd the war round Ilium,
So may one say hereafter; then,
in lack of such a husband
To ward the day of slavery,
on thee will fall new sorrow.
But oh, may death my senses shut,
and piles of earth conceal me,
Ere-that I see thee dragg'd away
and hear thy shriek of anguish."
But back into the bosom of
The child recoil'd with wailing, scar'd
the brass, and crest of horsehair,
Which from the helmet's topmost ridge
Then did his tender father laugh,
Then pois'd his little son aloft,
and dandled him, and kiss'd him,
And rais'd a pray'r to Jupiter
grant ye, that this my infant
soon may become his father's like,
And when from battle he returns,
'Far greater than his sire is he';
and may he with him carry
The gory trophies of a foe,
Thus saying, in the mother's arms
And she her own dear child receiv'd
Laughing amid her tears: the which
And soothing her with hand and voice,
From the first day he saw the light,
But thou, returning to thy house,
to thine own work betake thee,
but men must care for battle,——
All, who in Ilium are born,
So gallant Hector spake, and took
But his dear partner, leaving him,
With many a turn and lingering,
Soon at the mansion nobly plac'd
Did she arrive, and found within
Aged and young; and in them all
she wak'd a love of sorrow.
So they, for Hector still alive,
in his own home were wailing.
For, sooth, they counted not that he,
Would look on them again, escap'd
from hands and might of Argos.
Nor long did Paris stay behind
But he his glorious armour donn'd,
As when a charger, closely kept,
Bursting his halter, o'er the plain
Accustom'd in the flood to bathe
his head, and off his shoulders
Rusheth the mane abroad; and he
Lightly his knees then carry him
So from the highth of Pergamus
Who there still linger'd, where with him
and I in sooth detain thee
Him Hector of the motley helm
Thy deeds of battle may despise;
I hear from Troians, who endure
on score of thee much travail.
But go we: afterward will we
Jove grant, that we unto the gods
with wine the bowl of freedom,
When we from Troas have expell'd

div.note: Messeïs, Hypereia,—two fountains; see 2, 734.

div.head: BOOK VII

l.seg: After such parley, from the gates
A wafting breeze, what time as they
and toil their limbs hath loosen'd;
So then the brothers twain appear'd
Right thro' the neck with pointed lance,
Would leap; but down to earth he fell,
and there his limbs were loosen'd.
But when, from heaven looking down,
As high from Pergamus he gaz'd
So did the twain from adverse part
And her the prince, the son of Jove,
and why dost thou so eager
To Argives willest thou to give
But if my counsel thou regard
be found; if thus is pleasant
to storm and rieve the city."
But tell me, how designest thou
to stay the war of heroes?"
To her the prince, the son of Jove,
he, one to one, make challenge
Then close to Hector's side he stood,
and spake his word of counsel :
Wouldest thou haply hark to me?
and sooth, am I thy brother.
Cause all the rest to sit at ease,
But from the Achaian host do thou
For not yet is thy destiny,
that death and fate shall hend thee;
He spake; and great was Hector's joy
Along the Troian front he mov'd
Grasping his spear midway; but all
Over the armies took their seats,
Upon the lofty beech of Jove
Where amid shields and waving plumes
As when the Westwind rising new
and streaks the sea with darkness;
So were the bright unmoving hosts
Over the plain. Then Hector spake
"Hear me, ye Troians! hear me too,
That I the counsel may declare
But bodeth on the double host
Till either ye the ramparts gain
Or we your bravery lay low
Now then, whome'er his soul may bid
to join in fight against me,
Such terms of battle do I bear;
If with the lengthy edge of brass
Let him my armour strip, and bear
But let him give my body back
From Troian men and wives of Troy
in rightful flames may shrive me.
But if Apollo aid my vow
And hang it on the temple-walls
But will his corpse restore, to bear
So shall he find meet burial
Who hard by Helle's brackish flood,
shall heap for him a barrow.
Shall say: 'Behold the sign of one
So shall one say hereafter: then,
they all were dumb in silence:
After long pause, at length stood up
And spake with words of contumely,
If no one now of Danaï
But oh! may all of you, who here
thus side by side are seated
be turn'd to earth and water!
And I myself against this man
will arm me: but in heaven
The prize of victory is judg'd
Had not the chieftains of Achaia
By the right hand his brother seiz'd,
For thee or us: refrain thyself,
a man than thee far stronger,
Did shudder to confront his might
Hie thee away and take thy seat
And for this man the Argives soon
Be he intrepid as he may
Yet do I deem, right gladly he
his limbs will rest, if safely
He spake, and by wellreason'd words
Who yielded and forbare to strive.
But Nestor to the Argives rose,
and spake his word among them :
How deep would groan the aged heart
Who in his own abode of yore
And ask'd of me the parentage
Now, if he heard that all of these
do quail at sight of Hector,
He his dear hands full oft would raise
That from his limbs his soul might go
Would that I had (O father Jove,
Such prowess of my youth, as when
By a new name did signalize,
For-that he not with lengthy lance
Him did Lycurgus kill by guile,
Within a narrow path, where naught
Death to avert; for starting up
Right thro' his body thrust the spear
and down to hard earth dash'd him,
And from the corpse the armour stript,
In this, himself thenceforth was clad
And troth! they trembled with alarm,
and none stood forth to front him:
Against his vaunting: yet was I
of all by birth the youngest :
But I his brunt encounter'd, and
So slew I then a warriour
For plentiful in bulk he lay
Oh that I still were thus in youth,
But ye, who glory in the name
To meet the brunt of Hector, now,
not one of you is eager."
Thus spake the old man scornfully,
rose far of all the foremost,
And after him the son of Tydeus,
And next the two Ajaces rose,
"Now thro' your whole list cast the lot
For he, with service good, will aid
Yea, and himself to his own life
When thus he spake, each hero mark'd
And cast them in the morion
Then all the peoples made their pray'r,
And gazing to the vasty sky,
Or to himself the stately king
And shook within the casque: then out
The lot of Ajax: mid the crowd
Bare and display'd it unto all
But each in turn rejected it
But when through all that company
he pass'd, and reach'd the hero,
and cast it in the helmet,
Then to the herald's hand outstretch'd
And knew the token of his lot,
and in his soul was joyful.
It to the ground before his feet
he flung, and straight address'd them:
"My friends! the lot, full sure, is mine:
and all my soul within me
But come! do ye, meanwhile that I
Or even with loud voices; sith
of none do fears possess us.
For no one, fierce in wilfulness,
By force; nor yet by skill of arms:
for 'tis my hope that I too
Was born and bred in Salamis
So spake he; then to princely Jove,
And gazing to the vasty sky
Grant thou that Ajax carry off
They pray'd: but he in dazzling brass
And when around his tender skin
he all his gear had fitted,
Forward he hurried, such in guise
Moving to war in chase of men,
Hath match'd to fight in bitterness
and with his feet beneath him
And e'en to Hector's self the heart
Yet now no longer choice to him
was left, to shrink or shuffle,

div.note: Brackish; the word also, and indeed generally, means Broad. Each rendering is explained by supposing that Homer regarded the Hellespont (or Dardanelles) as a river and not a sea.
A far more delicate excuse would have been, that Menelaus had recently fought; but Agamemnon is too agitated to be delicate.

l.seg: Then Ajax nearer came, his shield
Which Tychius for him had wrought
and held his home at Hyle,
And made for him the motley frame
and cas'd with brass the seventh.
Bearing this shield before his breast,
Stood near to Hector, front to front,
and spake a word of menace :
now shalt thou learn to surety,
But he, withdrawn within his own
But we, the rest, may still suffice
And many is our crew: but come,
Tamper not thou with me, as tho'
But troth! wellvers'd am I in fight
Toward the right, toward the left,
I know to wield the bullhide
Amid the race of scouring mares
I know to press the hurtle,
And in the standing-fight I know
But, being what thou art, I fain
Not by sly peering, but in front,
if haply I may hit thee."
He spake, and poising, forward threw
Upon its outmost edge of brass,
which spread as eighth upon it.
Thro' the six folds the stubborn point
But in the seventh fold the hide
And hit the shield of Priam's son,
which equal was on all sides.
And thro' the corslet's crafty work
Close to his side from front to back
Harmless: for quick the hero flinch'd,
Then with the hand did each of them
And clos'd in fight anew, in guise
The son of Priam with his spear
Nor might the metal force its way;
but first the point was broken.
Then Ajax with a mighty bound
smote on the shield, and thro' it
Drove deep and far his javelin,
And reach'd his neck with force to gash;
and out the dark blood spouted.
But he, retiring on the plain,
A stone, which there behind him lay,
And with it hit the seven hides,
Full in the centre of the boss;——
and round it rung the metal.
The massy weight, like stone of mill,
Nor might his knees resist its swing.
Cast on his back, and prostrate
He fell, with shield against him press'd:
Then in close combat of the sword
But that the heralds, who from Jove
Were present, one from Troians, one
And they betwixt the twain held out
A herald sage in canny thought,
in feats of war to combat:
to both of you hath favour,
And both of you are warriours;
of which we all have sample.
But now arriveth Night: to Night
For he it was, to hero-glee
Let him commence; then gladly I
"O Ajax, sith as God to thee
both size and force hath given
And cunning, and to wield the spear
This day; but afterward will we
For now arriveth Night: to Night
So all the Achaian host shalt thou
Those chiefly who are near and dear,
Whilst I, within the mighty wall
Bring gladness to the men of Troy
to win my life of Heaven.
But come now; let us mutual
That thus hereafter each may say
Thus as he spake, he gave to him
But Ajax offer'd him in turn
So parted then the twain: and one
The other hasted to the crowd
of Troy: and these were joyful,
When they beheld him thus, alive
So up the city led they him
But Ajax, him on other side
But when within the royal tent
For them a bull five years in age
They kill'd and gash'd and skinn'd and truss'd,
And nicely slash'd the smaller bits,
And broil'd them all right cleverly,
After the toil had found its end,
and all the feast was ready,
They banqueted, nor did their soul
Yielded the chine, from end to end,
of food and drink were ended,
Foremost of all the aged man
Who thus with kindly soul harangu'd,
and spake his word among them:
"O Atreus' son, and ye the rest,
Sith as we weep for many lives
Whose dusky blood is spilt, beside
By eager Ares, while their souls
Thee it behoveth, with the dawn
with steers and mules from all sides
Hither will bring the valiant dead;
then will we burn the bodies
Anent the galleys, high at hand;
Each to the children of the slain
may bear their bones and ashes.
And we, around the fires of death,
And in the ramparts, eke may we
Thro' which our charioteers may drive
And let us on the outer side
deep dig a moat beneath it,
Which will along the camp protect
When thus he counsell'd, all the kings
To them, a leader of harangue
That I the counsel may declare
Come, let us Argive Helen give
and all her gear around her,
For Atreus' sons to bear away:
That thus we may not deal, do I
So spake the sage, and down he sat;
The lord of bright-hair'd Helen rose,
But if of very truth suchwise
Then surely have the gods themselves
And roundly speak it out; that I
But all her gear, whate'er I brought
All that will I give back, and eke
from my own stores increase it."
So spake the prince, and down he sat;
Who thus with kindly soul harangu'd,
and spake his word among them:
And hold remembrance of the watch,
That I the counsel may declare
Now in the city, as afore,
let each man take his supper,
And hold remembrance of the watch,
But let Idæus go at dawn
for whom this strife hath risen,
And add the prudent word hereto,
To stay them from the din of war,
He spake, and they all duteous
[Along the host, by rank and rank,
they took each man his supper;]
And with the dawn Idæus went
And there he found the Danaï,
Then, standing in the midst, to them
"Oh Atreus' son, and ye the rest,
A charge I bear from Priam's self
To speak (if so to all of you
for whom this strife hath risen.
Of wares and equipage to Troy;
All this will he give back, and eke
from his own stores increase it,
But her, the wife of early love
No wise doth he restore: but sooth!
Also I bear the prudent word,
To stay you from the din of war,
they all were dumb in silence,
Nor Helen's self: for even one
May know, that Ruin's meshes are
So spake he; and Achaia's sons
How they reply: and for myself,
I too thus find it seemly.
But as regards the slaughter'd dead,
I grudge thee not to burn them.
For when a man is reft of life
and death has seiz'd his body,
No one can stingy be to him,
in soothing fires to shrive it.
But to the oaths let Jove give heed,
Thus having said, aloft he rais'd
to all the gods his sceptre;
Stood in the midst, and spake his tale:
Alike, the corpses in to bear,
So, from their galleys tightly plank'd,
Were urgent to bear-in the dead,
the Sun his beams was casting,
Forth risen from the waters deep
Into the steep of heaven, when
Then hard was it to recognize
But, warm tears dropping for the slain,
Grieving with inward heart, did heap
So likewise on the other side
Grieving with inward heart, did heap
And to their hollow galleys back,
When not as yet the dawn was come,
but still the night was doubtful,
And they, beside the fires of death,
And in the ramparts, eke did they
Thro' which the charioteers might drive
So did they, on the outer side,
deep dig a moat beneath it,
Ample and broad, with pointed stakes
Such were the toils which occupied
And gaz'd upon the mighty work
Then Neptune, shaker of the earth,
A rampart there have built, and dug
And far abroad as Dawn is shed,
so wide shall be its glory;
"Good Spirits! what a word was that,
Some other of the gods mayhap
One who than thee was weaker far
But far abroad as Dawn is shed,
so wide shall be thy glory.
But now, go to! as soon as e'er
Do thou break up this rampart huge,
and sweep it all to ocean,
And once again the mighty shore
So shall the vast Achaian toil
Down sank the sun; yet cannily
And oxen slew amid their tents,
But galleys many stood at hand
Sent forward by the prudent mind
Did Jason's son a present send,
Then for supply of wine went down
Or some with slaves, as barter-price;
Yet, all night long, for them was Jove
Oft from their cups they spilt the wine;
nor any dar'd to drain them,
Till with libation he should greet
they took the gift of slumber.

div.note: Them, seems to mean the Achaians.

div.head: BOOK VIII

l.seg: o'er all the earth was scatter'd,
Silent then listen'd all the gods,
That I the counsel may declare
Whomso of all the blessed gods
Back to Olympus shall he haste
quick will I catch and fling him,
Into far distance, where the pit
Where brazen all the pavement is,
Then shall ye know, how far am I
of all the gods the strongest.
Or come, adventure, all ye gods!
so may ye surely learn it.
When I a golden chain have dropt
Lay hold, and throw your force on it,
all gods, both male and female!
Yet never shall ye down to earth
e'en though ye toil your utmost.
But after this, should I in turn
Aloft I draw the Earth itself,
and Sea, and all within them.
Then might I bind the chain around
to swing and wait my pleasure.
Thus did he charge them: thereupon
they all were dumb in silence,
After long pause, at length replied
Right well are we aware ourselves,
we none the less have sorrow,
Now from the war, in truth, will we
But counsel to the Argives still
not with a soul so earnest
Have I harangu'd; and fain would I
to thee, my child, be gentle."
So spake he; then beneath the yoke
Brazen of foot and swift to fly,
He on his own immortal skin
with gold was clad; and grasping
on his own seat he mounted,
And flogg'd to drive them, nothing loth
In the mid regions, over Earth
There did the Sire of gods and mortals
And loos'd them from the car, and spread
Quickly amid their tents, from it
Fewer in numbers; yet their heart,
e'en so, was bent on struggle,
Then open all the gates were flung,
and out the folk came rushing,
The footmen and the horsemen both,
Hides clash'd on hides and spear on spear,
and might with might of heroes
Each upon each leant hard, and rous'd
Then rose there, all around, of men
and reek'd the earth with carnage.
While that the morning lasted still
So long from either side the darts
had touch'd his noon of heaven,
two fates he cast within it,
He rais'd the scales; then tilting fell
The fates of Troas mounted high
And he, unwilling: but his horse
Takes its first growth from out the skull;
Into the brain the shaft had pierc'd:
And writhing round the pointed barbs,
While the old man, with cutlass hasting,
Which, at the side, entangled him,
Whither, as coward in a crowd,
dost turn the back on battle?
Beware, lest, as thou fleest, one
But wait, till from the aged man
He spake; nor did divine Ulysses,
But rushing onward, pass'd to reach
Then Tydeus' son, tho' left alone,
Thy force is languid; Eld to thee
But come, this car of mine ascend,
What virtue hath the breed of Tros,
Hither and thither fleet to scour,
Which from Æneias once I took,
To these then let our squires attend,
but we will on the others
Straight at the Troians coursertaming hold our course; that Hector
May learn, if haply eke to me
Thus when he spake, the charioteer
Squires of the princes, took in charge
the car and steeds of Nestor;
But on the car of Diomedes
Forthwith did Nestor in his hands
Eager and straight he came; but quick
It miss'd the mark, and hit him not;
but struck the squire beside him,
Him, as the horses' reins he held,
So dropt he from the chariot :
Swerv'd with alarm: but he by life
Yet left he him, there as he was,
to lie, tho' for his comrade
Inly distraught; but look'd to find
Long did the steeds a ruler lack;
Call'd up beside him, and the reins
Then deeds beyond repair had been,
Yea, and the foe in Ilium,
Had not the Sire of gods and mortals
he threw a bolt of lightning,
All blazing on the ground, before
And dreadfully the flame shot up
Straightway did Nestor from his hands
And, with a soul by fear subdued,
"Come, son of Tydeus, turn to flight
to us, if so he will it,
Shall he bestow: no human force
Only, my heart and soul are here
For Hector in harangue will say
'The son of Tydeus fled from me
So will he boast hereafter: then,
may the earth yawn to gulp me!"
To him in turn the charioteer
If ever Hector thee shall call
Nor will the wives of buckler-wielding
Who know too well, that thou in dust
Upon the word, he turn'd to flight
"O son of Tydeus, thee of yore
With seat, with dainties, and full cups,
But now will they dishonour thee,
Shalt thou set foot and I give way;
nor shalt thou bear our women
When thus he vaunted, Tydeus' son
Longing to wheel his horses round
Thrice did he hold with mind and soul
And thrice did Jove the Counsellor
And to the Troians token gave
But Hector rais'd his voice aloft
Be men, my friends! nor negligent
O simpletons! who now with toil
Feeble and nothing worth: for ne'er
And lightly will my steeds o'erleap
the moat that deep is digged.
But at what time I reach beside
That I with fire may kindle well
All scar'd and blinded by the smoke,
After this word, he call'd aloud,
to cheer and urge his horses:
For, the heartsoothing wheat to you
And wine she mingled for your drink,
Sooner than e'en to me, who vaunt
by speed, that we may capture
How that the guides and shield itself
Would we from off the shoulders strip
Well might I hope, if both of these
Before the night is gone, would mount
When thus he vaunted, shame and rage
Bestraught, she mov'd upon her throne,
The soul within thy breast beweep
Yet gifts to thee they consecrate
Many and pleasant: thou in turn
For if we chose,——all we, by whom
Alone on Ida might he sit,
Never would I, that we the rest
with Jove the son of Saturn
Contend; not e'en by force combin'd:
Meanwhile the space from galley's poop
Was fill'd with crowds of fugitives,
Driven to strait; and Hector's self,
the son of Priam, drave them,
when Jove to glory rais'd him.
Now had he burnt with wasting fire
But that the queenly Juno mov'd
He hied and hurried, passing on
And to Achilles' bands, which haul'd
Last on the strand, on bravery
O shame, ye men of Argos!
Whither are gone the boasts, wherein
we said that we were bravest?
O empty vaunters; ye who once
And emptying the bowls, with wine
That every one would stand in war,
Or for two hundred: now are we
Who shortly will with wasting fire
O father Jove, didst e'er before
Yet, hither roaming to my woe
No beauteous altar yet of thine
burnt fat and thighs of oxen,
But Jupiter! this wish at least
Let but my people's lives and limbs
When thus he spake, the Sire was griev'd
Assent he nodded, that the host
Forthwith an eagle sent he forth,
Which held a swift hind's progeny,
And by a beauteous altar's side,
Jove, source of every mystic Voice,
But they, discerning that from Him
had come the bird of omen,
Might boast, in front of Tydeus' son,
And drive beyond the moat his steeds,
But far the foremost, cast he down
who turn'd to flight his horses ;
But as he turn'd, Tydides fix'd
Right in his back, and thrust it thro',
So from the chariot he dropt,
and o'er him clang'd his armour.
And upon these the Ajaces twain,
The ninth was Teucer, twanging oft
Who shelter'd him beneath the screen
Then Ajax slowly would his shield
Peering around, if in the crowd
Hit one or other, left him there
But he returning, like a child
Then who was first of Troïans
All these successive cast he down
And close beside him took his stand,
Shoot thus, and to the Danaï
And, meaner tho' thy birth, within
Now, in far distance tho' he is,
do thou on glory mount him.
But unto thee so much I say,
After myself, thy hand shall first
A tripod, or a pair of steeds
Or a fair woman, suited well
in common bed to meet thee."
Never stop I: but syne as first
Thenceforth in ambush with my bow
I wait, and slay the foeman.
E'en now have issued from my string
And all within the limbs are fix'd
Only to hit this raving dog
as yet my art hath fail'd me."
So answer'd he, and from the string
In purpose straight at Hector aim'd;
for much he long'd to hit him.
But of his mark he miss'd, and struck
Whom erst a mother bare to light,
In form like to the goddesses,
And as a poppy leans aside
its head, which in a garden
Surcharg'd with its own fulness is
So did he bend his head aside,
But Teucer quickly from the string
In purpose straight at Hector aim'd;
for much he long'd to hit him.
Yet once again he miss'd; for still
Eager for battle, fell the shaft,
and pierc'd him thro' the bosom :
So dropt he from the chariot :
Swerv'd with alarm; but he by life
Yet there he left him lying, tho'
But chancing nigh at hand to see
Bade him the coursers' reins to take:
Seiz'd with his hand a massy rock,
his soul was bent to crush him.
And on the string adjusted it:
While as he drew it, struck his arm,
And snapt his bowstring: to the wrist
his arm was numb'd: he stumbled
On to his knees, and from his hands
forth fell the bow and arrow.
But Ajax, when his brother fell,
But ran and overstrode his limbs
and spread the buckler round him.
Then, on their shoulders propping him,
Then straight toward the moat so deep
did they thrust back the Achaians,
And Hector in the foremost mov'd,
In guise no other, than as hound
Chaseth the buttocks and the hips
And watcheth when he wheeleth round,
So Hector closely kept in chase
And alway slew the hindmost man;
and scar'd the rest before him.
and up the moat they clamber'd
Fleeing, and many fell in death
Then, checking by the galleys' side
And each to other made appeal,
and to all gods in heaven
Did every one with lifted hands
His eyes like Ares, pest of man,
Care, if the Danaï do now
By dint of one man's force: but he,
His madness bridleth not, and lo!
To her with word responsive spake
"Ay, troth! would this man many a time
be riev'd of life and spirit,
And perish on his father's soil
But that, by evil mind possest,
Nor doth he hold in memory,
how oft and oft I rescued
would weep; then Jove would send me
In hurry from the lofty sky
to aid him with my presence.
But if within my canny mind
To bring from Erebus the dog
Never had he the direful streams
Of Thetis, who his knees embrac'd
That he to honour would exalt
Yet one day, troth! shall he again
But for us twain now harness thou
That I meanwhile within the house
The sight will gladden Priam's son,
Or whether some of Troians too,
Fallen in death, with flesh and fat
shall glut the dogs and vultures."
Down on the pavement of her sire
Whose tissue she herself had wrought
Then, in a martial tunic clad,
then with her feet she mounted,
And grasp'd her spear,——vast, weighty, stout,
Of heroes, whom for wrath she dooms,
But Juno keenly with the scourge
The gates of heaven boom'd aloud,
The Hours, who hold beneath their trust
Betwixt these gates they guided clear
But when from Ida father Jove
turn thou the twain! nor suffer
To thwart my purpose; for in sooth
For roundly will I say, and this
Out of the seat will cast themselves,
Nor when ten times the Sun hath clos'd
Shall they of wounds beheal'd, wherewith
So may the greyey'd shun to fight
But less with Juno I in truth
For she, whatever be my bent,
is alway wont to cross it."
He spake; then on her errand sped
And from the mounts of Ida, up
Meeting them at the foremost gates
There she detain'd them, and from Jove
For Saturn's son hath threatened, if
Out of the seat will cast yourselves,
Nor when ten times the Sun hath clos'd
Shall ye of wounds be heal'd, wherewith
So mayst thou, Greyey'd! shun to fight
But less with Juno he in truth
For she, whatever be his bent,
is alway wont to cross it.
So utter'd Iris swift of foot,
Urge that we twain with Jupiter
wage war in sake of mortals.
Of them, as chance may rule, let one
his mind and soul may purpose
With such avowal, back she turn'd
Then did the Hours unyoke for them
And free from harness fasten'd them
But father Jove from Ida drave
the seats of gods receiv'd him.
But on a golden throne, himself,
And as he press'd his feet beneath,
There, at the side of Jupiter
Alone; but no salute they spake,
But he in his own mind discern'd,
No toil, I trow, hath wearied you
The Troians to destroy, for whom
Surely, so far as heart and hands
Not all the gods should turn me back,
Before ye came in sight of war
For roundly will I speak it out,——
Back to Olympus had ye come,
He spake; thereat they mutter'd deep,
Close sat they, side by side, and woes
But Juno hid not in her breast
her wrath, but thus address'd him :
"O son of Saturn, grim and dire,
Right well are we aware ourselves,
we none the less have sorrow,
Now from the war in truth will we
But counsel to the Argives still
"At morrow's dawn, if such thy will,
Thou more than ever shalt behold
no pause shall make of battle,
Till from his galley-side it wake
E'on on the day, when, face to face,
For so doth Heaven will. But I
Take no account, not even if
The ends of Earth and Ocean, where
Nor from the breezes, but by depths
But thee and all thy moodiness
I heed not, e'en if roaming
Thither thou venture; for than thee
When thus he spake, no answer came
Then sank the brilliant light of day
The Troian army lost the light
With vows and longings, hopes and joy,
At distance from the galleys meet,
On a clear space where open ground
Then they, dismounted from their cars,
but in his hand he wielded
A lengthy spear, which measur'd ells
Glitter'd its brazen head, whose neck
But resting then his arm on this,
Now had I thought outright to quell
Only, too soon did darkness come,
The Argive army, ships and all,
And furnish out our suppers; but
and thence stout sheep and oxen
Bring ye right speedily; and eke
And bread from out the public stores,
and pick ye sticks in plenty,
That we, thro' livelong night, until
May kindle copious fire, whose blaze
shall reach the vault of heaven;
Lest haply under screen of night
Troth! would I not, that one of them
But that each man may carry off
a gash, at home to muse on,
And let the heralds dear to Jove
To gather boys of tender age
On to the towers built by gods,
And let the women, weak of sex,
Kindle a brilliant light; and let
Lest in the absence of the folk
Thus be it done, as I harangue,
Nor more of this be said, if this
Again will I at dawn address
My trust it is, hence to drive out
Whom noisome fates have borne to us
And verily, this night, must we
for our own selves be watchful;
But, with the morning's early dawn,
So shall I learn, if Diomed,
Unto their wall will drive me back,
Make havoc of his life, and bear
To wait my spear's arrival; but,
Shall he in death be prostrate laid,
What time to-morrow's Sun is high.
For would that I so surely
Immortal might be, and my days
And I such honour meet, as eke
Surely as doth the coming day
Then every one his sweating steeds
And duly fasten'd them with thongs,
And sent unto the city's gates,
and thence stout sheep and oxen
Brought out right speedily, and eke
And bread from out the public stores;
And from the plain the breezes bare
the steam to highth of heaven,
And Priam's self and all the folk
and fires beside them kindled many.
And as around the shining Moon
when all the sky is breathless,
And every lofty peak is shown,
And from behind the cloven blue
And all above thee seemeth Stars,
So many fires, betwixt the streams
Shone then in front of Ilium,
A thousand fires along the plain,
I say, that night were burning,
And close to every glaring blaze
And by their chariots the steeds,

div.note: Them, seems to mean the Achaians.

div.head: BOOK IX

l.seg: After such guise the Troians kept
which send their blasts from Thrake,
Rise into knolls, and on the beach
cast many a heap of seaweed;
So fitfully the heart was toss'd
The son of Atreus, deep in soul,
Hither and thither pac'd, and bade
By special call on every man
But not by shoutings: he himself
So in full gathering they sat
as some dark source of waters,
So he with heavy groaning spake
hath plotted, and doth urge me
So seemeth it the will to be
And yet will lower; for his sway
But come! as I the word shall speak,
Thus did he charge them: thereupon
they all were dumb in silence.
Dismay'd with sorrow, long were all
As seemly is in council, Prince!
so take it not in anger.
Saying, unapt for war was I,
With which the Argives, young and old
with half a gift hath honour'd:
He with the sceptre gave to thee
But, what is mightiest of sway,
valour, to thee he gave not.
Now if for craven backward course
Hie thee away: the path is free:
Stand many, which along with thee
But here, I ween, will stay the rest
Until we pillage Troy. Or if
their will be such, let them too
An end of Ilium: for, troth!
So spake he; and Achaia's sons
"O son of Tydeus, thou in war
Nor will gainsay it: yet the word
no end of deed hath pointed.
And young thou verily art still;
to me, of all my children
But come, let me, who make the boast
to be than thee far older,
Speak out and follow up the whole
Cast on my word dishonour; not
Hearthless and reckless of the right,
Is he, who loveth numbing feud
And furnish out our suppers; next,
Unto the younger men such charge
To banquet call the elder men,
of wine are full, which daily
Is carried o'er the billows broad
All cheer hast thou to welcome guests,
So, when on summons many meet,
Who best adviseth thee; and troth,
great need have all the Achaians
Burn many fires of war. To whom
This night will either whelm in woe
the army, or will save it."
He spake; and they full readily
and the two sons of Ares,
To each one pac'd a hundred youths,
Inside the moat, without the wall,
they went and took their stations;
In that mid space their fires they made,
But Atreus' son into his tent
On the good cheer before them laid
of drink and food were ended,
Foremost of all the aged man
Who thus with kindly soul harangu'd,
and spake his word among them :
With thee my speech will end, from thee
A prince to many peoples; ay!
Thee then behoveth it in chief
Wisely to speak: but every deed,
And I my counsel will avow,
as best to me it seemeth:
For, other better thoughts than these,
As judge I, from the day whereon
Thou from his tent, O Jove-deriv'd!
Not surely with applause from us;
In ample words and strong : but thou,
But let us even now take thought,
Of gentle words and pleasant gifts
we yet may soothe and win him."
That man may count, whom Jupiter
As, him to honour, now hath he
Again to win him, fain were I,
And splendid gifts will I rehearse
Of gold ten talents duly weigh'd,
And seven tripods new to fire,
Not poor in precious gold were one,
have won for me as prizes.
And seven women will I give,
I did pick out; whose beauty far
These will I give, and with them her,
of whom I then depriv'd him,
That never did I touch her bed
So much shall all presented be
Our host by heaven's favour rieve
Then let him lade a galley
From all the Achaian booty, ere
And let him take the choice himself
But if we reach Achaian Argos,
Wedlock to him and royal state,
Of these, whoso is dear to him,
Lead her to Peleus' house; but I
with seven will I grace him,
Ire, the land of grassy hills,
All these at distance short behold
And yeomen stout within them dwell,
like to a god, shall honour,
Such things will I make good to him,
Then yield he should. Troth, Aïdes
But therefore eke of all the gods
Let this man homage pay to me,
And forasmuch as I by birth
may boast to be the elder."
That speedily they seek the tent
Or else, myself will name the men;
With him let mighty Ajax go,
But now, bring water for the hands;
That we to Saturn's child may pray,
When thus he spake, to all of them
And first upon the princes' hands
Thereat the youths to many a bowl
mov'd round to all in order.
So when libations they had made,
And from the tent of Atreus' son
But many a charge the charioteer,
With wink and gesture unto each,
That with all effort they appease
Then went the twain along the strand
And many a prayer to the god
So pac'd they to the Myrmidons,
and reach'd their tents and galleys.
Him with the tender harp they found
Fair was the harp, with silver bar,
With this he did his heart amuse,
Alone in presence of the chief
might have his fill of singing.
Then forward stept the twain, and first
And stood before him. Starting up
Quitted the seat whereon he sat,
and, harp in hand, receiv'd them.
soon as he saw them coming.
"Hail! friendly pair! great need, I ween,
Who unto me, though sore displeas'd,
And upon chairs repos'd their limbs,
And stronger mix the wine, and quick
a cup for each man furnish.
For now within my chamber sit
men who to me are dearest."
So did he charge: Patroclus then
Before the blazing fire he plac'd
With chine of sheep, fat chine of goat,
The rest he slash'd in smaller bits
But when its noisome force was spent,
and all the flame was faded,
He spread the glowing embers out,
and stretch'd the spits above them,
When all was roasted well and dish'd,
With bread from baskets fair: the meat,
Close to the chamber's adverse side;
cast in the flames a firstfruit.
On the good cheer before them laid
of drink and food were ended,
And, with his goblet fill'd anew,
Nor now with thee; but here are laid
For banquet. Yet no care have we
And strike with terror. Doubt there is
For nigh unto our wall and ships
No more to be withheld, but rush
Reckless alike of men and gods;
For 'tis his vow to chop away
And in the scorching flame to wrap
All scar'd and blinded by the smoke,
Now grimly in my soul I dread,
These threats of his, and Fate for us
To perish here in Troy, afar
But up! O prince! if still, tho' late,
thy heart is bent to rescue
No remedy may then be found:
How from the Argives mayest thou
ward off the day of evil.
Upon the day, when he from Phthia
'Prowess and strength, my child! on thee,
So choose, will they bestow; but thou,
Aged and young alike, the more

div.note: Boreas (the N. E. wind) blows from Thrace to an Athenian; and the Greeks in general call this the Thracian wind. Zephyrus (the West) nearly blows from Thrace to a man in Troas. Homer seems here to have combined these inconsistent points of view.

According to the common interpretation, the former half of this line should stand "Which down a goat-abandon'd rock," i.e. abandoned even by goats; which is highly unsatisfactory. Liddell and Scott betray their suspicion that aigilips is connected with lips, a cliff; and if this be fundamentally the same word as lepas, a precipice, the p in the genitive is accounted for. On the other hand, that aigis as early as the Homeric period admitted the sense of a Storm, is a reasonable inference from the verb epaigizo. I therefore, until better informed, accept aigilips as a substantive, meaning strictly "a precipice of storms," or a Wetterhorn. The word recurs, Iliad 13, 63.

The twain can hardly mean the two heralds: 196-8 indicate that Ulysses and Ajax are intended. The ignoring of Phenix in this passage may seem to be a mark of patchwork which the poet never reduced to harmony.
Them, seems to mean the Achaians.

l.seg: Such charges did the aged man
Still, even now, thy rancour stay:
if thou from anger turn thee.
Then come, do thou give ear to me,
What gifts of friendship from his tents
Of gold ten talents duly weigh'd,
And seven tripods new to fire,
Not poor in precious gold were one,
What those tight steeds of Atreus' son
have won for me as prizes.
And seven women will he give,
He did pick out, whose beauty far
These will he give; and with them her,
of whom he then depriv'd thee,
That never did he touch her bed
As is the ordinance, O prince!
So much shall all presented be
Our host by heaven's favour rieve
Then let him lade a galley
From all the Achaian booty, ere
And do thou take the choice thyself
But if we reach Achaian Argos,
Wedlock to thee and royal state,
Of these, whoso is dear to thee,
Lead her to Peleus' house: but he
with seven will he grace thee,
Ire, the land of grassy hills,
All these at distance short behold
And yeomen stout within them dwell,
like to a god, shall honour,
Such things will he make good to thee,
But if from bottom of thy heart
Him and his gifts, yet pity thou
Us, who are perishing; who thee,
e'en as a god, will honour.
Truly thou wouldest in our heart
Full near would he to thee advance;
for, none his peer he deemeth
But him Achilles, fleet of foot,
The word which in my heart I hold
To stay you, each on other hand,
For like the gates of Aïdes
that man to me is hateful,
Who one thing hideth in his mind
But I my argument will speak,
as best to me it seemeth.
and he who much hath labour'd,
Nor aught of vantage do I win
But as the parent bird doth bear
Morsels of meat, whate'er she seize,
So likewise many a sleepless night
Your consorts to regain, have I
Aud with my galleys pillag'd twelve
From all of which I duteous
Receiv'd my booty: much he kept,
Howbeit, when some gifts he did
but me of all the Achaians
the wife who pleas'd my humour.
but now, what mean the Argives
By warfare on the Troians? why
This host assemble? was it not
do none but sons of Atreus
Her who is his, doth fondly tend;
Lov'd her, though purchas'd by my spear.
Sith force and fraud have seiz'd her,
The spoiler cannot win my will :
no farther let him tempt me;
But rather, with his other kings,
Ponder, the foeman's fire to ward
For troth, without my aid hath he
Hath built a rampart, and in front
Vast, broad and deep, with pointed stakes
Nor can he, even so, repel
Of Troian Hector: yet while I
Hector was rare beyond the wall
But only to the Skæan gates
There once to meet me waited he,
But now, sith I no pleasure take
To Jupiter and all the gods
Due sacrifice, and freight my ships:
then, when to sea I launch them,
Shall thou behold, if so thy will,
or if such things concern thee,——
But if fair voyage greet us from
On the third day may I attain
Now shall I all my other gear,
And all the chattel won by me:
The man who gave it, Atreus' son,
All open, as I speak it: so
Yet, though a dog in forehead,
Eye unto eye, my gaze to meet,
I deem, he would not venture.
No deed nor counsel will I join
by words shall he delude me.
Away with him in peace; for Jove
To me his gifts are foemanlike,
and as a straw I count him.
He offer'd me, and more beside,
so much as are the treasures
Two hundred men, who each go in
Or if so many were his gifts,
as sand and dust in number;
Not even so might Atreus' son
Not if in beauty she compete
Still I accept her not: for her,
let him from all the Achaians
and one than me more royal.
For if the gods have care of me
and home I reach in safety,
On Hellas and on Phthia dwell
From these (I reckon), whom I please,
my noble soul hath urg'd me,
With a betroth'd and wedded wife,
There to delight me in the wealth
For, recompence for life to me
is none, in all the chattel,
In former days, while peace was yet,
For, troth! by foray and by raid
ye get stout sheep and oxen,
And tripods eke by purchase come,

div.note: Boreas (the N. E. wind) blows from Thrace to an Athenian; and the Greeks in general call this the Thracian wind. Zephyrus (the West) nearly blows from Thrace to a man in Troas. Homer seems here to have combined these inconsistent points of view.

According to the common interpretation, the former half of this line should stand "Which down a goat-abandon'd rock," i.e. abandoned even by goats; which is highly unsatisfactory. Liddell and Scott betray their suspicion that aigilips is connected with lips, a cliff; and if this be fundamentally the same word as lepas, a precipice, the p in the genitive is accounted for. On the other hand, that aigis as early as the Homeric period admitted the sense of a Storm, is a reasonable inference from the verb epaigizo. I therefore, until better informed, accept aigilips as a substantive, meaning strictly "a precipice of storms," or a Wetterhorn. The word recurs, Iliad 13, 63.

The twain can hardly mean the two heralds: 196-8 indicate that Ulysses and Ajax are intended. The ignoring of Phenix in this passage may seem to be a mark of patchwork which the poet never reduced to harmony.
Pytho, afterwards called Delphi, from its new inhabitants.
Them, seems to mean the Achaians.

l.seg: But life to summon back again,
when once it pass the outwork
Of a man's teeth, no purchase then
For, Thetis of the silver foot,
Warneth me, that by double fates
If, here abiding, round the walls
But if I homeward sail, and reach
shall the end of death o'ertake me.
Yea, and to all the rest of you
Holdeth his own hand over her,
But ye, at your return, address
And speak your message out: for that
Bid them within their heart to frame
Which may the Achaian people save
Sith this which they devis'd is vain,
But in our tents let Phoenix stay
If such his pleasure, he with us
Shall voyage; but against his will,
surely I will not take him."
they all were dumb in silence,
After long pause, at length replied
fear for the galleys mov'd him.
be now in truth thy purpose,
Nor willest deadly fire to ward
At all, since rage possesseth thee;
how then could I be parted
From thee, dear child? but me to thee
Gave on the day, where he from Phthia
A child wast thou, to council new,
therefore with thee he sent me,
As one who words for thee might speak,
and guide to deeds thy effort.
So would not I from thee, dear child!
Promise, old age from me to strip,
Such as of yore; what time I fled
To shun the quarrel of my sire
Who, for his consort's brighthair'd maid
was fill'd with rage against me;
A maid, for whose endearments he
My mother. She my knees embrac'd
and oft with tears implor'd me
I heard, and wrought my mother's will.
Utter'd against me many a curse,
That never upon knees of his
From me begotten: troth! his word
Awhile I plotted in my heart
with the sharp brass to slay him;
But some immortal stay'd my wrath,
The many luckless jibes of men
While bearing thus my father's wrath,
No longer might endure to dwell
Encircling me, with many a pray'r
Were slaughter'd for them; many hogs
And from that aged prince's jars
strong wine was teem'd in plenty.
Nine nights they slept around me: guard
in turns they kept: nor ever
The fire went out, within the porch
But when the tenth night came on me
Over the court-wall, pass'd my guards,
Then I thro' Hellas' wide domain
Parent of sheep and deep of loam,
Who greeted me with forward love,
He made me rich, and gave to me
And lov'd thee from my inmost heart,
Rear'd thee, divine Achilles! for with
Ere on my knees I seated thee,
and gave thee tastes of dainties,
And held to thee the winecup: oft
out, and wet my bosom's vesture.
So then for thee I verily
Forecasting, that for me the gods
If-that thou mightest ward from me
For thee a ruthless heart to hold:
E'en they by penitence of men
Who in the track of Frenesy
and sound of limb; for alway
She plungeth far ahead of them,
Man's heart doth occupy; and they
but heal the wounds behind her.
But whoso to their word is deaf,
That Frenesy on him may come
Pay deference, and bend thy soul,
No prayer I to thee would make
And to the Argives succour bring,
But now, at once forthwith doth he
To give hereafter, and hath sent
Of all the Argives: whose address
Before such meed of honour came,
So have we heard from former days
When haply some of them were mov'd
Now I myself an ancient deed
But as it was, so will I tell
with foeman's rage to waste it.
Wrathful, that in the orchard's lap
Had Oeneus held: and other gods
But to the maid of mighty Jove
Then many a tall and fruitful tree,
with stem and branch and flower,
Out of the soil uprooted he:
The son of Oeneus, slaughter'd him;
Huntsmen and hounds; for such a foe
So huge was he: and many men
on the sad pile he mounted.
But she around his carcase rais'd
For the boar's head and shaggy hide,
Belov'd of Ares, fought; so long
Nor might they, many tho' they were,
Kindled a fury, such as eke
Then lay he by his wedded wife,
Consort of Idas, who was once
of men on earth the stoutest,
Went wailing, when the bright Apollo
The rancour which his mother's words
had stirr'd; who, stung with anguish,
For-that her brother he had slain,
spake many a curse to heaven;
she thrash'd with hand of passion,
Death to her child to give: and her
Who in mid darkness stalketh, heard,
Meanwhile from them, around the gates,
Rose clang and hubbub; and to him,
The chiefest of the priests arriv'd,
To pray, he would in rescue come,
There willed they to bid him choose
Of fifty acres; half of it
The other half, flat open field
Seeking to clasp his knees: but he
Yea, tho' to him his sisters too
Made prayer, still he more and more
Entreated, who of all to him
came thick, and on the towers
Mounted the foe, and scatter'd fire
So then at last with tears and wail
Of captur'd towns:- 'the men are kill'd,
While others lead the children off,
Then was the breast within him stirr'd,
to hear such deeds of evil;
He hied to go, and o'er his skin

div.note: The Greek text does not admit this sense, but runs: "her mother, like the Alcyon," which seems to me absurd. Alcyon is celebrated for grief at the death of her husband Ceyx, who was drowned, according to Ovid. The words of Homer in v. 564 leave it uncertain, whether Apollo slew Idas or carried off Marpessa. The common tale gives to Idas a longer life.
A long tale is here glanced at. The old interpreter says that Meleager slew five brothers of his mother.

l.seg: to him no more fulfill'd they
The many pleasant gifts, but he
But thou, of such a mind beware;
Turn thee to this, O child belov'd!
Come for thy meed: then like a god
Then, tho' thou rescue them, far less
will be thy praise and guerdon."
But him Achilles, fleet of foot,
to me such praise and guerdon
Will greet me. This will hold me here,
While breath doth in my bosom dwell,
and while my knees support me.
This also will I say ;-and thou,
Do not with wailing and with grief,
nor rightful do I count it
For thee to love him; lest by me,
who love thee, thou be hated.
Proper for thee it is, with me
Live as my peer in royalty,
Now these shall bear the message back,
In a soft couch shalt rest; but we,
A bed for Phenix, soft and dense,
to strew, that with the morning
They from the tent might quickly plan
thus spake his word among them :
Go we: for not by this approach
but back we now must carry

div.note: He seems to mean: "Honour forbids me to leave my ships and go to aid Agamemnon."

l.seg: Who now, I ween, wait in their seats
O ruthless! Yea, but every man
For son or brother kill'd; and he
In thee the gods unceasing wrath
have set: and lo! we offer
Revere thy own abode; for here
beneath thy roof we meet thee
From the full throng of Danaï;
and we, of all the Achaians,
Would alway fain abide to thee
But him Achilles, swift of foot,
All that thou sayest seemest thou
But anger swelleth in my heart,
How mid the host he outrag'd me
But ye unto your king return
and speak to him my message.
So soon as godlike Hector, son
will I give heed to battle.
And round my tent, I deem that he
will make a pause of combat."
He spake: then one by one they grasp'd
There did the aged man repose,
Slumber'd; and by his side was laid
a dame, whom he from Lesbos
Bestow'd, when Scyrus' isle he took,
But them, when to Atrides' tents
again their feet had borne them,
and spake a word of question.
Willeth he from the foeman's fire
Or hath refus'd, and anger still
To him replied the godlike man,
In sooth he willeth not his rage
to quench, but more than ever
Is swollen with disdain; and thee
How mayest thou the galleys save
But, for himself, so soon as e'er
Into the briny flood to launch
Yea, and to all the rest of us
Holdeth his own hand over her,
Such were his words; and others here,
Both Ajax, and the heralds twain
The old man, sleepeth there: for so
If such his pleasure, he with them
Will voyage: but against his will
they all were dumb in silence,
Dismay'd with sorrow, long were all
Would that thou haddest never sued
much more hast thou inflam'd him.
But him we now may let alone,
Or here abide: in time will he
The spirit in his heart may bid,
and when the god shall rouse him.
But come; as I the word shall speak,
Take ye this night your slumber, when
With pleasant bread and wine; for this
Then quick and urgently let each
set men and steeds in order
Thus spake he: then the gather'd kings
So, when libations they had made,
And laid their bodies to repose
and took the gift of slumber.

div.note: Boreas (the N. E. wind) blows from Thrace to an Athenian; and the Greeks in general call this the Thracian wind. Zephyrus (the West) nearly blows from Thrace to a man in Troas. Homer seems here to have combined these inconsistent points of view.

According to the common interpretation, the former half of this line should stand "Which down a goat-abandon'd rock," i.e. abandoned even by goats; which is highly unsatisfactory. Liddell and Scott betray their suspicion that aigilips is connected with lips, a cliff; and if this be fundamentally the same word as lepas, a precipice, the p in the genitive is accounted for. On the other hand, that aigis as early as the Homeric period admitted the sense of a Storm, is a reasonable inference from the verb epaigizo. I therefore, until better informed, accept aigilips as a substantive, meaning strictly "a precipice of storms," or a Wetterhorn. The word recurs, Iliad 13, 63.

The twain can hardly mean the two heralds: 196-8 indicate that Ulysses and Ajax are intended. The ignoring of Phenix in this passage may seem to be a mark of patchwork which the poet never reduced to harmony.
Pytho, afterwards called Delphi, from its new inhabitants.
"Nether-swaying Jupiter," i. e. Pluto.

The Greek text does not admit this sense, but runs: "her mother, like the Alcyon," which seems to me absurd. Alcyon is celebrated for grief at the death of her husband Ceyx, who was drowned, according to Ovid. The words of Homer in v. 564 leave it uncertain, whether Apollo slew Idas or carried off Marpessa. The common tale gives to Idas a longer life.
A long tale is here glanced at. The old interpreter says that Meleager slew five brothers of his mother.
He seems to mean: "Honour from Jove is mine, and I need no additional honour from the offer to aid Agamemnon."
Them, seems to mean the Achaians.

div.head: BOOK X

l.seg: Now all the rest by galley-side,
Kept thro' the livelong night repose,
Might be in slumber sweet detain'd;
but tides of thought did toss him.
And as when brighthair'd Juno's lord
So thickly from his bosom sobb'd
Deep drawing from his heart the moan;
When o'er the Troian plain he gaz'd,
Which burnt in front of Ilium, -
the sound of flutes and whistles,
And hum of men: but when he saw
Then many a hair with lowest roots
from out his head uptare he
But to his mind this path appear'd
the best; to go for Nestor,
The son of Neleus, first of men;
The twain might ponder, and from bale
Upright he rais'd him, and put on
But next, a fiery lion's hide
Vast, ruddy, reaching to the heel;
and took his spear beside him.
Who truly o'er the waters wide
And in his broad hand grasp'd a spear;
and hied to rouse his brother,
and as a god was honour'd.
But him he found, around his frame
At stern of galley; and to him
To espy the Troian camp? but I
do direly fear, that no one
and me is need of counsel,
For Jove's intent is chang'd, who now
Rather hath set his mind: for I
Have known one man so rueful deeds
As Hector, dear to Jove, hath wrought
He a mere man, no sacred birth
Deeds hath he done, which, I aver,
Lasting and long: such bale hath he
But hie thee hence, and summon now
Will go, and urge him to arise,
to cross, and keenly stir them.
For none as he their ear might gain,
sith as his son is leader
To marshal them; and eke with him
Am I beside the watch to stay,
and wait until thou join us,
Or hie me after thee again,
when duly I have charg'd them?"
"Stay on the spot, lest we perchance
Amid the gloom: for many are
And where thou goest, speak aloud,
and urge them to be wakeful,
And name the parentage of each,
And lavish praise on all; nor err
But let us both good service do
Jove with the past events on us
With such address and careful charge
And hied himself in speed, to find
But him he found within his tent,
On a soft bed; and close to him
His shield, two spears, and shining casque
Beside them lay his breadth of belt,
Leading the folk, he hied; for he
Now on his elbow raising him,
Address'd his speech to Atreus' son,
In covert of the dusky night,
Say, seekest thou some mule astray,
Speak out, nor silent come on me,
but tell, what errand brings thee."
whom in a sea of troubles
Beyond all mortals Jove hath plung'd,
While breath doth in my bosom dwell,
and while my knees support me.
But thus I roam, sith on my eyes

div.note: The Greek is ambiguous; but it is interpreted by v. 127.

l.seg: For, my sad heart doth ruminate
I fear: my thoughts within me
Steadfast no longer are, but faint,
But if thou aught of vigour hast,
The watch forgetting: close at hand
sitteth the foe; nor know we
Whether perchance in shades of night
but he, I trow, will struggle
With troubles more and worser, if
And others eke will we arouse;
And greater Ajax, match for gods,
if one were free to fetch him;
To thy displeasure will I chide,
nor will I hide my censure,
Who sleepeth, and to thee alone
But now his task it were, to toil
I bid thee to rebuke him;
For oft in listlessness he waits
Not from a coward slothful heart,
But keeping still his eye on me,
Now woke he earlier than me,
Him have I forward sent, to call
the twain of whom thou askest.
But go we: them I trust to meet
"'Tis well: for so, on future day,
none will of all the Argives
when he shall charge and urge them."
This said, the aged man put on
And at his shoulder button'd he
Then did he grasp a valiant spear,
And hied to go along the ships
Ulysses first he found, to Jove
And with loud call arous'd him: quick
And from the tent forthwith he came,
and spake to them in answer:
doth need so pressing urge you?"
Take no offence: so fierce distress
But follow, while we others rouse,
On our high council, to decide
He spake: then, entering his tent,
his breast, and mov'd behind them.
Lying in arms outside his tent;
Beneath their heads their bucklers lay;
Fix'd in the earth by upper end:
Of father Jove, flash'd from afar:
but he, their chief, was sleeping.
Beneath his body lay a hide,
But underneath his head was stretch'd
Then with his foot the charioteer
"Wake, son of Tydeus! why all night
dost cull the prime of slumber?
Along a swelling of the plain,
and little space doth ward them?"
When thus accosted, from his sleep
And wake the princes? aged sir!
To him in turn the charioteer
For, blameless children, troth! have I,
and a long train of peoples,
Who might patrol and summon you:
Standeth their future, or to live,
But come, swift Ajax rouse for us;
Sith pity for my age thou hast:
for thou than me art younger."
and took his spear beside him,
And started on his path: and them
But when amid the assembled watch
Not slumbering, I wis, they found
As, penn'd upon a field, the dogs
Of dogs and shepherds; all the night,
their hope of sleep hath perish'd:
So from their eyelids vanish'd then
As thro' that evil night they watch'd:
for to the plain they alway
Turn'd their regard, whene'er they deem'd
At sight of them the aged man
was joyous, and, to cheer them,
as now; and let not slumber
He spake, and crossing, pass'd the moat;
All who to council summon'd were,
Then o'er the moat, which deep was dug,
they cross'd, and took their station
On a clear space, where open ground
the shades of night inclos'd him.
Here duly seated, each to each
"My friends! can no man then be found
In his own daring soul, among
Would venture, if perchance he catch
Whether their bent be, here to stay,
Or to the city back to go,
Might he such questions duly sound,
His glory would to heaven reach,
For upon him shall every chief,
Bestow a black ewe, big with young,
And alway shall he find a seat
they all were dumb in silence,
which near us lies, to enter.
But, with another join'd, it were
When two together go, the one
How gain is won; but, when alone,
Yet all his mind more sluggish is,
He spake; then many wish'd to be
Both the Ajaces wish'd for it,
the son of Nestor wish it.
The son of Atreus wish'd for it,
And much Ulysses wish'd, the crowd
For alway he in daring soul
Who of the claimants bravest is;
But thou no reverence of heart
Where choice is free; nor take the worse,
Looking to birth; not even if
"If then in truth ye lay on me
In whom the heart and noble soul
If he be my escórt, I ween
Safe even out of blazing fire:
"O son of Tydeus, praise me not
too much, nor yet reproach me;
For, those who hear thee, Argives are,
But go we; for the night apace
The stars are far upon their course,
More than two parts are gone;
a third barely is left for action."
So spake they mutual; and both
Had left his own: and round his head
he plac'd a cap of bull-hide,
Ridgeless and crestless; such as guards
And sword; and plac'd around his head
With many a twisted strap within
On either side in thick array
a boar's white tusks were planted
Well and right cleverly; but felt
From Eleon, where reign'd of yore
For use of war: now, settled close,
So then the twain, when both were busk'd
Hied them to go; and on the spot,
They left; and maid Athene sent
beside the road, to greet them,
A lucky heron: mid the gloom
its cry they knew, tho' nothing
In every feat of danger stand,
Hearken to me, and chiefly now
"Eke unto me, O child of Jove!
Escort me, as of yore thou didst
Escort to Thebes, when forth he went
Thither a gentle word did he
Yet he on his returning path
who stoodest by, to prompt him.
In the same fashion now, beside me
And I to thee will sacrifice
Gold will I spread around its horns
and yield it to thy honour."
So utter'd they the word of pray'r,
When to the imp of mighty Jove
they thus had paid their worship,
They hied to go, as lions twain,
Eke Hector, neither yet did he
To sleep; but into council thick
When these together he had call'd,
The deed which in my mind I plan?
nor should his meed be scanty.
For I will give the chariot
On him, who hath the hardiment
Whether they guard them, as of old,
they all were dumb in silence. -
Among the Troians was a man,
A godlike herald was: the son
was rich in gold and copper,
And had in form no comeliness,
Five were the daughters of his sire,
Who then stood forth, and spake his word
But come, thy sceptre forward reach,
and swear an oath upon it,
On me thou wilt bestow, which bear
But I a spy to thee will be
nor vain nor short of promise:
For thro' and thro' the army I
will pass, till I arrive at
Join the high council, to decide
Then Hector held his sceptre forth,
and sware an oath upon it:
shall on these coursers mount him,
False was the oath which thus he sware,
Quickly behind his back he slung
On outer part a hoary skin
of wolf he wrapt around him,
With casque of weasel screen'd his head,
And from the army hied toward
None was for him by Fate decreed,
But when the crowd of steeds and men
But let us suffer him to pass
Then, rushing after, catch him quick:
or if his feet outstrip us,
Yet shipward press him with the spear,
Beside the path; and swiftly he,
When só far onward he was gone,
as in a match of ploughing
The mules unto the oxen give:
In new and sinking soil to drag
Then, in pursuit, on ran the twain.
He heard, and stood and listen'd ;
For o'er his mind this fancy rush'd,
But when within a lance's length
they reach'd, he knew the foemen,
And plied his supple knees to flee;
and sharply did they chase him.
As when, with jagged rows of teeth,
a fawn or hare with effort

div.note: A match. The original is obscure, and the sense here assigned conjectural.

l.seg: So then the son of Tydeus, with
From his own folk dividing him,
But when all bút he had achiev'd
In flight uuto the galleys, then
Boast earlier to fling the dart,
shall reach thee: nor, I reckon,
Long time shalt thou beneath my hand
He spake, and hurl'd his javelin,
but miss'd the man on purpose.
The point of its well-polish'd shaft
Glided, and fasten'd in the ground:
then still he stood, and trembled,
All pale with fear; and in his mouth
his teeth did champ and chatter.
Panting, the twain arriv'd and seiz'd
his hands: then weeping spake he:
For in our home is brass and gold
From these my sire would speedily
Soon as he learns of me alive
"Be of good cheer, nor let thy mind
by thoughts of death be troubled.
But come, explain thou this to me
Why from the army lonely thus
In covert of the dusky night,
Is it, the corpses to despoil?
or art thou sent by Hector
As spy upon the hollow ships?

Dolon replied, and while he spake,
Agameninon Agamemnon has also been noted as a typo in this segment, though not printed directly in this line.
Who gave assent to yield to me
Of Peleus' lordly son, and eke
gloom he bade me to adventure,
Whether they guard them, as of old,
To him with smiling glance replied
Hard are those steeds for mortal men
to tame and guide in harness,
But come, explain thou this to me,
Where is his martial armour laid?
how lie the beds and watches?
What are their plans? and are they
bent to stay anent the galleys,
Or to the city back to go,
With those who are high councillors
Far from the din of war, beside
As for the watches, none distinct
do guard and save the army.
For where a fire is kindled, there
are men awake to watch it :
Each guardeth other: but the allies,
they trust the charge of watching.
To him in turn responded then
Slumber they? or on ground apart?
But why thus carefully of me
For if ye eager are, the crowd
Whose steeds I saw, of all on earth
Whiter in hue than falling snow,
and like the winds in fleetness.
With him he brings a spectacle
which for a man and mortal
But now permit me to approach
Else bind me with a ruthless bond,
and here behind you leave me,
Till ye have gone and trial made,
To him with scowling glance replied
Sithence our hands have grappled thee;
But if, beneath my hands laid low,
thou here from life shalt vanish,
Then Dolon reach'd his spreading hand,
upon the beard to stroke him,
In guise of suppliant; but he,
Smote him in middle of the neck
And ere his lips as yet were still,
his head with dust was mingled.
Then off they stript the weasel-cap,
Wolfskin and spear; but these aloft
"With these, O goddess, be thou pleas'd:
Against the coursers and the beds
he rais'd aloft and plac'm them.
Then reeds collecting and the twigs
He made a token, clear to sight
This finish'd, onward went the twain,
And sudden came upon the band
Upon the ground in order fair
Brilliant were laid; and every man
But Rhesus in the middle slept;
and his swift chargers near him,
Which Dolon, whom we slew but now,

div.note: Double bow. So 8, 266. We have no English epithet to define the bow formed of two arches, which bend in reverse when unstrung.

l.seg: But come, thy stalwart might put forth.
Or else, the men shalt thou assail,
and I will tend the horses."
When thus he spake, the greyey'd maid
From side to side he slaughter'd them;
Rose, as the smiting cutlass fell:
red grew the earth with carnage.
And as a lion on a flock
Or goats or sheep, doth sudden pounce
So did the son of Tydeus then
Till twelve lay slain beneath him:
Seiz'd by the foot and backward trail'd
Nor tremble, stepping on the dead:
for new were they to slaughter.
But when unto the king at last
From him, the thirteenth, ravish'd he
Over his head a phantom dire
stood forth, the son of Tydeus!
Slung them together, drove them out,
and whipp'd them with his bowstring;
(For from the curious car to take
Then with a whistle notice gave
And outward drag it by the pole,
Or from the troop of Thracians
While all his soul with such debate
Close standing by his side, address'd
If haply by some other god
When thus she whisper'd, right he knew
the goddess' voice which warn'd him.
Quickly they mounted on the steeds;
Smack'd them; and fleetly they approach'd
Meantime no idle watch was kept
And soon as he with Tydeus' son
With her enrag'd, he rush'd amid
When empty he beheld the place,
And found the corpses heaving yet,
rose, as the tide of Troians
and gaz'd at deeds of horror,
But when they came returning, where
they slew the spy of Hector,
There did Ulysses, dear to Jove,
His comrade, leaping to the ground,
And plac'd them in Ulysses's hands,
They flogg'd the coursers, nothing loth
in flying race to speed them
And Nestor first the tramping heard,
"O friends, who to the Argive folk
Falsely or truly, shall I say
My ears do quiver with the tramp
Yet grimly in my heart I dread,
Not all his word was utter'd yet,
Quick to the ground dismounted they:
With the right hand saluted them
Whence are these coursers? took ye them
Or did some god fall in with you,
and give them as a present?
I mix; nor wont to tarry
Beside the galleys, troth, am I,
Yet coursers, such as these, not yet
with eye nor fancy saw I,
But rather ween I that some god
hath met you and bestow'd them.
to both of you hath favour,
To him in turn responsive spake
A god, who will'd it, easily
Of coursers nobler far than these;
the steeds, of which thou askest,
Slaughter'd their lord, and by his side
A thirteenth man, whose spoils are here,
Whom, to espy our camp, and sound
This said, across the moat he urg'd
When at the tightly joined tent
And on the poop Ulysses hung
High on his ship, to furnish out
From feet below, to neck above,
But when the tossing wave had wash'd
much sweat from off their members,
And had their hearts refresh'd, they stept
After their bodies thus were bath'd,
So sat they to the morning-meal,
out of the full bowl teeming.

div.head: BOOK XI

l.seg: The Queen of Morning from the bed
Uprose, to carry light to men
But to the sharp Achaian ships
Noisome, who bare within her hands
And to Achilles' bands, which haul'd
Last on the strand, on bravery
There did the goddess station her
to toil in war and combat.
The son of Atreus, shouting, bade
the Argive host to gird them
For battle: mid them he himself
First on his shins the dapper greaves,
He fasten'd; next, to guard his chest,
Which erst from Cinyras he gat
What time the mighty rumour reach'd
Would shortly on their galleys sail
Therefore on him bestow'd he it,
Ten stripes of blue and dusky steel
Its stripes of gold were six and six,
but those of tin were twenty.
On either side toward the neck
Did arch their throats; to rainbows like,
Then slung he round his neck the sword,
And guarded in a silver sheath,
Above, he took his muchwrought shield,
Round which ten brazen circles ran.
On the fair front in centre,
Mid twenty bosses of white tin,
The strap with silver was encas'd:
Was twin'd with three out-gazing heads,
But on his head a four-plum'd casque
A pair of valiant spears he grasp'd,
And from them shone the yellow gleam
In honor to the stately king
There on the moat in order due
But they themselves on foot, with arms
Stream'd wildly; and from early dawn
sith that he now was minded,
Along a swelling of the plain,
Stood round the mighty Hector and
And round Æneias, whom the folk,
e'en as a god, did honour;
But Hector held in front his shield,
which equal was on all sides.
And as a deadly star is seen,
So at one moment Hector did
to chide them; and as lightning
He flash'd with brass all over.
in months of wheat and barley
Placeth his reapers on the field,
There quickly many a row they cut,
and thick the swathes lie prostrate :
Made havoc, nor did either side
But raging darted on, like wolves,
Both held their crests; and Quarrel dire
For She was with the combatants,
No other gods were in the throng,
Each in his proper chambers, where
Nor car'd the Father for their wrath;
Apart from all the other gods,
And on the flash of brazen arms,
While that the morning lasted still
So long from either side the darts
But at what hour the woodman takes,
His midday meal, when as his hands,
Glutted with toil, his soul by love
At this same hour the Danaï
burst thro' the bands by valour,
Urging their comrades, rank by rank:
leapt down, and stood to meet him:
Nor might the helmet's rim resist,
Mid bone and brain the weapon splash'd,
heavy with brass; but thro' it
But off he sped with deadly force
sith he had stript their armour.
Two sons of Priam, noble one,
Once at the foot of Ida caught
but set them free for ransom.
Hit with his spear upon the chest
But with his sword, beside the ear
Down from the car: then hurrying
Knowing them; sith he saw them both
What time Achilles, fleet of foot,
And as a lion, springing light
of yore from Ida brought them.
Of a swift hind, doth easily
Coming within their lair, and quick
betwixt his strong teeth crunch them,
And she, tho' near, no aid can give;
But at the stalwart beast's attack
for trembling dire doth seize her;
So then of all the Troïans
From these; but eke the rest were
scar'd nor dar'd to meet the Argives.
For brilliant gifts had he receiv'd,
Out of their hands: but Atreus' son,
Rose opposite: then from the car
Many and costly; brass and gold
From these our sire would speedily
Soon as he learns of us alive
So did the Troian brothers twain
"In troth! if ye for father have
To kill them then and there, nor grant
He spake, and from the chariot
Pierc'd in his bosom by the spear:
Chopt off the hands he rais'd to pray,
leapt down: him too he slaughter'd,
And, like a pillar, sent his trunk
These then he left; but he, where'er
There sallied in, and eke the rest
By footmen were the footmen slain,
when these to flight were driven;
Thick rose the dust, which thundering
and from the plain beneath them
While fierce the foeman's blade was plied:
As when a fire may baleful fall
In massy flakes on every side
And, branch and root, down fall the
the curling wind doth bear it,
So fell in dust beneath the might
To vultures a far dearer sight,
who on the earth lay prostrate,
But Hector was by Jove withdrawn
From dust, from carnage and from gore,
from hail of darts and turmoil.
Where standeth a wild fig tree, near
But when the fleeing Troians reach'd
There rallying they form'd a band,
the Skæan gates and beech tree,
Yet others still amid the plain,
If a whole herd be haply scar'd
Who, in the dusk of night, on one
By his huge weight and stalwart teeth
He rends the victim, laps the blood,
its neck at once is broken;
So were they chas'd by Atreus' son,
Who alway slew the hindmost man,
and scar'd the rest before him.
By his attack: for direful was
on face or back lay prostrate
But when at length his feet would soon
And lofty wall have borne him,
then the Sire of gods and mortals
Assum'd his royal seat, and held
Then spake his summons and command
While he amid the foremost foes
The son of Atreus, raging fierce,
So long let him from fight withdraw,
But if a spear-thrust wound the king,
And he upon his car shall leap;
or if an arrow reach him,
Vouchsafe, to slay the foe, until
When in the west the Sun shall sink
There found she godlike Hector, son
she sped from highths of Ida.
Then Iris swift of foot drew near,
and stood, and spake her message.
Sire Jupiter hath sent me forth,
While thou amid the foremost foes
to thee this word to carry.
The son of Atreus, raging fierce,
So long do thou from fight withdraw,
But if a spear-thrust wound the king,
And he upon his car shall leap;
or if an arrow reach him,
Vouchsafe, to slay the foe, until
then Jove to thee doth puissance
When in the west the Sun shall sink,
So utter'd Iris swift of foot,
leapt to the ground in armour.
thro' all the army went he,
Then did they gather to a ball,
The Argives on the other side
So in new fight confronted stood
Was first to sally, bent to shine
Utter for me, ye Muses, now,
Who of the adverse army first
a man both tall and comely,
When with advancing years, he reach'd
Still would the king detain him there,
So from the bridal chamber he,
But in Percota left he then
And came on foot to Ilium,
This was the man, who foremost now
When they to shorter distance came,
The son of Atreus miss'd his mark,
Yet did the girdle's motley work
Like lead, was blunted in the shock,
The spear-shaft, tugg'd it to himself:
Then with the sword he smote his neck,
so from his hand he pluck'd it;
For her a hundred kine he gave
Further, a thousand goats and sheep,
at once; and eke behote he
And to the Achaian troop retir'd,
Discern'd his brother's fall, his eyes
With sideway spearthrust came he on,
Unseen, and smote him in the arm,
Right thro' the flesh he pierc'd, until
The son of Atreus, shunning not
from fight and war, but sallied,
With spear-shaft nourish'd in the winds,
He by the foot was dragging off
The monarch reach'd him, as he stoop'd,
Fill'd up their destiny of life
Now while his blood from out the gash
well'd fresh and warm, assail'd he,
With spear and sword and mighty stones
But when the wound was dry and stiff,
and all the blood was clotted,
Then thro' the frame of Atreus's son
As when the lancing throe assails
So thro' the frame of Atreus' son
Into the car he sprang, and charg'd
for sore his heart was smitten.
"Oh friends, who to the Argive folk
To you I leave it, to defend,
Me with the Troïans to wait
So spake he: but the driver lash'd
Their breasts with streaks of foam were white,
While from the fight they bare away
But Hector, when afar he saw
Be men, my friends! nor negligent
Gone is a hero-chief; and Jove
To me high boast; but drive ye straight

div.note: Eileithuiai-is their Greek name.

l.seg: He, by such words, in every breast
As when against a savage boar
A huntsman by his voice doth fill
So Hector, Priam's son, a peer
Which on a sea of violet
Then who was first, and who was last,
a sport of death and plunder
when Jove to glory rais'd him?
he slew; and next, the many.
Clouds by the brilliant Southwind brought,
And by the wayward gale's assault
the crests of spray are shiver'd;
So the thick crests of Argive men
Then deeds beyond repair had been,
And now the fleeing Argive host
Come, gentle heart! and stand by me;
for troth! it were a scandal,
If that our galleys captur'd were
but short will be our pleasure:
more than to us hath favour."
He spake, and from the horses hurl'd
Thro' the left bosom with the spear:
Laid prostrate of the selfsame prince
Those then they left upon the ground,
Then plunging mid the crowd, the twain
Rush two wild boars with souls of pride
upon the hounds that chase them:
So, sallying from bay, did these
a car, and two bold brethren
but they his word obey'd not :
For why? the Fates to gloomy death
So from their bodies Tydeus' son,
Emptied the spirit and the life,
Nor were his steeds at hand to flee;
But them his squire apart detain'd;
Rag'd thro' the foremost braves, until
But Hector mid the ranks descried
the twain, and rush'd against them
With piercing cry; and after him
But let us stand and hold our ground,
He spake, and poising, forward threw
But from the brass the brass did glance;
so his fair skin it reach'd not,
There to his knees the hero sank,
Low on the ground; and both his eyes
But while the son of Tydeus went
where on the earth it lighted,
Came near to thee: but now anew
To whom, I ween, in din of darts
But when, next time, on thee I light,
If, eke to me, among the gods
I hend, shall be my onset."
He spake, and straight the armour stripp'd
Meanwhile, where by the mason's hand
Behind a pillar hid, and aim'd
The people's shepherd ; who, at ease,
And sturdy helmet: but the foe,
Twang'd it: nor vainly did the bolt
speed from his hand, but lighted
On the right foot, amid the sole;
and thro' and thro', the arrow
Piercing, within the ground was fix'd.
And from his ambush bounded forth,
"It wounded thee: nor sped my shaft
in vain: might Heaven grant me
To hit thee in thy lowest lap,
So would at length the Troïans
But, naught dismay'd, to him replied
Wouldest thou try, in arms complete,
No succour then thy bow should bring,
nor all thy sheaves of arrows.
Now, that my footsole thou hast graz'd,
Nor more care I, than if thou wert
For of a pithless worthless man
Far otherwise in sooth from me
If that at all it hit the foe,
it quick doth lay him lifeless.
With mangled cheeks his wife is left,
And while on crimson'd earth he rots,
more birds than women watch him."
He spake; and close before him stood
Out of his foot: but thro' his flesh
Into the car he sprang, and charg'd
for sore his heart was smitten.

div.note: Horn-brilliant, proud of thy horn-bow.

l.seg: was lonely left; for no one
Of Argives by his side abode,
"Alas! what now for me is left?
to turn my back, were shameful,
Fearing the crowd; yet worse it were,
But, O fond heart! why holdest thou
