For well know I, that coward men
To stand his ground right sturdily,
While all his bosom and his soul
And flankm round on every side,
As when on traces of a boar
Yet round they stream, and on the spot
So then Ulysses, dear to Jove,
But, springing up, he first assail'd
High on the shoulder; after him
Beneath the bossy shield he reach'd,
and pierc'd him in the navel:
So in the dust he fell, the earth
These then he left; but with the spear
But to his succour Socus hied
a man, to gods a rival;
Who at short distance took his stand,
That thou hast two such heroes slain,
Or else beneath my spear laid low,
He spake, and thrusting struck the shield,
which equal was on all sides.
And thro' the corslet's crafty work
And from the ribs stript all the skin:
Permit its deadly course to reach
And back his step withdrawing, gave
"Ha, wretched man! in sooth on thee
Me truly thou hast stay'd awhile
But upon thee, I promise, here
This day alight; and thou, low laid
To me a glory, and a life
While thus he spake, the other turn'd,
and quick to flight betook him:
But ere he might escape, the spear
And pierc'd the helpless back right thro',
So dropt he with a crash: thereat
Ha, wretched man! never for thee
In sleep of death thy eyelids close;
but wrangling fowl shall pluck thee
He spake, and drew the massy spear
Out of his wounded flesh, and eke
But after it his blood the more
gush'd out, and griev'd his spirit.
They all in scuffle rush'd on him
But he, a little, back withdrew,
Thrice did he lift his voice, as high
as one man's throat may compass,
Then suddenly his thought he spake
My ears are compass'd by the voice
In semblance like to that, as tho'
But hurry we along the crowd;
For our brave friend I fear, lest he,
Be harm'd, and to the Danaï
He spake, and led the way: behind,
Then soon Ulysses, dear to Jove,
they found; and Troians round him
Swarm'd, as a pack of red wild dogs
Around a horned stag, whose side
He hurrieth, while warm his blood,
and while his knees support him,
Till the swift arrow conquers: then
Rend him within a shady brake:
but if the god bring thither
A baleful lion, quick the dogs
But Ajax nearer came, his shield
And stood beside him: quickly then
out of the tumult led him,
Holding him by the hand, until
the squire drave close his horses.
But Ajax on the Troians sprang
and slew a son of Priam,
On to the plain with tossing gush,
And many a dry and rotten oak,
and many a pine it beareth,
So then did gallant Ajax deal,
Nor yet did Hector hear it;
far on the left was fighting,
Where chiefly heads of heroes fell
the bands of young men wasting.
Unless the lord of brighthair'd Helen,
In his right shoulder piercing him
For him the Achaians, breathing might,
If that the battle change its course,
And quickly to the galleys hold
He spake: to him the charioteer
And straightway mounted on his car,
And flogg'd the coursers, nothing loth
in flying race to speed them
Saw tumult in the Troian ranks,
doth rout them: well I know him;
For ample beareth he the shield,
But thither eke let us direct
Where signally both horse and foot,
but they, to the sharp smack attentive,
with gore of men were pelted,
Splash'd from the tires and horses' hoofs.
And pierce the human mell, leapt in;
nor from the spear withdrew he
For long; but in the Danaï
With spear and sword and mighty stones
He visited; but shunn'd the fight
Aghast he stood, and backward threw
Turning him round, and gaz'd askance,
And as when dogs and rustic men
and, thro' the long night watching,
Balk of the dainty food his maw;
Hungry and scar'd; for darts thick flung
And burning brands, which harry him,
And in the morning, sorely griev'd
So Ajax from the Troians then
fear for the galleys mov'd him.
As a dull ass doth on the fields
Who on his hide break many a stick;
yet he the corn doth enter
And crop the juicy blade; in vain
And hardly may they drive him out,
when he is fill'd with fodder:
So then the allies from distance call'd
On his mid shield with lengthy pikes,
And Ajax one time bent his soul
Rallying firm, and check'd the bands
At other time he turned to flee;
The foeman's inroad on the fleet;
Flung from bold hands, stood partly fix'd
Ere the fair skin they might attain,
Perceiv'd him by thick darts distrest,
And hurl'd his shining spear, and struck
The people's shepherd, neath the heart,
Then onward rush'd he, and would strip
in his right thigh was planted:
There was it broken short, and sore
And back he to his comrade-troop
"O friends, who to the Argive folk
Rally, and ward the rueful day
Sorely is press'd, and scarce may scape
But front, and meet your champion,
When thus the wounded hero cried,
they clos'd their ranks, and hurried,
till Ajax came and met them:
And quickly fac'd he round, when first
he reach'd his troop of comrades.
While thus, in guise of blazing fire,
The sweating mares from off the field
Him, as he came, divine Achilles
For he upon his galley's poop,
Stood gazing on the toil severe
Then from the galley shouted he,
His comrade: he, within the tent,
did hear; and forth he issued
A peer to Ares :-this to him
But him Achilles, fleet of foot,
Now deem I that the Achaïans
Around my knees : for need at length
But now, Patroclus, dear to Jove!
hie thee, and ask of Nestor,
What wounded hero in his car
To me his hinder aspect seem'd
but his front view I saw not,
And hied to speed him by the tents
When to the tent of Neleus' son
dry the sweat from off their garments
For them a mingled drink was made
From the fair isle of Tenedos,
She first before them swiftly plac'd
With feet of dusky blue; and next,
Which the old man from home had
brought. Double its stem: on margin
Four ears stood out; aside of each
To move it from the board, when full,
did from the table lift it.
Then with Pramneian wine the dame,
Mingled therein a drink for them,
First goats' cheese into it she par'd,
And bade them to indulge their hearts,
the pleasant draught had freed them,
Thereat from off his shining seat
Led him within with grasp of hand
and urg'd him to be seated;
friend! nor may I be persuaded.
Majestic, quick to wrath, is he
To ask, what wounded hero thou
I know it; for I see Machaon,
Of that dread man; who presently
Be few or many wounded sore?
Over the army wide is spread?
Waits he, till, on the water's edge,
The foe's invading fire shall warm
And we ourselves, line after line,
be slain? for now no longer
Sinew have I, as once I had
O that I still were thus in youth
As when of old a fray arose
What time, in strife for captur'd kine,
who held his home in Elis.

div.note: To omit 98 lines would here be a great and obvious improvement. These lines may have been patched in by the poet himself, to gratify some Pylian hearer. No one will seriously defend such inopportune garrulity by alluding to Nestor's wine-cup: yet the passage has its interest to us.

l.seg: Pledges of booty we would seize,
and he his kine would rescue;
But from my hand a dart was flung,
and mid the foremost struck him;
So down he fell; and round the corpse
But booty from the plain we drave
For fifty were the herds of beeves,
the flocks of sheep were fifty,
Fifty the grazing troops of swine,
the goats' broad herds were fifty;
But three times fifty were the heads
All of the gentler sex; and foals
These to the town by night we drave
And glad was Neleus, that success
But when the Dawn her face had shown,
All, whosoe'er on score of spoil
When, scant in numbers, we engag'd,
For-that, in former years, had come
For, of male offspring, children twelve
But, sole survivor, I of these
So then my parent for himself
And a vast flock of sheep; and pick'd
For, to him also, large redress
For when, to join the race, he sent
Kept; and sent off the charioteer,
in grief to lose his horses.
At such affairs, and deeds likewise,
that none might miss his portion.
the gear, and round the city
On the third day came forth, themselves
With general levy: mid their ranks
Children in age, nor yet wellskill'd
Round this their warriours did spread,
But when they cover'd all the plain,
By night, in Pylos gather'd she
of war: nor me did Neleus
Allow to busk me for the fight,
For I, he said, not yet had skill
Near to Arene falls a stream
flow'd the Pylian troops of footmen.
In general levy thence we rush'd,
And at the noon of day attain'd
A bull to Neptune did we slay,
Lastly, a heifer of the herd
Along the host, by rank and rank,
we took, each man his supper,
And by the river-banks we slept,
Eager to storm our city, throng'd
But sudden now must they confront
For when with shining beams the sun
Our ranks upon them rush'd, with pray'r
First I a hero slew, and won
a prince, who held in wedlock
So down in dust he fell; but I,
Shrunk this and that way, panic-struck
at fall of such a hero,
Then, thro' their ranks, in darted I,
Two men from every one did gripe
Now had I Actor's offspring quell'd,
Out of the battle rescued them
Then to the Pylians did Jove
For we, across the widespread plain,
Until our steeds that land of wheat,
There the last hero did I slay
and left him; but the Achaians
And all, to Jove of gods, gave praise,
Such was I, when in truth I was,
He surely, all too late, will know,
Upon the day, when he from Phthia
We two, then entering your home,
There found we in its ample walls
did burn fat thighs of oxen
Within the cloister of his yard;
And pour'd from it the sparkling wine
So round the carcases you both
Stood in the threshold. Starting up
Led us within with grasp of hand,
and urg'd us to be seated,
as is the due of strangers.

div.note: The twins, named Molions after their mother (as is supposed), are regarded by Homer as having Actor for their titular father, Neptune for their true father. Cteatus and Eurytus are their names, 2, 621. See also 13, 185 and 207.
The Achaians. The phrase here suggests, that the Epeians of Elis were not strictly Achaians.

l.seg: But when of eating and of drink
Then foremost I our errand told,
and urg'd you, us to follow.
Willing were ye; and they, your sires,
in many words did charge you.
and play the chief to others;
on thee in turn laid charges :
Older of birth art thou; but he
But speak a prudent word to him
So spake thy aged sire; but thou
e'en now, if-that he hear thee.
Who knoweth, if with heaven's aid
But if, with heart foreboding, he
Which, from the lips of Jupiter,
Yet let him thee permit, and send
And for the fight entrust to thee
Gain respite of their toil;-and short
from off the tents and galleys,
Unto the city back to drive
He spake; and strongly did bestir
But when Patroclus near the ships
Round which the Argives causes judg'd
Here met he, limping out of war,
Down from his head and shoulders
flow'd the sweat; and dark blood gurgled
Out of the fretting wound; but yet
sent forth a wail of sorrow:
So then, your doom it was, afar
With whiteness of your_fat to gorge
Thinkest, the Achaians yet will stay
Or that, beneath his spear subdued,
But all the Achaïans will fall
For all who formerly bare lead,
But me at least save thou, and lead
And from my thigh the arrow cut,
Out of the wound with water warm;
Who was himself by Cheiron taught,
One in his tent doth wounded lie,
Himself; the other on the plain
Yet even so, in sore distress,
I may not lightly leave thee."
He spake, and grasp'd beneath the breast
And led him to a tent. A squire
With water warm; then with his hands
Were all arrested; thus the wound
was staunch'd, the blood was stopped.

div.note: Blameless, perhaps opposed to Sorcery, as 6, 171. But the word in some connections may seem to mean Refined, Liberal (arts). See 9, 127; 13, 637. Indeed Tacitus says "sanctissimas Arruntii artes," for refined and noble accomplishments.

div.head: BOOK XII

l.seg: And moat avail, with which their ships
That the sharp galleys and much spoil
But, built against the immortals' will,
So long as Hector was alive,
And while as princely Priam's city
So long did eke unharm'd abide
And whoso of the Argives liv'd,
In the tenth year, and straight embark
Turning on it the rivers' might,
Where crests and oxhides fell in mud,
But bright Apollo turn'd their mouths
for nine days on the rampart,
to swamp the walls in ocean,
Led the attack, and from the depth
and mix'd them with the billows,
Then all the mighty shore again
with heaps of sand he cover'd,
After the rampart flat was laid;
Turn'd back the rivers, where before
Such were the after-deeds to be
But then did fight and clamour blaze
The timbers of the towers crash'd
with many a blow, and, fainting
Were hemm'd in fear of Priam's son,
But he, like to a hurricane,
As when mid hunter-troop and hounds
With grim delight of strength; but they,
Frontwise do stand, and shower thick
their darts; but flight nor terror
Moveth his noble heart; but him
And to what part he dasheth straight,
there yield the ranks of hunters:
Thus Hector thro' the maze of men
did rush, and to his comrades
Gave charge to cross the moat; nor yet
Might dare it; but they loud did neigh,
Too close for passage: all along
Hung over, and sharp stakes aloft
Not easily might there the steed,
Down venture; but the ranks of foot
Then, standing by bold Hector's side,
Madly across the moat we drive
And it, sore troublous is to pass:
Are planted; and anent to them
For in its narrowness, I ween,
Against the foe, hath ta'en resolve
That, far from Argos, shorn of fame,
But if they rally in recoil
and drive us from the galleys,
Chasing in turn, and we within
Back to the city reach, beneath
But come, as I the word shall speak,
Let every squire upon the moat
But we ourselves on foot, with arms
Shall in a body follow close
Will not withstand, if, sooth! for them
and won the heart of Hector;
leapt to the ground in armour.
There on the moat in order due
But they themselves, in five-fold band
The rampart to destroy, and fight
went third; but by the horses
Two sons of Priam; but with them
who to the fourth was leader,
Who Glaucus chose beside, and eke
For these among them seem'd to him,
but he mid all was signal.
Now when with frames of oxen-hide,
No more to be withheld, but rush
Were, to the blameless counsel of
But not the son of Hyrtacus,
Was willing there to leave his steeds
But to the galleys sharp approach'd
O simpleton! nor troth! for him,
For he unto the galleys' left
did hie him, where the Achaians
Out of the plain were streaming back
There, thro' he drove his car and steeds;
for at the gates he found not
nor the huge bar athwart them.
But men did hold them open wide;
Some of their comrade, who from war
Thither with purpose straight he held
With piercing yell; for verily they reckon'd
No more would be withheld, but rush
O simpletons! for in the gates
The twain before the lofty gates
did stand, as on the mountains
Highheaded oaks, which day by day
the wind and rains do buffet,
Yet firmly still they hold their ground,
So then the chieftains twain, on hands
Truly the others would, at first,
But when the Troïans they saw
Like boars of field and mountain, which
Of dogs and hunters; slant they dash,
Snapt from the roots; and clash their tusks,
until some dart may slay them:
So unto these the shining brass

div.note: Deucalus. The form of the name is Deucalion in 13, 451.

l.seg: But those with weighty stones did fling
Themselves to succour, and the tents,
As on the ground do flakes of snow
So from their hands a flying stream
By massy weights, like stones of mill,
Slapt on his thighs with double hand,
"O father Jove! and eke dost thou
But they, as wasps with stripy waist,
or bees, which make their houses
No other wise do these refuse
to yield them from the portals,
He spake, nor by his argument
Who upon Hector chose to set
And troth! for me, like to a god,
all things to tell, were toilsome.
For round and o'er the stony wall
Fought for the galleys; and the gods
were pierc'd in heart with anguish,
Nor might the brazen helm resist
the weighty spear; but thro' it,
Mid bone and brain the weapon splash'd,
Next he for death and plunder hent
While, at the girdle aiming well,
Then drawing from the scabbard quick
amid the crowd, and smote him
All these successive cast he down
While from the bodies of the slain
and burn with fire the galleys:
But standing at the trench's brink
For, while they eager were to pass,
Who in her talons bare away
For writhing backward in her grasp,
it tore her neck and bosom,
Till, stung with anguish, down amid
the gazing crowd she dropt it,
And on the breezes of the wind
Then, standing by bold Hector's side,
Castest on me some blame, as tho'
In peace or war thy mind to cross,
Yet roundly will I speak my thought,
as best to me it seemeth.
For I this issue do forebode,
Eager to pass the moat and wall
Who in her talons bare away
ere her dear nest attain'd she,
Nor might complete the chase, and bear
So, if by mighty strength we burst
And force the Achaians to retire;
Shall we along the selfsame course
Sith, many a Troian shall we leave
May with the wasting brass lay low,
spell forth to thee the vision.”
To him with frowning glance replied
But if in very truth such wise
Then surely have the gods themselves
Who wouldest urge my heart to be
To trust: but I, in view of these,
Whether toward the east and sun
they on the right hand hie them,
Or leftwise haply they may sheer
One omen is for ever best,
But, as for thee, why dreadest thou
For if the rest of us shall all
Rush upon slaughter, yet for thee
no fear there is to perish;
Sith, heart to sally, none hast thou,
But if thyself shalt skulk away,
and damp them for the battle;
Forthwith, beneath my spear laid low,
shalt thou of life be rieved."
With such address he led the way,
Which on the galleys carried straight
Made many a trial to destroy
Deep-rooted in the ground, to serve
At these they mightily did toil,
By force: nor yet the Danaï
But they for fence and battlement
From under them assail'd the foe,
Along the towers, to and fro,
And when they any might descry
This one with words of gentleness,
Or eke of middle state (for, troth!
not all of men in combat
Alike may quit them): now for all
And this yourselves too know, I ween:
The foe's upbraidment, turn his back
this day, may haply grant us
The onset to repulse, and chase
Thus did the twain with forward shout
But they, -as thick the flakes of snow
fall in a day of winter,
When Jove the Counsellor is bent
Snowing on mortals: mid the lull
of winds, he sheds it constant,
and the fat tilth of peasants:
Yea, and along the hoary brine
the shores and creeks it lineth,
Save where the billows washing up
the storm from Jove is heavy:
So they on either side did fling,
Nor then might yet the Troïans
Have bursten thro' the mighty bar
To sally, as a lion bounds
Forthwith, he held in front his shield,
which equal was on all sides,
Brazen and fair, by crafty smith
And started on his path; in guise,
Whom, when of flesh bereaved long,
In sally on the sheep, to pierce
For tho' beside it swains on guard
with dogs and spears await him,
But either he some spoil doth seize,
Or by a dart from some brisk hand
So then Sarpedon, match for gods,
To mount upon the foeman's wall
In Lycia? and why do all
as peers of gods regard us?
Why are we grac'd by wide domain
Now then beseemeth us, I ween,
And lead the Lycians to meet
Brave spirit; sith among the first
O gentle friend! if thou and I,
As heav'nly gods, not I in sooth
Nor liefly thee would I advance
Now, sith ten thousand shapes of Death
tho' sly of foot and nimble ;-
Onward! and glory let us earn,
or glory yield to some one."
He spake: nor then did Glaucus flinch,
So, side by side, the twain led on
Shudder'd: for he the tower held,
He then along the Achaian wall
He might some chief espy, and win
Quick he descried the Ajaces twain,
Standing; and Teucer, from the tent
Nor distant; yet, to reach their ears
by shout, his throat avail'd not;
From banging at the shields, and helms
And at the gates: for all of them
would try to burst an entrance.
Then forward he to Ajax sent

div.note: Any-gait; in any way, at any rate, at all events.

l.seg: Rather the twain: for that of all
Here threatens: for with weighty force
Who staunch and wight were heretofore
But if there too distressful strife
press hard, yet let them spare us
But sped to go along the wall
And quickly reach'd the Ajaces' side,
Thither to go, that, for awhile
at least, ye stay the danger;
Rather the twain: for that of all
There threatens: for with weighty force
Who staunch and wight were heretofore
But if here too distressful strife
press hard, at least afford them
So spake he, nor reluctant found
Go thither; but again will come,
when them I well have rescued."
And by their side Pandion bare
When at the tower they arriv'd
So front to front both sides did dash,
Him with a rugged stone he smote,
no man, tho' young and sturdy
with both his hands would raise it.
Yet on the helm with fourfold ridge
he from aloft did swing it.
Helmet and bones and head were smash'd
at once; and like a diver,
He from the lofty tower plung'd,
Where he the arm unguarded saw;
and check'd his glee of valour.
Might pry more closely at the wound
Soon as he saw it: he natheless
With lucky aim of spear he hit
And pluck'd the weapon out: thereat
Follow'd the spear, and round him clash'd
And tugg'd toward him: all of it
With a wide breach was open laid,
and made a road for many.
Both sons of Telamon rush'd up;
Struck, on his breast, the brilliant strap
But Jove forbad his proper son
Then Ajax with a mighty bound
smote on his shield; and thro' it
Who from the breach retir'd a space:
nor yet did he withdraw him
Entirely; sith his soul was smit
By my own single strength to force
the more, I deem, the better."
He spake: the people quail'd beneath
The Argives on the other side
Within the rampart: then for both
And force a passage to the galleys:
And hurl them from the rampart back, when once their band attain'd it.
But as within a common field
With rod in hand, and inch by inch
So by the battlements were these
Then by close stab from ruthless brass
Thus everywhere with gore of men
Nor might they, even so, avail
To earn hard sustenance by wool,
So in their balance was the war,
by weights on both sides, equal,
Gave higher glory, who was first
So shouted he with piercing voice
And on their galleys scatter well
He spake exporting: all of them
did ope their ears to hear him,
And straight ahead against the wall
Then Hector clutch'd a massy stone,
Broad at the base, but sharp above:
not two men pick'd for sinew
might heave it from the pavement,
(Men such as now are seen), but he
alone with ease did swing it:
took off for him the burthen.
As when some lusty swain the fleece
of a male sheep may carry,
and slight the toil to raise it;
So Hector, lifting high the stone,
Which clos'd the lofty two-leay'd gates,
thro' which one bolt was fasten'd.
There, at short distance did he stand,
Full on their middle; stepping broad,
to make his swing the stronger.
The hinges both were torn away:
The bars were shatter'd by the blow;
Then in did gallant Hector leap.
Mantled his cheeks; the sheathing brass
Two spears he brandish'd: baleful gleam
burnt in his eyes; nor any,
Save only god, might check his rush,
when thro' the gates he bounded.
Facing about, to right, to left,
To climb the rampart: nothing loth,
Stream'd in, and fore'd the Danaï

div.head: BOOK XIII

l.seg: WHEN to the galleys Jove had thus
There left he either host with toil
Justest of men, who feed on milk,
But he no longer unto Troy
For surely in his heart he deem'd,
Nor was a fruitless outlook kept
For he too held a lofty seat,
Whence, open to the view, appear'd

div.note: I interpret Abioi to mean "without bows." The ancients did not, because they looked for truth in such notices by Homer. He seems to me to have conceived of the Mare-milkers, as Herodotus of the Argippaeans.

l.seg: Then from the mountain's craggy highth
Three steps he made; and with the fourth
he reach'd his goal at Ægae;
Brazen of foot and swift to fly,
He on his own immortal skin
with gold was clad; and grasping
on his own seat he mounted,
And o'er the billows 'gan him drive.
their lord, and frisk'd around him.
The sea with gladness op'd its lap,
Skimm'd o'er it; nor was, underneath,
Here Neptune, shaker of the earth,
And loos'd them from the yoke,
and cast ambrosial fodder near them
For pasture; and around their feet
Proof against mighty blows or craft;
Might firm abide, the while he went
Went, like to flame or like a squall,
And roar'd and skirl'd in unison;
The Argive galleys, and to slay,
out of the deep sea coming,
The Argives rous'd; and first address'd
For, tho' the Troians in a crowd
Hector, who vaunteth him a son
O would some god this argument
Then, let him rush his worst, but ye
Would dash him, even if himself
Smote both of them, and fill'd them full
Their limbs, both feet and hands above,
Which, bent another bird to chase
first knew the voice of heaven,
And to the son of Telamon
Nor troth! is he our oracle
For, as he started, clear I mark'd
And gods, tho' gods they be,
to men are easy of discernment.
Yee, and myself do feel within
Both feet below and hands above,
Both feet spring forward; and my hands
Grasping the spear; and e'en alone
tho' he be measureless in fury."
But now their sinews were unstrung
And anguish rose within their heart
to see the sight of terror,
When in a crowd the Troïans
Gazing at these, big tears they dropt,
nor thought to scape the mischief:
Then he, to stir their bravery,
on you in sooth I trusted
But if ye slackly take in hand
Now do we see our day of doom
Good Spirits! troth, a marvel great
That men of Troy have hardihood
to reach the ships of Argos;
Men, who were like to timid hinds before;
To panthers or to wolves become,
or to wild dogs, a quarry,
Knew not our onset to abide
But now, far from their walls they fight,
But if the widely ruling king,
Pelides fleet of foot; yet we
But heal the mischief: healsome are
Nor ye neglectful well may be
Who all are bravest in the host:
Were slack in battle, never I
with such a man would wrangle
Or blame; but verily with you
O gentle hearts! by this neglect
May bring on us: but, each of you,
Within his bosom print: for lo!
For now doth Hector, good at need,
Direful; who hath the mighty bar
Then stood, around the Ajaces twain,
Stand firm, and fenced spear with spear,
So helmet was by helmet screen'd
With ridges bright each other touch'd:
The javelins were like to kiss,
when sturdy hands would wield them,
But the men's hearts were gone in front,
and burn'd to join the battle.
Forward in plump the Troians rush'd;
for at their head was Hector,
Roll o'er the margin of a cliff,
Have wrench'd the brute crag's fastenings
Aloft it bounceth: with the shock
On the flat plain; nor farther then
may all its impulse roll it :
Threaten'd to reach, right thro' the tents
Slaying: but when he lighted on
He paus'd, full near them: for in front
With swords and with twohanded pikes
Kept him to measur'd distance off:
so to retreat they drave him.
Then shouted he with piercing voice
Stand firmly by my side: not long
Me hath the chief of gods impell'd,
He, by such words, in every breast
Did march, and forward held his shield,
which equal was on all sides,
And hit him on the tough bullhide,
which equal was on all sides:
Nor might he pierce it thro', for first
Low in the shaft: yet far aloof
The leathern buckler from his side,
might reach him. But the hero
Then hied he him, along the tents
Out of his proper tent to fetch,
a lance which there he treasur'd.
Meanwhile, the others join'd in fight,
was first to slay a hero,
Back came he then to Ilium,
Signal, and dwelt in Priam's court,
Him Teucer reach'd beneath the ear
And back withdrew it: then, as falls
Which on a farseen mountain's top
So did he fall: and round him clash'd
Then rush'd the son of Telamon,
He, seeing it, by scanty space
Which deadly on the bosom struck
So with a loud crash down he dropt,
and o'er him clang'd his armour.
Then Hector from the hero's head
would rush to snatch the helmet
But Ajax with the shining spear
at Hector thrust; nor pierc'd him:
(For all his flesh with dreadful brass
From both the corpses: these forthwith
Leaders of Athens, mid the host
As when from dogs with jagged fangs
Two lions snatch a goat; aloft
within their jaws they bear it,
Raising it from the ground; so him
Carried aloft, then stript his arms:
And sent the sever'd head to whirl,
So down into the dust it came
Then Neptune, shaker of the earth,
And hied to go along the tents
To whet the souls of Danaï;
Bare from the war, beneath the ham
laid charge, and forth was coming :
For still to meet the war he burn'd.
Accosted; but in voice he seem'd
The Ætolians; and by the folk,
e'en as a god, did honour.
The threats, which once at Troïans
"No man, I reckon, is in fault:
That far from Argos, shorn of fame,
But Thoas! to withstand the foe
Alert, and eke dost others urge,
Now, more than ever, stir thee well,
and one by one exhort them."
Then Neptune, shaker of the earth,
Safely return, but here to dogs
But hither hie thee quick, and take
thy arms, and let us sally
In speed; if haply only two

div.note: See note on 11, 749 (the two Molions).

l.seg: But thou and I are trained, eke
against the brave to quit us."
Thus said, again the god was mix'd
He donn'd his beauteous arms, and gat
Showing a sign to mortal men ;
So brilliant was the brass, upon
Nor distant from the tent was he,
the weapon's point doth rack thee?
Or com'st in errand after me?
Idle within my tents to sit,
In quest return I, if perchance
A spear: for that which heretofore
Against the shield of Priam's son,
"Spears, if it list thee, thou in sooth
shalt find or one or twenty,
From far to skirmish with the foe:
Of helms and corslets laughing bright
Are many Troian spoils; but now
too far they lie to take them :
But do among the foremost stand
If haply to some other man
My battle be unseen, yet thou
"What is thy valour, well I know:
For if beside the galleys now
nor may his soul be steady;
From knee to knee he shifteth him,
His heart within him, boding doom,
While-that the brave man changeth not
Not even there might any man
For if, in mell of battle, thou
Not from behind the blow would light,
but in thy breast or belly,
While onward rushing to the van
But hie thee to my tent, and there
Clutching from out the tent a spear,
In guise as Ares, pest to man,
moveth to war, and with him
Terror, his darling son, is seen,
Who even in a hardy soul
But they from Thrace accoutre them,
nor yet to both the armies
Alike may listen, but to one
Such then proceeded to the war
Whether toward the right of all,
Or, as I ween, toward the left ?

div.note: "There are four Ephyras," says the old scholiast: but seven towns of this name are counted in Dr. W. Smith's Geogr. Dict.

l.seg: The Ajaces twain, and Teucer, who
These will a glut of turmoil give
And frantic as his purpose is,
hard will he find the effort
to quell, and burn our galleys,
to none of men is second
Who mortal may of fabric be
and eat the fruit of Ceres,
Whose body may by pointed brass
Keep we to leftward of the host;
so shall we know full quickly,
or glory yield to some one."
Led in the path, until they reach'd
like to a flame in fury,-
They all in scuffle rush'd on him
As when by might of whistling winds
In season when along the roads
they burn'd in heart for carnage.
With long fleshgashing pikes of brass
And eyes were blinn'd by brazen gleam
And beaming shields and flashing helms,
Hard were the heart, which saw such toil
Diverse in spirit and in aim,
Jove, as I said, will'd victory
And Neptune, from the hoary brine
vex'd that they sore were worsted
Truly of both, the breed was one;
one and the same their birthplace;
So they, alternate, both ways haul'd
and had his home in Troas,
And sued Cassandra for his bride,
The royal damsel: so he fought,
And hit him as he lofty strode,
and pierc'd him thro' the body;
Nor did the corslet aught avail,
which, wrought of brass, encas'd him:
So with a loud crash down he dropt. Thereat the victor boasted :
"Thee chiefly of the sons of men, Othryoneus! extol I,
If truly thou wilt all achieve,
who pledg'd to thee his daughter.
We too behest would make to thee,
To lead from Argos as thy bride,
Whoso may fairest be of form;
if that with us thou capture
But off! with me! so shall we make
His foeman by the foot: thereon
Kept at his shoulders, breathing hot:

div.note: To behight, is, to mention by name, to specify; hence, to promise. So a behest, is a thing specified or promised; though used latterly for a command.

l.seg: Then toppled he, as may an oak
But of the wits, which erst he had,
to scape the hands of foemen,
and pierc'd him thro' the body :
Nor did the corslet aught avail,
which, wrought of brass, encas'd him:
Were from the Troians driven to
Stung by the loss of Asius,
and stoop'd to shun its fury,
which equal was on all sides,
And by the turner's skill was cas'd
Snug under covert here he crouch'd,
Pass'd over; but the buckler jarr'd
Graz'd by the spear: nor vainly then
from his broad hand it issued,
But hit a shepherd of the folk,
Where lies the liver neath the heart;
He will (I ween) rejoice; sith I
Nor did he, tho' with anguish stung,
But ran and overstrode his limbs
and spread the buckler round him.
Then, stooping to receive the load,
with moans of sorrow bare him.
Either to veil in gloom of night
Her in the chambers of their home
Supremely lov'd at heart; for she
mid all her friends of childhood
Therefore in all the breadth of Troy
But he, her lov'd one, now beneath
For Neptune witch'd his sparkling eyes
For neither backward might he haste
or like a pillar, stood he
Pierc'd thro' his bosom with the spear,
and burst the tunic round him,
But now right harshly did it clank,
So with a loud crash down he dropt;
but deep the spear was planted
Within his heart, which, panting high,
made the shaft's end to quiver,
Three against one, despite thy boast,
So mayest thou be taught, what blood
Who, for the oversight of Crete,
Minos in turn a son begat,
Over the ample Crete: and now
hither my ships have borne me,
To thee and to thy sire a woe
To fetch Aeneias: him he found
That to a chieftain so approv'd
Then near before his face he stood,
Now to avenge thy sister's lord,
hath hent for death and plunder."
He spake, and strongly did bestir
But stoutly waited, as a boar,
The riot of a rushing throng:
then high his back doth bristle,
And his two eyen gleam with fire;
and in his jaws he whetteth
His tusks, full resolute of heart
to stop both hounds and huntsmen.
These calling to his aid, did he
Dread I Æneias fleet of foot,
who now on me is rushing;
Who, heroes in the fight to slay,
Yea, and of youth the flower hath,
For if, with such a heart as this,
my years to his were equal,
Full shortly should he take or yield
He spake: then all of them, one soul
Closing their rank, stood side by side,
Follow'd, as doth behind the tup
To seek the water, giving joy
When he the crowd of people saw,
Rattled, as each at other hurl'd
in weight a match to Ares,
Hanker'd with ruthless brass to gash
and stoop'd to shun its fury:
Then far behind him in the earth
his lap, and drain'd his vitals :
So in the dust he fell, the earth
Out of the corpse; yet might not he
From shoulders of the dead; for sore
Much had the sinews of his feet
Whether to sally for his spear,
And tho' in standing-fight he well
No longer nimble were his limbs
While he retreated step by step,
and struck the son of Ares,
So in the dust he fell, the earth
How that his offspring dear was slain
He, by the will of Jupiter
As eke the other deathless gods,
Rush'd at him, wounding with the spear

div.note: Enyalius, a rarer name for Ares.

l.seg: Out of his hand then fell to earth
Regain'd the weighty spear, from where,
near to the wrist, he fix'd it;
Then back unto his comrade troop
Out of the harshly roaring war
His speedy coursers, which in rear,
These to the city carried him,
While from his newly wounded wrist
The rest no pause of combat made,
Thereat to one side drooped he
And helmet after it: so him
With sudden sabre leapt on him,
Which, running upward from the back,
All these did he clean shear away:
Fell on his back, and spread abroad
Would strip, with widely glancing gaze.
Goaded from every side with spears
His tender skin behind it screen'd:
Even amid the storm of darts,
No lack of foemen fronted him,
yet eye to eye he fac'd them,
Nor for a moment steady held his spear;
and in his heart was aiming
Either some distant foe to hit,
While thus forefencing to assault the crowd,
Thrust at the middle of his shield:
Grudging him longer days of life,
It, like a stake fire-harden'd, snapt,
and half of it was fasten'd
but half on earth was splinter'd.
Then back he to his comrade-troop, avoiding fate,
With spear beneath the navel pierc'd
Then forward falling round the spear
As struggleth on the mountain wide a bull,
Have with the thongs of nooses bound,
So for a little while, not long,
Pluck'd out the spear: but Adamas
Then with a mighty sword of Thrace
To earth the triple crest was tost
Regain'd it, as it roll'd across
His bow, defied him: so they met;
the one, to dart his weapon
All eager; but the other drew
Therewith the son of Priam hit
From the broad sieve of winnowing
Leap by the peasant's jerk; so then,
But in the hand which held the bow
The son of Atreus, good at need,
Which pierc'd the hand right thro', until
And trail'd the ashen shaft along,
But from his flesh quick pulling it,
Did gently bandage up the wound
with wool of sheep well twisted,
Which from a sling his ready squire

div.note: The winnowing sieve (or shovel) is embraced in the spread arms of a man, and jerked up and down.

l.seg: to his last goal was hurried,
By thee, O Menelas, to die
When they to shorter distance came
The son of Atreus miss'd his mark,
Nor might his utmost force avail
right thro' to drive the weapon;
For the broad bullhide warded it,
His shaft was snapt: but he in heart
Thereat, the son of Atreus drew
Wielded a shining axe of brass,
Lengthy, well polish'd: so the twain
The Troian struck the topmost ridge
Under the plume: above the nose,
And low before his feet in dust
Then writhing dropt he; but with heel
Troth, thus shall ye the galleys leave
For all the outrage, which on me,
Nor trembled at the bitter ire
The god of strangers; who shall soon
O ye, who, welcom'd once by me
and eke much gear beside her.
Yet, howso eager, here or there
will ye from Ares stay you. —
O Jove, our Father! thee in sooth
Mid gods and mortals; yet from thee
to men who deal in outrage, —
All which doth every man, I trow,
Than fighting; yet the Troians are
Such vauntings utter'd o'er the slain,
Stript from his skin the bloody arms
and gave them to his comrades ;
But he himself again went forth,
Behind his father dear; — nor e'er
Who with a thrust of spear would pierce
Nor might his utmost force avail
right thro' to drive the weapon.
Then back he to his comrade troop,
Round glancing cautious, lest some dart
And in the right haunch planted it;
but thro' and thro', the arrow
Under the bone a passage found
Down on the spot he sank, in hands
Breathing his spirit forth, and lay
on to a car did lift him,
So, for his host indignant, forth
A man there was, both rich and good,
who held his home at Corinth,
Oft was he warned by his sire,
A seer, — that he by dire disease
Or by the Troians be subdued
Of these the latter would he choose,
Onlaid by Argos, and the griefs
Him did the arrow strike betwixt
the ear and jaw; and quickly
Out of his members life was flown,

div.note: Polyeidus means "much-knowing."

l.seg: While thus, in guise of blazing fire,
Not yet had Hector, dear to Jove,
That here, upon the galleys' left,
Were ravag'd; yea, and haply eke
with his own strength endued them.
For Hector stay'd, where he at first
had pass'd the gates and rampart
Where by the hoary billow's edge
Had drawn aloft their galleys: here
For-that here chiefly men and steeds
were staunch and wight for battle.
Led by the son of Peteos,
But o'er the Phthians Medon and
But Medon was of meaner birth,
Long time in Phylake; for he
A kinsman of his father's bride
And round the bottom of their horns
And nothing but the polish'd yoke
doth mark the field in outline:
So they advancing side by side

div.note: The inhabitants of all Attica seem to be meant by the Ionians.

l.seg: Many and gallant, who in turn
with toil and sweat might stagger.
Nor might their courage firm abide
For brazen helmets none had they
and wool of sheep well twisted
So with one band the leaders then
by stealth; nor did the foemen
Then from the galleys and the tents
But, standing by bold Hector's side,
Yet not in every kind at once
For unto one man chiefly God
A second in the dance has skill,
a third with lyre and chantings :
Within the bosom of a fourth
Which eke has cities sav'd; and Jove
But I my counsel will avow,
as best to me it seemeth.
Some with their weapons hold aloof;
Whether in plump to fall upon
Now from the galleys to return
Quake, lest their debt of yesterday
Sith by their galleys, now, a man
and won the heart of Hector;
leapt to the ground in armour,
and take my share of battle,
But hither quick will I return,
when duly I have charg'd them."
He spake; then as a raging bird
With piercing scream, and flew across
But they, when Hector's voice they heard,
But he amid the foremost ranks
But them no longer might he find
Some were within the rampart, pierc'd
Lord of the brighthair'd Helen, near
Cheering the comrades at his side,
But Hector, when he close was come,
with words of scorn address'd him:
to be a dastard bear me.

div.note: The Greek, instead of "raging bird," has "snowy mountain"; which I cannot believe that Homer said. I have imagined oreï niphöenti to be a corruption of orneoi thuonti.

l.seg: Here on the spot. But those our friends
are slain, of whom thou askest.
Both in the hand; but deadly fate
Now, whitherso thy heart and soul
I trow, abandon me, so far
But no man, howso eager, may
When thus the hero spake, the word
Who onward mov'd, where chiefly was
Who on the former morn had come
Then on rush'd they, with weight and mass
Which from the thundercloud of Jove
And doth the briny flood bestir
Hollow, and bald with hoary pate,
So then the Troians closely wedg'd,
And Hector, Priam's son, a peer
Led them; and forward held his shield,
which equal was on all sides,
thick plates of brass were welded,
This way and that he tried, amid
If, as beneath his shield he mov'd,
Yet nowise daunted he the heart
The Argive heroes? not, in sooth,
But by the evil scourge of Jove
And verily thy heart, I ween,
hopeth; but straightway eke to us
Hopeth; but straightway eke to us
I plight, that earlier by far
Yea, to thyself, I say, 'tis near,
Shalt prayer lift to Father Jove
shall bear thee to the city."
While thus he boasted, from the right
Cheery to see the bird of Jove.
And queenly Juno gave me birth
And I such honour met, as eke
Surely as doth the present day
Bear common ruin: mid the crowd
If to await my lengthy spear
Thy lily skin; and thou, subdued
With fatness of thy flesh shalt gorge
Thus having spoke, he led the way:
From other side the Argives too
So to Jove's brilliant æther reach'd

div.head: BOOK XIV

l.seg: Nor might the winecup longer hide
Do thou then, sitting here, abide,
Until the bath for thee be warm'd
So shall thy skin right pleasantly
from gore and filth be cleansed.
I forth to glance around must go,
He spake, and seiz'd the sturdy shield
And sallied from the tent; and straight
Routing them from behind; and down
As when the mighty deep amid
So heav'n the aged hero's soul,
Whether among the crowd to go
In adverse fight: and round their skin
As foemen foemen stabb'd with swords
who in the war were wounded,
For at far distance from the fight
Were from the hoary wave retir'd;
On to the plain; and built in front
close to their poop, the rampart.
Nor might the margin of the beach,
but the folk were closely straiten'd.
poop before poop, they drew them,
And fill'd the mighty depth of shore
But now the princes, fain to see
Heaving with grief, felt new dismay
I tremble lest dire Hector crown
Which he in full harangue to Troy
That back to Ilium would he
Than he our galleys wrap in flame
Ye spirits! do then others too
Foster against me in their heart
to play their best in battle?"
To him in turn the charioteer
For prostrate in the dust is laid
And now beside their very hulls
Blazeth abroad; nor might thy gaze,
Know, from which side the Achaian rout
this way and that way flounders:
So are they slaughter'd in melay;
Our part it is to ponder, how
Your-selves to enter war; for vain
Nor hath the moat availed us,
It to our galleys and ourselves
So seemeth it the will to be
That far from Argos, shorn of fame,
For well I knew, when zealously
So now again know I, that he
As peers of blessed gods, but ties
the hands and might of Argos.
But come! as I the word shall speak,
Down to the briny flood divine
And moor them to the mooringstones aloft,
Arrive; if haply in her gloom
May stay them: instantly would we
And flee with all: for shame is none,
even by night to save us :
For better 'tis, by flight to scape,
To him with frowning glance replied
Of sorry wretches! not to us,
Art thou thus eager then to leave
Of Troy, in sake of which we here
Hush thee, O prince! lest other folk
A word, with which no man at all
from out his heart to utter,
with war and din of battle,
But upon us shall ruin fall
Seaward ye drag, the Achaian host
So will thy counsel baleful be,
Meant I, against their will to bid
Into the briny flood to launch
Now, may the man be found, to speak
Whether he young or old might be,
to me it were a pleasure."
spake forth his word among them :
"Near is the man, not far to seek,
if ye to hear be willing,
Nor grudge and wonder, for-that I
For unto Portheus were of yore
Sire of my sire, who mid them all
He in his native land abode;
Decide. In wedlock there he held
So dwelt he in a wealthy home,
Teeming with wheat; and many were
Many his flocks of sheep: but how
he with the spear was foremost
In all Achaia, this, I trow,
from none of you is hidden.
Wherefore, ye may not call my breed
Or scorn upon the counsel cast,
Come! enter we the war! perforce;
Let us, beyond the reach of darts,
by wound on wound be stricken.
But yet, new courage may we breathe
He spake; and they all willingly
Forward they hied, and in their front
Nor was a fruitless outlook kept
In semblance of an aged man
And, by the right hand grasping tight
"O son of Atreus! now, I ween,
With deadly gladness, whilst he views
Sith of right judgment, in his heart,
Now perish may he, as he is,
Yet against thee the blessed gods
But troth! the Troian leaders still
Over the dusty breadth of plain :
and thou thyself shalt see them
far from the tents and galleys."
For,-loud as thousands nine or ten
of men who shout for battle,
Out of his breast send forth; and shed
to toil in war and combat.
Jove's brother and her own employ'd;
and in her soul was joyful:
But Jove upon the highest top
Seated beheld she, and the sight
Then doubtful plannings stirr'd the mind
How might she best delusion frame
Love's nearer pressure; then would she
So to her chamber hurried she,
no god but she might open.
she clos'd; then first with waters
Of Jove set free, cast wide its scent
to earth and ends of heaven.
Herewith her fair skin ointed she,
which for the queen of heaven
And in a girdle girded her
Next, through her ears' well-pierced flaps
Three-ey'd and piec'd of many a stone;
like to a sun its glory.
When thus around her tender skin
she all her gear had fitted,
Forth from the bower hasted she,
Apart from other gods, to her
if I some favour ask thee?
Or scornful wilt thou haply be,
To her in words responsive spake
Speak forth thy meaning: me my heart
If complish it I can, and if
Over all mortal men, and eke
For I, on visit to the ends
Wend unto Ocean, source of gods,
Who in their own halls nurtur'd me,
set low the abode of Saturn
To these on visit go I now,
For they long time withholden are
rage on their soul hath fallen.
If I by words and blandishment
And join them in the rightful bed
Alway should I by them be held
For in Jove's bosom restest thou,
Thus saying, from around her breast
In it was fondness, in it charm,
Right winsome; which hath stolen oft
This into Juno's hand she gave,
"Here! in thy bosom place the belt complex:
Within it all thy wish is wrought,
nor vain shall be thy errand."
So answer'd she, and drew a smile
Who smiling took from her the belt
and in her bosom plac'd it.
But Juno, hasting on her path,
She to the snowy mountains sped

div.note: To behight, is, to mention by name, to specify; hence, to promise. So a behest, is a thing specified or promised; though used latterly for a command.

l.seg: Above the summits; nor was Earth
And closely did she press his hand,
"O Slumber, lord of all the gods!
lord of the race of mortals!
Now too obey me: and to thee
all days will I be grateful.
So soon as by his side I lie
Then I a throne to thee will give,
Golden and fair; which my own son,
By art shall fabricate, and eke
Which to thy dainty feet shall yield
who Source is held of all things.
Nor lull him into slumber, save
when he himself might bid me.
Ay! for thy urgency of yore
Then did I overcloud the mind
Shed balmy round him: thou meanwhile
Afar from all his friends. But Jove
And in disorder tost the gods
Me sought he, and from heaven high
had hrel me lost in ocean;
But Night, who tameth gods and men,
to whom I fled for rescue,
Receiv'd me: then his hand he stay'd,
And now this other featless toil
But hearken further! I to thee
and to be call'd thy partner,
thro' many a year doth hanker."
Come then, and swear to me by Styx,
And of thy hands lay thou the one
The other on the sparkling sea';
Who sit with Saturn under Earth,
To me thou verily shalt give
thro' many a year doth hanker."
But as he bade, she sware the oath,
who hold the name of Titans.
After she had perform'd to swear
and all the oath was ended,
With mist as garment wrapping them,
At Lectus; where they quitted first
Over the dry, and neath their feet
Therefore he mounted on a fir
Thro' the low air grew tapering,
Snug in the branches of the fir
a seat of shelter found he,
In form not other than the bird,
Is Chalkis by the immortals call'd,
but men the nighthawk call it.
Nor sooner on her gaz'd, than love
So there confronting her he stood,
Nor here stand ready for thy feet

div.note: Behight, i. e., name, stipulate, promise.

l.seg: "I now, on visit to the ends
Wend unto Ocean, source of gods,
Who in their own halls welcom'd me
To these on visit go I now,
For they long time withholden are
rage on their soul hath fallen.
I journey to the ample halls
For never yet did love so much
a peer to gods in counsel;
Who royal Perseus bare to me,
to me brought forth, and Minos;
nor thy own self, bewitch me,
As now I am on fire for thee,
"O son of Saturn, grim and dire,

div.note: Acrisione, i. e. daughter of Acrisius; Danäe.
Phoenix, or, the Phoenician; called Agenor by later poets.
Persephoneia, the same name as Proserpina of the Latins. Her mother is Demeter, Ceres.

l.seg: Upon the tops of Ida, where
all things are wide and open,
Say, what if haply some among
Espy us twain asleep, and haste
Unto all gods? never would I
back to thy palace trust me,
Uprising from that bed: in sooth,
But if thy will be thus, and if
Thou hast a chamber of thy own,
Thither go we to lie, sith now
the bed to thee is pleasing."
"O Juno, fear not thou at all,
lest god or man behold us.
In such a golden cloud will I
Thro' it no ray of sun shall pierce,
whose eye of all is sharpest."
So speaking, Jove with both his arms
The lotus, fraught with juicy dew, -
In masses thick and soft, which them
a cloud of gold was woven,
and clasp'd in arms his partner.
Sent upon errand to the god
And standing close in front of him
While Jove on Ida sleepeth yet,
Short though it be; for soft and deep
So spake he, and departing sought
But Neptune more than ever burn'd
And mid the foremost ranks straightway
Yet no too keen regret of him
shall be, if we the others
But come! as I the word shall speak,
These let the strongest heroes take;
Screening their heads, and in their hands
Onward! but I myself will lead :
I deem shall longer hold his ground,
Also, whoe'er is stout to fight,
should to a weaker yield it."
He spake; and they all willingly
Then quickly did the kings themselves,
Who, passing in review thro' all,
Good warriours good arms put on,
So when their skin in dazzling brass
They hied to speed them: at their head
Wielding within his breadth of grasp
Which, like to lightning, flash'd abroad;
The Troians, them on other side
Of Argos: then with vast uproar
Nor on the pebbly beach so much
out of the deep doth drive it;
So loudly, when it riseth fierce
Nor wind, which in its hour of wrath
Such, as was then the voice abroad
When each upon the other rush'd
Who straight before him fronting stood;
nor failed he to hit him,
Just where the double breadth of strap
Which did his tender skin defend,
Enrag'd, that fruitless from his hand
And back he to his comrade troop
But, as he yielded, after him
Seeing the weighty stones which roll'd
Many, to moor the galleys sharp;
Smote on his bosom, near the neck,
And as a top is whirl'd around,
As by the stroke of father Jove
Thus then fell sudden in the dust
Out of his hand he dropt the spear;
And helmet; and around him clang'd
Hoping to drag his body off;
and thick their bolts they darted,
In showers; yet not one of them
Nor closely stab him; for in front
Nay, nor did any of the rest
With hands uplifting bare him out
His speedy coursers, which in rear,
These to the city carried him,
But when unto the ford they reach'd
There from the chariot on earth
they rested him, and o'er him
Pour'd water: then he breath'd anew
On to his knees, from out his mouth
But soon again back on to earth
he sank, and dark night cover'd
His wilder'd eyen; for the bolt
Meanwhile the Argives, when they saw
the son of Enops, pierc'd him;
Whom erst to Enops tending kine
Bare on the banks of Satnious.
stabbing his side: and round him
So in the dust he fell, the earth
deep in his skin, and resting
Upon such staff, doth better walk
down to the house of Pluto."
And chiefly did the soul bestir
The son of Telamon; for he
And, at the foe retreating, quick
Just where the head and neck are join'd,
and where the spine is ended,
There enter'd it, and keenly shear'd
So down the hero dropt; but ere
his knees and shanks had fail'd him,
On to the earth much sooner shot
his head and mouth and nostrils.
Have ye not paid a costly life
To me not worthless seemeth he,
Mayhap as brother or as son
Beknoweth him; for sooth his form
He spake, but knew him well: and grief
Then with his long spear Acamas,
who by the feet would drag him.
Tam'd by my spear; nor long, I ween,
To leave a kinsman in his halls,
Offspring of Phorbas rich in flocks,
But now, beneath his eyebrow thrust,
the spear drove out the pupil
And issued thro' his head behind.
Sank sitting to the ground; until
the foe, his sharp sword drawing,
Smote him in middle of the neck,
and dash'd both head and helmet
Off to the ground, albeit still
Thro' the eye's socket. He the head,
and spake a word of boasting.
and bid the sire and tender mother
Ay, for the spouse of Promachus
Never again may joy to see
He spake; and all the Troïans
And each did singly look around
Utter for me, ye Muses, now,
The people's shepherd, in the flank;
His vitals; but the hero's soul
so there did darkness veil his eyen.
But most of all, Oïleus' son,
For of Achaians none to him
was peer, on foot to follow
When Jove did counsel flight to men,
and hearts were smit with panic."

div.head: BOOK XV

l.seg: fleeing, and up the moat they clamber'd
In panic, and full many fell
But on the tops of Ida
Jove woke from slumber, at the side
So stood he, starting up, and view'd
The latter routed in the fray;
But Hector lying on the plain
he saw, and, round him sitting,
His comrades: fainting he at heart,
dropt blood from mouth and nostrils,
And painfully did gasp; sith not
Had smote him. Him with pity view'd
the sire of gods and mortals,
And with a direful scowling glance
Hath godlike Hector stopt from war,
Nor know I, whether haply now
Thypelf shalt first the folly taste,
Or dost forget, how once aloft
A pair of anvils from thy feet,
and round thy hands a fetter,
so thou in clouds and æther hungest.
Yet of their concourse was there none
I caught, them clutching one by one
I toss'd from off the threshold,
Till swooning he the earth might reach.
Nor yet did this appease me,
Smarting with anguish for the fate
Whom, with the wind of Boreas,
So thou to thickly peopled Cos
But thence my power rescued him,
Restor'd him to his proper home
Now, that from trickeries thou cease,
For which thou camest from the gods
Thus when he spake, with terror quail'd
"I call to witness now this earth
And dripping water of the Styx,
Greatest and direst is of oaths:
yea, by thy head so sacred,
And our own early bed, -by which
Not surely for the sake of me
I ween, his proper notion doth
Sith at the galleys he beheld
But (let me say it) eke to him
Into that path to go, wherein,
The sire of mortals and of gods
Sudden reverse his bent of soul,
thy heart and mine to follow.
But if sincerely from thy breast
Hie thee among the tribes of gods
That she amid the wide array
May speed her, and the earnest word
From battle to withdraw him straight
And breathe in him new might, and give
Back from the battle shall he turn,
In flight they hurry. He in turn
Patroclus, soon to fall in death
when many a youth in Orcus
Calls him to vengeance; with the rest,
But great Achilles, for his friend
As first I undertook for him
Upon the day, when by my knees
But, from the mounts of Ida, up
As darteth forth the mind of man,
"In such a place was I, and such,"-
All gather'd in the hall of Jove.
Upstarted from their seats at once,
She of the rest took no account,
Troth! Saturn's son hath scared thee,
Do thou, in chambers of the gods,
wilt hear, with all the immortals,
doth show us; nor, I reckon,
Will he to all alike give joy,
Such words did queenly Juno speak,
and took her seat among them.
Then in the hall of Jove the gods
Forcing upon her lips a smile,
Whether we will some limit set
Of word, or haply of constraint:
in strength and might is foremost.
he list to offer, take ye.
For in the battle fallen is
and pour'd an utterance of wailing.
"Blame not my purpose, O ye gods,
Albeit eke for me be doom
Struck by the thunderbolt of Jove,
So spake he, and accordant bade
His coursers, and himself would don
Then other turmoil had been wrought
By the fierce wrath of Jupiter
But that Athene, smit with fear
Out o'er the threshold rush'd, and left
And off his head the helmet drew
Then taking from his sturdy hand
the brazen spear, she fix'd it

div.note: Orcus, the underworld. This word is Latin, not Homeric: so Tartarus.

l.seg: Troth! vainly hast thou ears to hear;
thy shame and wits are perish'd
Dost wish, thyself with many a woe
Despite thy anger, back to haste
And to the other blessed ones
And Argives; but on us will come
Thou therefore for thy bonny son
For one than him far mightier
Slain either is, or shall be soon :
and troth, the task were toilsome
Of all the world the kith and kin
Thus speaking, she upon his throne
"Jove biddeth both of you to come
But when arriv'd ye be, and raise
And sat upon her throne; but they,
At Gargarus, and round his form
was wreath'd a cloud of incense:
Stood they; nor when the twain he saw,
That to the bidding of his spouse
The rightful errand of my words,
Returning mid the tribes of gods
But if he choose not to obey
Me to withstand: for I than him
And earlier of birth; and yet
To hold pretension as my peer,
And as when hailstorm from the clouds
Chill'd by the gust of Boreas,
And standing close in front, address'd
Returning mid the tribes of gods
But if thou choose not to obey
Then doth he threaten thee in turn,
with adverse force to hie him
With him to shrink; sith he than
thee in force is greatly stronger,
And earlier in birth; and yet
To hold pretension as his peer,
"Ye Spirits! troth, tho' grand he be,
If me, who am in rank his peer,
For we, three equal brethren are,
First Jove, next me, then Aïdes,
All things in threefold lot are cast:
each hath his share of honour.
To me the hoary brine for aye
When lots we shook; to Aïdes
Jove had as his the ample sky
But Earth to all is common yet,
So will not I by Jove's decree
Abide within his own third share,
But truly let him not by force
More seemly were it and more right
Whom he himself begat, to scold
These, when he sendeth urgent word,
This must I bear to Jupiter
from thee, so stern and hardy?
Or wilt thou somewhat bend and yield?
Then Neptune, shaker of the earth,
Only my heart and soul are here
with words of gall to wrangle
But now, indignant though I am,
to this will I submit me.
Yet one thing will I farther say
and from my heart will threaten.
nor suffer us to storm it
And to the Argive arms to give
Know he: a feud betwixt us twain
and soon the Achaian heroes miss'd him.
"Go now, dear shining one! and seek
The god who circleth quaking Earth
did hear of yore our battle,
Was it, that he did earlier
Beneath my puissance: else, the toil
But now within thy hands assume
And in their faces flaunting it,
And in him waken mighty force
Unto their galleys reach in flight
and to the flood of Helle.
of word and work bethink me,
So spake he; nor Apollo then
And down from Ida's tops he went,
So found he godlike Hector, son
Sitting, nor prostrate still; for he
Then, near beside him standing, spake
Sittest thou faint at heart? perchance
To him, with puny vigour left
"Who art thou, noblest of the gods!
that face to face dost ask me?
How Ajax, good at need, did smite
with huge stone on my bosom,
While I his comrades slew; and quell'd
And verily I thought to see
the dead, and house of Pluto,
Upon this day: so near did I
"Now cheer thee! such reserve of war
Hath forward to befriend thee sent
And I myself, in front of them
As when a charger, closely kept,
Bursting his halter, o'er the plain
Accustom'd in the flood to bathe
his head, and off his shoulders
Rusheth the mane abroad; and he
Lightly his knees then carry him
So too with supple foot and knee
Soon as he heard the voice divine,
And as when dogs or rustic men
and rocks that cheat the footstep,
At length, beneath their shouting rous'd,
With swords and with twohanded pikes
But when they Hector saw amid
They trembled, and the soul of all
And good in standing fight: but few
When in full mote Achaian youths *
Who then with kindly soul harangu'd
and spake his word among them:
Yet verily each heart of us
Lately had perish'd by the hands
But now some heav'nly god anew
So now, as I forebode, again
will be for here he standeth
But come, as I the word shall speak,
Back to the galleys bid we all
But for ourselves, who boast to be
If we may haply stagger him:
and he, I ween, tho' eager,
Will shrink to meet the Danaï
and in their troop to mingle."
He spake; and they all willingly
Back to the Achaian galleys hied
Forward in plump the Troians rush'd,
and at their head was Hector,
With lengthy strides; and in his front
Clad round his shoulders with a cloud;
Dreadful, with shag of fringes rough,
Worker of copper, gave to Jove
This ægis holding in his hands,
But eke the Argives clos'd their ranks
From either side arose: then leapt
Partly were fix'd within the limbs
Ere the fair skin they might attain,
So long from either side the darts
But when he flouted full in face
Shaking his arm, and eke himself
Was all bewitch'd with fear, and straight
And as, amid a herd of kine
do two wild beasts make havoc,
Fill'd them with fear, and glory gave
Thereon each hero slew his man,
Æneias next for death and spoil
But Medon was of meaner birth,
Long time in Phylake; for he
A kinsman of his father's bride
And Sphelus was his father call'd,
As in the foremost ranks he fled;
and thrust the weapon thro' him.

div.note: Elder chiefs and princes spoke in the council; the younger men only in the public assembly, and then but rarely, it seems.

l.seg: While from the slain the victors stript
Then Hector rais'd his voice aloft
There, on the spot, for him will I
But him before our city's wall
he lash'd his horses on the shoulder,
With his broad foot did easily
The scarp'd embankments of the trench,
Solid and broad; so far, as when
Over this road in ample squares
With costly ægis on his arm.
The Achaian rampart, e'en as when
a child lays low his sandheap;
Who, where the billow lately dash'd,
a playful wall hath built him
Of sand, and when his sport is done,
with foot or hand o'throws it :
So then by thee, bright Eïus,*
Of Argives vain was made, and eke
But, checking by the galleys' side
And each to other made appeal,
and to all gods in heaven
Did every one with lifted hands
Made prayer to the gods, his hand
Burning to thee the costly limbs
of sheep or eke of bullock,
Besought thee for a safe return,
The Troians also, at the voice
Of wind impress it; for 'tis this,
And now, their coursers driving in,
at poop of galley fought they,
Mingled in battle, foe to foe,
which on the decks were stored,
Fasten'd with clamps for naval fight,
and tipt at end with copper.
He in the tent was seated
So long, and sooth'd his heart with talk;
and as a balm of anguish,
Drugs o'er the painful wound he spread,
But, when he after was aware,
were fill'd with scream and tumult;
Then loudly groan'd he, and straightway
Smote on his thighs, and pour'd
May I beside thee stay; for lo!
But thee, let now thy squire amuse;
Must quickly hie me, if perchance
I win him to the combat.
Who knoweth, if, with heaven's aid,
May stir his spirit? useful is
He, with such words, on speedy feet departed;
to ward them from the galleys.
Nor might the Troians yet break
And force a passage to the tents
As by a cunning workman's hands,
Taught perfectly, the rule doth draw
was drawn the fight and combat.
But Hector made his chief attack
Around one galley had the twain
The Troian, out to drive his foe
and wrap in flames the galley,
Nor he, the assailant to repel,
Then gallant Ajax with his spear
So with a loud crash down he fell,
and dropt the brand beside him.
But Hector, when before his eyes
Laid prostrate in the dust, in front,
Not yet, in such a strait, my friends!
But save the son of Clytius,
nor let the Achaians strip him,
So spake he, and with shining spear
And miss'd him: but the weapon hit
of yore had slain a hero :
Then quitted he his early home,
and dwelt, as squire, with Ajax.
To this man, as by Ajax's side
he stood, the spear of Hector
Enter'd his head above the ear;
Into the dust from off the poop
But Ajax shudder'd at the sight,
The son of Mastor, whom we both
With honour like to parents dear,
hath slain. Where now thy arrows
Death swiftly dealing, and the bow
He spake, and Teucer caught the word,
and ran, and stood beside him,
Holding within his hand the bow
Fraught full with arrows: quick he shot
By the first arrow Cleitus fell,
Holding within his hands the reins.
He with the steeds was busied :
For there kept hé; -for kindly aid

div.note: Eius is clearly a surname of Apollo: its meaning is less certain. Sophocles makes it leïus.
Cousin. In 20, 238, Clytius is brother to Priam.

l.seg: Where thickest justled were the ranks:
On to himself; which none of all,
For deep within his neck, behind,
So from the chariot he dropt;
And on him many a charge he laid,
at hand to keep the horses,
Then Teucer took a second shaft
And would an end of war have made
Had he the chieftain slain, in whom
Nor this the canny mind escap'd
For while as in the blameless bow
Stray'd other way, and from his hand
Then Teucer shudder'd at the sight
All rudely, who from out my hands
hath cast the bow and arrow,
That stoutly it might carry home
"Dear fellow, leave thy bow in peace
and all thy sheaves of arrows;
But in thy hands take lengthy spear
Then, tho' the mastery they win,
So spake he: Teucer then replac'd
But round his shoulders quickly slung
And on his gallant head he put
Lastly, he grasp'd a valiant spear,
Hied on his path, and speedily
ran up, and stood by Ajax.
But Hector, when his eye descried
Be men, my friends! nor negligent
For lo! these eyes are witness,
the bolts by Jove are crippled.
And eke whomso he lessen will,
As now the Argives willeth he
to crush, but us to succour.
But fight ye at the galleys, all
in plump: and if for any
The doom of death allotted be,
To die, if need be; but his wife
Back to their native land belov'd
He, by such words, in every breast
But Ajax, he on other side
Or save our fortunes, beating back
the Achaian ships, then hope ye
Each man his native land to reach
urge them, our fleet to kindle?
Not to a dance he summoneth,
For us no counsel and intent,
Foot against foot and hand to hand
Better it is, now once for all
to live or else to perish,
Men who are noway peers to us, -
on the shore's edge, thus vainly."
He, by such words, in every breast
And prince of Phocis: Ajax hent
Comrade of Phyleus' son, and prince
But Meges saw, and rush'd on him:
Flinching aside; so him the thrust
But Croismus' bosom, in his stead,
to meet the spear was destin'd:
Crashing he fell, and Meges stript
to wield the spear right skilful,
This man, approaching, with the spear
And pierc'd his midmost buckler thro';
but the close corslet sav'd him,
Which, at the hollows jointed well,
Brought it of yore from Ephyra,
His host Euphetes, prince of men,
on him as gift bestow'd it,
Which from the body of his son
But Meges on his helmet's top,
Thrust heavy with his spear, and broke
Fell all dishonour'd in the dust,
While Dolops yet the fight maintain'd,
Meanwhile as succour to the foe
He on the flank approach'd unseen,
Right thro' the hapless Troian's breast,
Then rush'd the heroes twain to strip
But to the kinsmen of the slain
Who, while the foemen were afar,
Signal, and dwelt in Priam's court,
On him did Hector cast reproach,
Or doth it scape thee, how the foe
But follow! for no longer choice
Against the Argives: choice is none,
but them to slay, or suffer
He spake, and led the way: behind,
"Oh friends, be men, and in your souls
And each to other bashful be
are safe, and few are slaughter'd :
So spake he; and his comrades
eke themselves for fight were earnest.
They in their inmost bosom cast
his word, and fenc'd the galleys
With barrier of brass; but still
Nor swifter is of foot; nor e'en,
as thou, is bold in battle,
If from the ranks thou wouldest leap,
sith he had the youth excited;
Who, leaping from the foremost ranks,
Turning on every side his gaze :
As from him flew the javelin :
nor did he aim it vainly,
But struck in middle of the breast
So with a loud crash down he dropt,
and o'er him clang'd his armour.
as hound on fawn that's wounded,
Which, as it springeth from its lair,
But godlike Hector saw, and ran
Skulking away, like some wild beast,
Which, having haply kill'd a dog
So hurried Nestor's son. At him
But quickly fac' Round, when first
he reach'd his troop of comrades.
Who alway waken'd in their heart
The meed of glory, till he cast
Her pray'r ill-omen'd: this was Jove
The blaze before his eyes to see
Driving the Troians from the ships
Who rag'd, as eke may Ares' self,
Foam circled from his gnashing teeth:
Gleam'd his two eyen; but the helm
Amid the battle: sith to him
Was Jove himself; who him alone
for, troth! his hour of greatness
Soon to expire was destin'd: sith
the day of doom was bringing.
To force a passage, troth! he wish'd,
Where thickest he the crowd beheld
not even so he forc'd them,
like to some rock of ocean,
Of whistling gales, and turbid waves
which forth are belch'd against it:
So mid the war the Danaï
But he amid the scuffle leapt,
Plumping, as on a galley sharp
Whose bulk by cloud-born squalls is fed
and all by foam is cover'd.
The direful blast against the sail
Shrink with alarm; for close they ride
So direfully the heart was rent
Then as the kine, on whom may come
In the great pasture of a marsh,
Graze; and a herdsman in their midst
doth stand, not yet well trained
Thus, as the cows move, moveth he:
The beast a heifer hath devour'd,
From such a father worser far,
to fight, to run, to counsel;
For he in knowledge might defy
Who by his fall to Hector gave
For, backward turning him, he tript
Which, reaching to his heels, he bare,
By this entangled, down he fell,
right on his back: the helmet,
Bang'd on the hard ground suddenly,
ran up and stood before him,
And in his bosom plung'd the spear,
Slew him: but these unable were,
Rescue to bring him; for themselves
They in full gaze the galleys held;
Which foremost on the strand were haul'd:
Yet cluster'd densely round their tents,
while each to each spake threat and counsel.
Touching the knees of every man,
"O friends, be men, and in your souls
Fearing the blame of other men;
His children and his tender wife,
his lot of land, and parents,
or whether death have ta'en them.
On sake of these dear absent ones
I by your knees beseech you
Firmly to hold against the foe
He, by such words, in every breast
And maid Athene from their eyes
full light was shed around them,
Alike, where stood their inmost ships,
So knew they Hector good at need,
Who from the galleys stood aloof
and fear'd to join the combat,
And who beside the galleys sharp
No longer then it pleas'd the soul
But he along the galleys' decks
Wielded within his hands a pike,
As on the horses' backs is seen
Who, picking from a numerous herd,
And, from the country driving them,
Stand gazing at him: calmly he
From back to back, as listeth him;
So Ajax on to many decks
and rais'd his voice to heaven;
Would, mid the crowd of Troïans
To save the galleys and the tents.-
But he, in guise no other, than
On to a flock of winged fowl
Or geeese or cranes or longnecked swans;
By his own mighty hand press'd on,
and with him urg'd the people.
Then once again beside the ships
Thou mightest deem that men in war
Each upon other rush'd: so fierce
hop'd not, but there to perish:
But each man of the Troïans
With fire to kindle yonder fleet
With such forebodings of the heart
At length did Hector touch the poop
To Troy,-nor brought him back again
Fighting about this ship, I say,
Did hand to hand each other waste;
As with a single heart possest,
With axes and with mighty swords
rimm'd with black edge of iron,

div.note: Elder chiefs and princes spoke in the council; the younger men only in the public assembly, and then but rarely, it seems.
iron. The Greek epithet is strictly blackbound or blackrimmed. I do not feel sure of the sense.

l.seg: Some fell from hands upon the ground,
and dark earth flow'd with carnage.
But Hector, when he once had seiz'd
the poop, no more would yield it,
But grasp'd the adornments in his hand,
"Fire! bring ye fire! and eke yourselves,
Jove granteth now to us a day,
Their ships to capture; which, despite
And laid much misery on us,
Who, when I eager was to fight
Forbade my marching to the war
and stopt my train of people.
So spake he: they with greater zeal
No longer then did Ajax wait;
for sore the darts distress'd him:
Yet but withdrew him step by step,
And left the even galley's deck,
Upon a bench of seven feet :
thence with his spear repell'd he
The Troians from the ship, whoe'er
Be men, my friends! nor negligent
Deem ye, that now reserves we keep
No city hold we near at hand,
Wherein might beaten troops be hid
But, on the plain of Troïans
Leaning against the depth of sea,
Frenzied he spake, and on the word
Rush'd onward with the wasting fire,
Him with long spear did Ajax thrust,
So laid he prostrate hand to hand

div.note: Auxiliar. I imagine the Homeric aosseo to be a softened sound of aoxeo, related to aero, as in Latin auxilium to augeo.

div.head: BOOK XVI

l.seg: So far'd the hardy struggle there,
Pouring adown his cheek warm tears,
as some dark source of waters,
At sight of him divine Achilles
Who, tripping at her mother's side
And, to be lifted in her arms,
Or is to thee alone some news
Either of whom, by fate's decree
how now their bands are wasted,
Lock not thy breast, but speak it out;
and so, we both shall know it."
To him with heavy groans didst thou,
Take no offence: so fierce distress
For all who formerly bare lead,
with many a drug are busied,
Me never may such anger seize,
as that which thou dost foster!
Woe on thy valour! why should
men of future ages bless thee,
If thou from Argos willest not
O cruel! never sire to thee
Nor Thetis was thy mother: nay,
so wild, so rugged is thy bosom.
But if, with heart foreboding, thou
Which, from the lips of Jupiter,
Yet me send forward quick, and lend
Of Myrmidons; so may I bring
some ray of light to Argos.
And on my shoulders grant to me
Stand off from battle, and hereby
Gain respite of their toil;-and short
from off the tents and galleys,
Unto the city back to drive
To him Achilles, fleet of foot,
Naught from the lips of Jupiter
to fill with awe my bosom.
Only, my heart and soul are here
When one, who is in sway and force
His equal to bereave of right,
and back his prize to ravish.
Grim anguish deem I this; sith I
Whom by my spear I purchased,
Her from my hands did Atreus' son,
Snatch from me back again, as tho'
But leave we these things, past and gone;
Troth! thought I, never would I stay
Till the loud onset of the war
Yet thou, my own illustrious arms
Sith, like a cloud of dusky blue,
The Argives lean, hemm'd every way,
Of land remaining; while on them
Cheery: for they no longer view
else would they fill the gulleys
To me were kindly minded: now,
Nor longer in the skilful grasp
Rageth the spear, which once would save
Nor rumour reacheth me as yet,
From hated mouth: nay, but the voice
to save the fleet from ruin,
they of sweet return despoil us.
But thou, as in thy mind I set
And glory; who with public train
When from the fleet the foe is scar'd,
and cheaper wilt thou make me:
Nor, O my friend, in foeman's play
But turn thee backward, soon as thou
some ray of light hast open'd
Unto the fleet, and leave the hosts
Would it might be, - O father Jove,
That of all Troïans who live,
and eke of all the Argives,
Not one from slaughter might escape,
Then would we twain, of Ilium
But Ajax might no longer wait;
for sore the darts distress'd him.
The mind of Jove o'ermaster'd him,
The shining helm a rattle kept:
The left, which stedfast ever held
Yet they, with darts hard pressing round,
to dash him off avail'd not.
A noisome gasping cramp'd his breast,
Flow'd from his limbs on every side,
And respite gain; but every where
Utter for me, ye Muses, now,
How first the deadly fire was cast
Above its head of metal smote
And lopt the metal sheer away :
Brandish'd in vain the crippled shaft:
And shudder'd in his blameless soul
Of Argive battle, bent to yield
but they on the sharp galley scatter'd
The busy fire curl round the poop,
Smote on his thighs with double palm,
The spurting of the foeman's fire
And if the galleys captur'd be,
and I the folk may gather."
quick in dazzling brass encas'd him.
First on his shins the dapper greaves,
He fasten'd; next, to guard his chest,
Borne by Pelides fleet of foot,
About his shoulders next he slung
Brazen of edge; and after it
And on his gallant head he put
Lastly, he pick'd two valiant spears,
Alone, of all the equipments borne
The lance he took not, -weighty, huge;
Beside might brandish: none but he,
For his dear father Cheiron gat,
to be the death of heroes.)
The steeds, right speedily he bade
Than whom he only honour'd more
Who eke most faithful was to him
Chesnut and Spotted, both of whom
Whom, as along a lea she graz'd
Who, mortal as he was, kept pace
All in their tents with panoply
Hold in their heart, and on the fells
from some dark source of waters
From off the surface: forth they belch death-gore,
Such then did all the Myrmidons
Fierce circle: and in midst of them
Urging the shielded men to war
Fifty the galleys sharp of prow
His comrades, came with every one,
Five governours he made, to whom

div.note: Homer perhaps conceived of Harpy as a flying mare. The Harpies elsewhere are ravenous monsters, griffins. Harpy there may be translated Rapacious, but here Rapid.
