The Greek root Harp is the Latin Rap; and in Homer the cognate roots Karp, Kraf, denote Speed. Some may choose to render it, "The Harpy Spry-foot bare of yore": See 19, 400.

l.seg: Who claim'd Spercheius as his sire,
Streaming from Jove. Him Peleus' child,
Yet Borus was his sire esteem'd,
Who boundless dower paid for her,
Daughter of Phylas, gave to life,
Beheld, and lov'd her, as she danc'd
Lay at her side by stealth; and she
Eudorus, swift of foot to run,
But when at length the goddess, who
Woke him to light of day, and he
But aged Phylas took the child
The son of Maemalus; but he
to wield the spear was signal
Beside their leaders, then to all
Which at the Troïans did ye
During my day of anger, when
O cruel son of Peleus! troth,
on gall thy mother fed thee;
Heartless! who keepest by the ships
Right homeward let us wend our way
To me, assembled, thus ye spake

div.note: Hermeas slew Argus, who persecuted Io. (2, 103.)

l.seg: for which ye erst did hanker;
Here then to fight with Troïans
He, by such words, in every breast
And when their monarch's voice they heard,
more close the ranks did pack them.
For some highroofed house a wall,
to screen the force of tempests;
So fitted then were bossy shields
For helmet was by helmet screen'd,
With ridges bright each other touch'd:
And in the front of all, two men
To head the Myrmidons in fight.
Into his tent: there open'd he
the lid, that clos'd a coffer,
Dainty and varied; which to him
Had plac'd for convoy on the ship,
And cloaks, a shelter from the blast,
Nor he to other god would pour,
Out of the coffer this he took
and fill'd with wine the goblet.
Then standing midmost in his court,
and Jove the thunderloving knew it:
Dwell with unwashen feet thy seers
In former time to my request
Now, once again implor'd, do thou

div.note: The twins, named Molions after their mother (as is supposed), are regarded by Homer as having Actor for their titular father, Neptune for their true father. Cteatus and Eurytus are their names, 2, 621. See also 13, 185 and 207.
The Achaians. The phrase here suggests, that the Epeians of Elis were not strictly Achaians.

l.seg: Knoweth to combat, or his hands
Then only, when I hie with him
But after from the galleys he
the din of fight have chased,
May he to me forthwith return
With comrades, round his side who fight,
Such was his word of prayer;
Far from the ships to drive away
He granted; but the safe return
from battle, this he gave not.
and pray'r to Jove the Father, -
Then forth proceeded from the tent,
and still in soul did hanker
March'd steadily, till on they rush'd
like wasps, who hold their houses
By the roadside; whom children weak
are wont in sport to harry,
With valiant bosom forth they fly,
Then from the galleys sallied out;
Be men, my friends, nor negligent
So shall we honour Peleus' son,
Beside the galleys, and the squires
So shall we teach to Atreus' son,
He spake; and in every breast
Quick on the Troïans they fell,
But when the Troians saw the chief,
The heart of all held grim debate,
At length his anger had renounc'd,
and chose the bonds of friendship.
So each did singly look around,
Where most were justled, near the poop
Who brought them from far Amydon
On the right shoulder him it hit;
Down in the dust, and round his corpse
Thus from the beach he dravethem off,
and quench'd the fire that sparkled ;
And there the half-burnt ship was left.
The Troian army took to flight:
As when from off the summit high
And every lofty peak is shown,
And from behind the cloven dun
The foeman's fire, did breath regain:
yet was no pause of battle.
For not yet did the Troïans
Scar'd from the dusky ships, beneath
Thereon each hero slew his man,
Among the leaders. First of them,
With pointed spear, and crush'd the bone:
he to the earth fell headlong.
And Phyleus' son, in ambush hid,
Piercing the pillar of his leg,
where thew of man is thickest:
The weapon's point his sinews rent,
Rush'd angry, and before the dead stood firm:
Ere Maris might a wound implant,

div.note: Homer perhaps conceived of Harpy as a flying mare. The Harpies elsewhere are ravenous monsters, griffins. Harpy there may be translated Rapacious, but here Rapid. The Greek root Harp is the Latin Rap; and in Homer the cognate roots Karp, Kraf, denote Speed. Some may choose to render it, "The Harpy Spry-foot bare of yore": See 19, 400.
Hermeas slew Argus, who persecuted Io. (2, 103.)

l.seg: Into the shoulder, brake the bone,
and stript the limb of sinew:
So, with a loud crash down he fell,
Thus by two brethren brethren twain,
Whose sire the dread Chimæra rear'd,
Smote on his neck, and warm'd the sword
There crimson Death his eyen press'd,
With spears they miss'd each other,
Darting in vain; thereat with swords
snapt at the hilt his sabre.
But deep the foeman's griding sword
His head by skin alone hung on,
and there his limbs lay helpless.
when he would mount his horses:
So fell he from the car, and mist
Right through the mouth of Erymas:
The white bones splintering: and out
the teeth were dash'd: his eyen
Were filled both with blood: but it
out of his mouth and nostrils
He spurted gasping: so did Death
As noisome wolves on lambs or kids
To hurl his weapon: he in turn,
With breadth of shoulder alway screen'd
Had shelter from the noisy darts
In sooth, the foe's recruited strength
he saw, and tide of fortune;
Yet stood he, even so, awhile,
But as when, after sky serene,

div.note: Homer names the sire, Amisódarus.

l.seg: Cometh a cloud o'er heaven's face,
So shriek and terror, scowl and squall,
and left his host of people,
The pole short snapping, many left
But they with scream and panic
Fill'd all the paths, once that their
bands were scatter'd: and to heaven
Back from the galleys and the tents
But where Patroclus saw the folk
for he on Hector cheer'd them,
To overhend him bent; but him
And as beneath a tempest's weight
all the dark earth is loaded
Upon a day of autumn, when
Jove poureth down his greatest glut of water
Jove poureth down, if he with men
Who in assembly of the folk
Then all the flowing hollow brooks
from the high clouds are filled,
And many a steep outstanding cliff
is rent by gush of waters,
Which streaming to the purple sea
Resound with mighty moan, the while
the works of men they ruin :
Patroclus, when his course had cut
Back to the galleys hemm'd them in,
Their aim, the city-walls to reach :
but twixt the ships and river
So, with a loud crash down he dropt.
By second sally. Close he sat,
From out his hands the bridles
Dropt to the ground. But close at hand,
thro' his right cheek the foeman
Urg'd-on the spear, betwixt his teeth;
then o'er the car's rim drew him
Haul'd by the weapon; as a man,
With line and dazzling brass may draw
So from the seat with shining spear
And cast him full upon his fàce:
so there his spirit left him.
Thereafter, with a stone he struck
Upon the head direct, and clave
Headlong to earth he fell, and him
All these successive cast he down
But when Sarpedon, son of Jove,
in sooth, ye now are nimble.
But I myself will meet this man;
so shall I well inform me,
Who is the chief, that swayeth wide
And who of many a gallant soul
He spake, and from his chariot
leapt to the ground in armour.
leapt also, when he saw it,
Out of the chariot: and they,
With pity seeing them, the child
Did to his sister and his wife,
My heart by double thoughts is torn,
Whether to snatch him still alive
And plant him safe on Lycia,
in his fat soil of plenty,

div.note: Sacred fish. I cannot be pleased by the interpretation, a fish of God, i. e. huge. Whales and grampuses are not caught by line and hook, nor sharks by one man sitting on a rock. I think a special kind of fish must be intended.

l.seg: Or unto death resign him, slain
"O son of Saturn, grim and dire,
A man, who mortal is of birth,
Do it: but we, the other gods,
not all shall praise thy doing.
This also will I say, and thou
If to his own abode and home
Think, may not other eke of gods
Born from immortal gods; in whom
But if thy son is dear, and if
His body now resign to death,
But, when his time of life is gone
and breath hath left his body;
Then balmy Slumber send and Death,
His brothers and his kinsfolk there
With flames, with pillar and with mound;
which are the dead man's honour."
the sire of gods and mortals.
He shed from heaven gory drops
Who far from home, in loamy Troy,
When they to shorter distance came,
With shining spear. He groan'd, and fell,
and gasp'd, and breath'd no longer.
The others swerv'd apart: the yoke creak'd loudly,
Were tangled, sith the outer horse
in dust and death was prostrate.
For, drawing from his brawny thigh
In sallied he, and slash'd away the out-horse,
The living steeds came clear and straight,
But the two heroes met once more
Missing him on the left: then he
But hit, where round the ceaseless heart
Then toppled he, as may an oak
Gnashing his teeth, the gory dust
And as a lion, mid the herd
Picking hath slain a flame-hued bull,
And he beneath the lion's jaws
"Glaucus, my friend! thou warriour mid heroes!
Now, if thy heart be keen and true,
And rouse their chiefest men to fight
Next eke thyself, my friend! for me
For I in after-days to thee
Strip me, who fall before the fleet
and urge my host of people."
Thus when he spake, all-ending Death
The victor, tramping on his breast,
drew out the spear, and with it
Follow'd the membrane of his heart
Eager to flee, sithence the car
But anguish grim on Glaucus came,
to hear his friend adjure him.
Grasping he pinch'd his arm, thereat,
Shot from the lofty wall, to ward
"Hear me, O prince! who haply art
When man in trouble is, as now
Is wrung, nor may the blood be stanch'd;
Nor firmly can I hold my spear,
Whose Father standeth off, nor would
But thou, O prince! heal thou my wound,
And grant me puissance to exhort
While I, to save our chieftain's corpse,
Forthwith the fretting pangs he quell'd,
and round the gash so troublous
He stanch'd the dusky gore, and
breath'd high prowess in his bosom.
and in his heart was joyful,
He rous'd their chiefest men to fight
with mighty strides he hied him,
And standing close in front of them
Who far from friends and land of birth
In sake of thee: but thou, methinks,
not eager art to aid us.
But friends! stand nobly at his side,
The person of the dead, enrag'd
When thus he spake, the Troians all
from head to foot were seized
Held him a bulwark, tho' of soil
Came with him: in them he himself
Prostrate the chieftain lies, who first
Sarpedon. Oh! if now we might
- And from his shoulders strip his arms,
With ruthless weapon some lay low,
who fight their lord to rescue."
So charged he: but they themselves
Around the carcase of the slain
And Jove a deadly darkness spread
When of the Myrmidons was slain
Who as a prince had oversway'd
In former day; but having slain
a kinsman bold, he hied him
in train of battle sent him
Him gallant Hector with a stone
hit, as he touch'd the carcase,
Upon the head direct, and clave
Prone on the corpse he fell, and him
Straight thro' the foremost ranks he dash'd,
And on the Troians, diddest plunge,
With a huge stone the hero hit
Back then the foremost ranks withdrew,
a man may fling on trial
In public game, or e'en in war
so far the Achaians drave them.
a man who dwelt in Hellas,
Him Glaucus wounded with the spear
in fleet pursuit he caught him.
So with a loud crash down he fell.
Sith fallen was a noble man;
So then, assembled round the corpse
stood they: nor yet the Achaians
Prowess forgat, but on the foe
A priest was made, and by the folk
e'en as a god was honour'd.
and jaw did strike, and quickly
Out of his members life was flown,
Hoping to hit him, as he march'd
He, seeing it, by scanty space
In earth alighted; where its force
made the shaft's end to quiver,
[Thus, far behind him in the earth
and at his foeman scoff'd he:
My spear a thorough end had brought,
had I attain'd to hit thee."
The might of every man, whoe'er
Mortal are thou alsó: but if
I might in turn be lucky
With a full hit, then thou, so bold
To me shalt glory yield, and life
He spake; but brave Patroclus then
Not for reproachful words, my friend!
From carcase of the slain; but first
must earth hold many a hero.
Issue of battle is from might;
Therefore no weight of words we need,
He spake, and led the way; behind,
As when the woodman's steady axe
From many a hand drops ponderous,
and far is heard its echo;
So from the broad expanse of land
Hard was it for a thoughtful man
So was he wrapt from head to foot
in dust and gore and weapons.
And alway throng'd they round the dead,
Swarm noisy thro' the stalls in spring,
when pails with milk are wetted;
Thus (say I) round the dead they throng'd.
Did Jove a moment turn away
to watch it, and bethought him
Waste with the weapon, and despoil
should the bluff toil be doubled.
Still further should the bonny squire
And hem them to the city-wall
and doom more lives to carnage.
So into Hector first of all
Who knew the sacred scales of Jove,
To flight betook him, calling loud
that all should flee beside him.
stood firm, but all were scatter'd
In terror, sith their king they saw
Prone in assembly of the dead :
When o'er his body Saturn's child
Then from his shoulders stript the foe
To his companions gave, to bear

div.note: Homer names the sire, Amisódarus.

l.seg: "Go now, dear Shining one! draw forth
Cleanse off the cloudy gore, apart;
Around his sacred form; and send,
His brothers and his kinsfolk there
With flames, with pillar and with mound,
which are the dead man's honour."
So, spake he: nor Apollo then
And down from Ida's tops he went
to the grim cry of battle.
Cleans'd off the cloudy gore, apart;
Around his sacred form; and sent,
From evil doom of murky death
But alway than the wit of man
the wit of Jove is higher :
Who then into his bosom breath'd
Then who was first and who was last
a sport of death and plunder,
All these he slaughter'd; but the rest
did each of flight bethink them.
Patroclus climb'd on high; and thrice
But when the fourth time on he sped,
a man than thee far stronger."
Aw'd by the immortal voice, the chief
But Hector in the Skæans kept
Or all his scatter'd folk exhort
to coop them in the city.
Yet youthful was he still for war:
Who on the banks of Sangarus
Like unto him, the son of Jove
Oh that, as weaker I than thee,
by so much I were stronger!
Not to thy joy then wouldest thou, mayhap,
This said, again the god was mix'd
Give order, mid the war to lash his coursers.
Hied him to enter mid the throng,
pass'd by, nor car'd to slay them;
Patroclus, he too on the earth
His left hand held the spear; his right,
Which rugged sparkled, heav'd it high;
But hurl'd it fiercely: not in vain;
but struck the squire of Hector,
Full in the forehead: both the brows were crush'd;
Out of his face his eyes were torn:
so plung'd he, like a diver,
and there did life forsake his body.
If somewhere on the fishful sea
to dive for oysters lik'd him,
He many a mouth might glut, from
As now upon the plain so light
So eke the Troïans, I ween,
With rush like to a lion's rush,
Hath on the bosom felt a wound,
and his own valour slays him ;-
But Hector, e't too on the earth
Which, both with empty craving maw,
Around a slaughter'd doe may fight
Hanker'd with ruthless brass to gash
But Hector, when he once had seiz'd the head,
As blowing from the East and South
Which, each upon the other, grind
With rustling scratch; and when they snap,
Made havoc, nor did either side
As round his corpse they fought:
had touch'd his noon of heaven,
Thenceforth from either side the darts
But from what time the Sun declin'd,
Out of the mall of darts they dragg'd
And from the itian din; and stripp'd
And at the foes, with bitter soul,
Upon them thrice he rush'd, in weight
With dreadful yell of battle: thrice,
But when the fourth time on he sped,
Then unto thee the fates unveil'd
For now the direful Shining one,
Behind the hero stood the god,
Who then with palm descending smote
his back and breadth of shoulders.
Dazzled and giddy were his eyes:
Rattled aloud: with dust and gore
But of a godlike man it screen'd
To wear it round his proper head
Next, in Patroclus' hand was snapt
Huge, weighty, stout, with iron point well-capt;
Down with its belt on earth was cast
Lastly, the princely son of Jove,
So stood he all aghast. Behind,
Betwixt his shoulders fix'd the spear,
Who, in swift feet and horsemanship and spear,
when he first of his car made trial :)
Who first at thee, O charioteer Patroclus!
Nor did subdue thee. Back he ran,
But from the wounded shoulder first
Listing not well in foemen's gripe
But he, beneath the god's assault
But Hector, when from far he saw
And stabb'd him in the lowest lap,
and draye, right thro', the weapon.
So dropt he with a crash,
and sore the Achaian host afflicted.
As when in fight a wiry boar
If on the mountain's top the twain
About a petty well, where each
would slake his thirst of water;
At length the panting hog is taught
In turn did Hector with the spear
Then, vaunting o'er the fallen foe
And in thy galleys lead away
A train of Troian women, reft
from home and day of freedom;
O simpleton! but, them to save,
Spank forth in garniture of war :
and with the spear am signal
Who, when to war thou camest, laid
such charge as this upon thee :-
Hither come not to me again,
The gory harness thou hast rent
So (deem I) then he spake, and troth!
To him, with puny vigour, thou,
And Jove, the child of Saturn, give
Full easily; for from my breast
If twenty heroes like to thee
They all should perish on the spot,
But me hath deadly Fate o'ermatch'd,
thou but third to slay me camest.
This also will I say; and thou
Eke not for thee abideth life
Standeth beside thee forceful Doom and Death,
While thus he spake, the end of death
Out of his members flew his soul,
and reach'd the house of Pluto,
Then gallant Hector to the corpse
With such address, he from the gash
Tramping upon the dead man's breast,
And much to hit him long'd:
but him the nimble horses rescued,

div.note: Homer names the sire, Amisódarus.

div.head: BOOK XVII

l.seg: NOR was it hid from Atreus' son,
That neath the feud of Troïans
He thro' the foremost ranks advanc'd,
And round the hero's body mov'd,
as round her calf a heifer,
And forward held his spear, and shield,
which equal was on all sides,
Full resolute to slay the man,
whoso might dare to front him.
Retire! withdraw thee from the dead,
For none of all the allies renown'd,
Smote down Patroclus with the spear
Lest also thee I hit, and rieve
for man to vaunt too proudly.
No thought so arrogant, I trow,
Dwelleth, or in the savage boar,
Long vantage of his years enjoy'd,
Nor deem I, he, on proper feet,
to his own home did hie him,
With pleasure to his consort dear,
So will I thy pretension crush,
in sooth! if thou withstand me.
But I in turn my counsel give,
To hide thee in retreat, (and not
— Too late the fool is prudent."
He spake, but him persuaded not;
now shalt thou pay the forfeit,
For that my kinsman thou hast slain,
And in her bower newly built
didst make his bride a widow,
And on our parents hast entail'd
To their affliction I in sooth
some balm of grief might carry,
If now I bear away thy head
and eke thy arms, and place them
Nor now much longer, I opine,
But soon the riddle must be spelt,
He spake, and thrusting struck the shield,
which equal was on all sides :
Nor might the metal force its way,
but first the point was broken,
By the bluff shield resisted. Next,
And, as the foe retreated, he,
where throat is fitted in the bosom,
And thro' the tender neck its point

div.note: For the death of Hyperenor, see 14, 516.

l.seg: So with a loud gash down he dropt,
and o'er him clang'd his armour.
His hair, that with the Graces vied,
And ample tresses, which with gold
A man may kindly rear a shoot
and it with a white flower buddeth;
And from its own pit wrencheth it,
was slain and stript of armour.
As when, reliant in his might,
Out of a grazing herd may snatch
By his huge weight and stalwart teeth
its neck at once is broken;
He rends the victim, laps the blood,
And while he havoc spreadeth wide,
So, to not one of them, the heart
Of Panthûs' son; but such a prize
Who then, in semblance of a man,
Hard are those steeds for mortal men
to tame and guide in harness,
This said, again the god was mix'd
Then, round his soul of black, with grief
One, stripping off the glorious arms,

div.note: Troian. Euphorbus was called a Dardan, 16, 806.

l.seg: Prostrate; and from the open gash
He thro' the foremost ranks advanc'd,
and like a flame of Vulcan,
"Alas for me! if now in fear
But if, for shame and honour, I
Combat by single force, to one
But, O fond heart, why holdest now
Whom God doth honour; — haply will
So, none of Danaï shall be
Shrink from the might of Hector, who
But if, how Ajax, good at need,
doth quit him in the battle,
might yet draw up the body
While all his bosom and his soul
Meanwhile the Troian ranks were come,
and at their head was Hector.
But step by step: not otherwise,
Whom from the stable dogs and men
And freeze his valiant heart; then he
But quickly fronted round, when first
he reach'd his troop of comrades.
He gaz'd on every side to see
the left of all the battle,
Cheering the comrades at his side
For bright Apollo in their hearts
He hied to run, and, quick arriving,
Must busy us, perchance to bear
All naked: for the arms are won
So spake he, and bestirr'd the soul
Along the foremost ranks he mov'd
Where Hector held the glorious arms;
Wishful with weapon sharp to cut
his head from off the shoulders
And toss the maimed trunk abroad
Then Ajax nearer came, his shield
For carriage to the Troian town,
to be his own great glory.
Stood forth, as round his proper brood
Leading abroad his young; but he,
And Atreus' son on other side,
Stood also, heaving in his breast
On Hector turn'd a frowning glance
In vain, when runaway thou art,
Who have in Ilium their birth,
For not of Lycians, I trow,
How, mid the riot, shouldest thou
He, while alive, much service did
to thee and to the city;
But thou no courage hast from him,
to scare the dogs and vultures.
Wherefore, if now by my advice
For if within the Troians now
And if we to the mighty town
we won it from the battle:
Gladly would then the Argives yield
For of such hero squire was he,
who is by far the noblest, —
He and the squires who round him
But thou, to look the foe in face
Or stand against the buckler huge
Much less, his shock wilt thou endure:
sith he than thee is stronger."
To him with frowning glance replied
"Glaucus! we know thee sage: and why
Ye Spirits! troth; my thought it was,
that thou, of all the heroes
Who say, I dare not to abide
Not at the battle shudder I,
nor at the tramp of horses :
But alway than the wit of man
the wit of Jove is higher;
But hither, friend! beside me stand,
For ever will I claim to be
Or some of Danaï will I,
Stop from his eagerness to fight
Then Hector rais'd his voice aloft
Be men, my friends! nor negligent
Which (when the hero I had slain)
From out the feud of battle. Quick
His comrades, not yet far; whom he
Who to the city bare away
There standing, he the arms exchang'd,
His own from off his body, these

div.note: He, i. e. Patroclus.
He, i. e. Achilles.

l.seg: To his dear sire; he to his son,
when old he grew, bequeath'd them:
Yet not the son, I wis, grew old,
Busking him in the heav'nly arms
Thereat he shook his head, and spake
"Ha! wretched! eke to thee is death
Who near approacheth thee: but thou
Of a surpassing chief, at whom
Of him thou hast the comrade slain,
And from his shoulders and his head
The armour: still will I to thee
In payment for this sorrow, that
From thee on thy return receive
the arms; and Ares dress'd him, —
and, thro' his limbs, within him
Pour'd strength & spirit. Thence he hied
And, as with mighty shout he came,
to all he wore the semblance
I sought not wider reach of sway,
When from your cities, one by one,
But that, to rescue from the hands
The Troian wives and infants small,
of gifts and food I harass
The Troian people, while of you
To conquer, or to perish: — such
Dragging his body to the ranks
Half of the spoils to him I yield,
And such as is my honour now,
such eke shall be his honour."
He spake; and weightily did they
To tear the carcase from the hands
A safe return for both of us
I dare to hope no longer.
Nay, nor so sorely tremble I
As for my proper head I fear,
And thine: sith Hector round about
And at our feet the steep abyss
call thou, if any hear thee."
Who at the board of Atreus' sons
As we ourselves, and with command
And by the gift of Jupiter
Of Danaï: so great a strife
But, of himself, hie hither each,
To leave Patroclus for a joy
and sport to dogs of Troas."
As thus he cried, Oïleus' son,
was first to reach his presence;
But, of the rest, what wit of man
in plump: and Hector led them.
As at the mouth, where shoals hem-in
Against the current rage and roar
The ridges of the circling beach
With such alarum went, I wis,
Pour'd mist in plenty. Troth! of
old ne'er hated he the chieftain,
Nor that Patroclus be a prize,
Endur'd he: therefore to his aid
Yet first the Troians drave away
Who, smit with panic, left the dead:
Tho' keen for slaughter, slew not one,
but dragg'd to them the carcase.
Nor long from rescue stood aloof
By Ajax; who in noble form
he hied, like to a boar in prowess, —
To drag him to their proper walls
In sooth, did tug him by the foot
a strap, in hope of favour
For, him the son of Telamon,
Close in the combat struck, across
Beneath the fury of the point
Yielded: for mighty was the spear,
and broad the hand that urg'd it.
Out of the open gash his brain
So from his hand he dropt to earth
side, fell prostrate to the carcase,
but soon his life was ended,
Too early ravish'd by the spear
with shining spear to hit him;
and stoop'd to shun its fury,
Him by the collarbone it hit,
So with a loud crash down he dropt,
and o'er him clang'd his armour.
Then Ajax thrust at Phaenops' son,
The brazen weapon piere'd his lap
So in the dust he fell, — the earth
With mighty whoops drew up the
Had then the Argives glory won;
Friendly and sage, who aged grew
Hid in such guise, the son of Jove,
So have I seen, that others, —
And eke with sorely scanty folk, —
Trusting upon their own strong hand,
So spake he: but Æneias knew
Seeing him face to face; and loud
But, sith some heav'nly god, but now
He spake, and from the van of men
Then did they gather to a ball,
Near him he took his stand, and pois'd,
And hit the son of Hippasus,
Beneath the liver, near the heart,
And he too on the Danaï
But nowhere could he reach the foe,
And bucklers fitted as a wall,
That none, withdrawing from the fight,
But, round the dead, should circle them,
And eke of Danaï: for these
Yet slain were fewer far of them:
Each to his fellow aid to bear
Thus battled they, like blazing fire:
nor sure was it to any,
That Sun or Moon was safe; for mist
the Sun's keen rays lay open.
No cloud on heaven's eye appear'd,
These, respite had from battle's force;
which each did aim at other.

div.note: Welkin, i. e. clouds. Germ. Wölken. The word is used by modern poets vaguely for the Sky, which also once meant Cloud.

l.seg: But those by darkness and by war
And chief the chieftains, wasted sore
As yet the tiding had not heard,
But still deem'd they, that he alive
Apart their battle held: for so
Urging to lead the fight aloof
But, all the day, the others bare
knees, shanks, and feet beneath them,
And hands and eyes, bespatter'd were;
while still they press'd the battle,
As when a man, for currying,
Bedrench'd with fat, the ample hide,
which once a huge bull cover'd;
They, from his hands receiving it,
do stand apart and tug it
In circle; and sith many tug,
The oil deep entereth; and it,
So they in narrow space did tug
The Achaians, to the hollow ships.
Such evil toil of men and steeds
Upon that day prolong. Nor yet
Far from the Argive galleys rag'd,
Therefore, not dead, but strong in life,
Patroclus from the gates of Troy
Sith not at all did Achileus
His friend high Ilium might storm;
For from his mother often this
But they around the hero's corpse
and man by man was slaughter'd.
And thus did one to other say
Yawn on us all! the which for us
Than to give up our hero's corpse
Unto their own abodes to drag
But thus did one to other talk
Be slaughter'd one and all, let none
Still went the stubborn tustle on;
Wept, soon as e'er that tiding bad
How that their charioteer was slain
Much by keen lashes of the scourge
Much by address of honey'd words,
and much by threats and curses.
But they the car would neither draw
Drooping toward the ground their heads;
Did warm tears trickle to the ground,
Thereafter shook his head, and spake
Was it, that ye, with wretched men,
should learn the taste of sorrow?
For, of all things that move on earth
and breathe the air of heaven,
Shall Hector son of Priam mount:
for not will I endure it.
Enough, that he the armour hath,
But I will vigour breathe to you
Grant I, to slay the foe, until
When in the west the Sun shall sink
Thus speaking, in the heav'nly steeds
Then they, from off their manes, to earth
shook down the dust, and quickly
foe, as after geese a vulture.
For easy was escape to him
Yet slaughter never might he make
At once to combat with the spear
A man, whose sire Laërkes was,
out of thy heart hath taken
Sound wit, and this ungainful thought
Alone? Thy partner of the fight
But him in turn Diores' son,
While yet he liv'd; but him in turn
But thou within thy hands receive
Then from the chariot will I
caught up the scourge and bridles.
These may I hope to capture,
If thou with me like-minded art:
Against the sally of us both
to try the shock of Ares."
Forward they went, their shoulders screen'd
Went in their company; and troth!
To slay the heroes both, and catch
Was fill'd in all his gloomy soul
But breathing even on my back:
That Hector, Priam's son, will stay
Till, either, both of us be slain,
The Argive ranks; or, e'en himself
The carcase, and the foe's repulse,
And from us two, -men yet alive,
For here with fearful weight press on,
But in the lap of destiny,
I too my dart shall fling; and Jove
will care to guide the issue."
threw the spear with lengthy shadow,
which equal was on all sides.
Nor might the hide resist it, but
thro' all the folds it hurried,
deep in the vitals pierc'd him.
And as, behind an ox's horns,
may chop, if young and sturdy,
And cleave the gristle through: the ox,
So, with a forward leap, fell he,
He, seeing it, by scanty space
In earth alighted; where its force
made the shaft's end to quiver,
Then in close combat of the sword
Had not the two Ajaces come,
Eager; and pressing thro' the crowd,
soon did they part the combat.
For, shrinking from the gather'd force
and spake a word of boasting:
To ease this loaded heart, that I
And mounted on the seat himself,
with hands, and feet beneath him,
To wake the Danaï to war:
for now his mind was turned.
Which all the labours of the field
So she, in circling purple cloud
and one by one arous'd them.
Whom at short distance she beheld;
Would ward the flying darts from me,
Gladly would I with hand and foot
His brazen ravage; sith on him
So spake he, and rejoic'd in heart
That unto her, of all the gods,
Such hardihood as hath a fly,
Tho' from the skin full oft repell'd;
Such hardihood in him she pour'd
a man both good and wealthy,
Most honour'd; for to him he was
Him, seeking to escape by flight,
Strike in the girdle: thro' and thro'
So with a crash he fell. Thereat
Drew from the Troïans the corpse
But, standing close to Hector's side,
Of strangers, chiefly lov'd, who held
In such resemblance, spake to him
He in the foremost ranks hath slain
So spake he, but a gloomy cloud
And thro' the foremost ranks he rush'd,
Thereon the son of Saturn took
And puissance to the Troians gave,
did first to flight betake him:
For while he forward alway mov'd,
a spear had struck his shoulder
had from short distance hurl'd it.
Next, Hector wounded on the wrist, and quell'd
Gazing around, he skulk'd to flee; for that
To grasp a javelin in hand
While Hector dash'd at Leïtus, — Idomeneus,
But in the shaft the long spear snapt: thereat
And while the son of Deucalus
high on his car was standing,
At him did Hector fling the dart
in turn, and barely miss'd him.
His leader, when he quitted first
On foot proceeding, to the foe
And came, unto his lord a light,
But his own life the forfeit paid
the ear and jaw: it shatter'd
His furthest teeth, and thro' mid
tongue drave on in forceful passage.
So from the chariot he dropt,
and shed on earth the bridles.
But in his own good hands full soon
Low stooping to the ground; and thus
"Ply now the scourge, till that thou reach
Upon the word, his captain lash'd
Back to the hollow ships: for, troth!
dread on his soul had fallen.
That on the Troians Jove bestowed
And know, the Troians holpen are
For from the foeman every dart
Good or bad fighter: any gait
But darts from our battalions fall
But come, and ponder we ourselves,
Both to the galleys how to draw
the corpse; and how, in safety
Who, hither looking, wounded are with grief;
May be withholden, but will rush
Now, might we some bold comrade find,
Hath not the mournful tiding heard,
that his dear friend is perish'd.
Yet nowhere may I such a one
For in the welkin are enwrapt
Make open sky, and cheery sight
And sith thy pleasure is to slay,
slay us in light of heaven."
So spake he; and the Father griev'd
and chas'd the mist of darkness,
And on them cheery shone the Sun,
Urge him in speed to wend his way
He hied upon his path, as hies
To pick the fattest of the kine
Who, all night watching, weary him,
Vain are his sallies: darts thick flung
And burning brands, which harry him,
And in the morning, sorely griev'd
From dead Patroclus. Dire his dread,
The Achaians leave him on the field,
And many a charge on Merion
and on the Ajaces laid he:
Let all remember now how kind
gracious to be to all men,
While yet alive: but now, alas!
do Death and Fate o'erhend him."
Gazing around on all sides, like
an eagle, who, of all things
That under heaven fly, they say,
to scan the earth is keenest;
Whose eye, when loftiest he hangs,
but straight at it he souseth,
On every side did wander, mid
If haply might he yet alive
the left of all the battle,
Cheering the comrades at his side
Then standing near before his face,
To thee a mournful tiding, which
Yet troth! I fancy thou thyself
Know, that the god on Danaï
And Troians hold the puissance: slain
But, speeding to the Achaian ships,
All-naked; for the arms are won
his words, and drain'd the juices
Of his young throat: with bitter tears
But hied to run, and gave his arms
Near to his side: but him in tears
his feet bare out of battle,
Nor was thy spirit willing then,
Thy succour to the fainting bands
To them, for succour, left he none
Where, speeding to the Ajaces' side,
"Him have I forward sent, to reach
And to Achilles fleet of foot
Sally he will not yet, howe'er
For nowise may he, bare of arms,
We for ourselves must ponder well,
Both, to the galleys how to draw
the corpse, — and how in safety
Ourselves to baffle Death and Doom,
Lift up and from the broil bear off
We, of a single name, who bear
So spake he: they obedient, in
the dead uplifted by the Achaians.
Then on they dash'd ahead; as hounds,
which on a boar that's wounded
But when, reliant on his might,
he turns to bay and standeth,
But often as the Ajaces twain
So often chang'd their skin its hue,
Dare further to rush on, and try
So carried then the eager pair
the corpse from out the battle
Flam'd ever broader; like to fire,
In its vast blaze the houses
So upon them, as on they march'd,
But they, as when a team of mules,
Whether a beam or mighty mast
Too much, their heart is worn away
at once by sweat and labour:
So, earnest, bare the twain away
The two Ajaces check'd the rush,
The water check, if, wooded well,
Balking their current of its will,
Fit channel; nor may all their force
and all their flood o'erthrow it:
So alway did the Ajaces twain
Behind them. These did yet press on;
Meanwhile the Achaians, as in plump
a cloud of daws or starlings
The hawk, who with the little birds
So then the Achaian youth, pursued
And many beauteous arms were lost
within the moat or round it,
yet was no pause of battle.

div.head: BOOK XVIII

l.seg: So then in guise of blazing fire
And found him forward, out beyond
Foreboding in his heart the thing,
Mayhap the immortals to my heart
As once my mother spake of yore,
and spelt the doom of heaven,
How that, while I in life abide,
Is perish'd. Troth! my charge it was,
Then to the galleys back to come,
While all his bosom and his soul
"Woe on my errand! thou, oh son
which I would had no fulfilment!
We combat; but his arms are won
Upon such words, a gloomy cloud
Then scooping in his joined hands
Down on his head he poured them,
Then lay he huge, of huge aspect,
And, with his own hands rending it,
Led off as booty of the spear,
And hurried from the tent, around
Lest, impotent of grief, his throat
Who sat within the briny depths
Thereat she cried aloud with grief,
With these the brilliant grot was fill'd,
and all did beat the bosom
Ceaseless: but Thetis spake to them,
and led the dirge of sorrow:
so, when ye all have heard me,
Full truly may ye know, what griefs
Ah woe upon my bridal bed!
Who to a mortal father gave
A chief of warriours: and he
grew tall like to a sapling.
And him, as in the orchard's lap
And sent him forth to Ilium
yet in the halls of Peleus
Ne'er shall my child returning meet
And while he liveth still for me
and sees the light of heaven,
Yet go will I, my offspring dear
What sorrow hath his bosom reach'd,
In tears attended: round their forms
But when to loamy Troas' beach
they came, in train they mounted,
Where thick the ships of Myrmidons
Then by his side, while deep he moan'd,
Who on her son's head cast her hand,
"My child, why weepest thou? and what
the grief, thy heart that wounded?
Lock not thy breast, but speak it out.
For he to galley-poop hath hemm'd
Where they, abandon'd of thy aid,
To her Achilles, fleet of foot,
Yet bringeth it no pleasure, sith
Patroclus, whom of all my friends
As my own peer. Him have I lost:
My splendid armour stript from him,
Right wondrous; which, as brilliant gifts,
Upon that day, when thee they gave
Oh hadst thou liefer neath the waves
There alway dwelt, and mortal wife
But now, that eke thyself may know
Thy child will shortly fall in death,
Bid me to live, or among men
shall first of life be rieved,-
dear child! as now thou talkest;
Sith eke for thee an instant doom
To her Achilles, fleet of foot,
"O might I instant die, sith as
to save my friend from slaughter
Was not to me assign'd: but he
But now, sith never wend I home
Nor brought a ray of light to him,
By godlike Hector slain in heaps;
I sit, a useless load on earth;
O might from mortals and from gods
And Rancour, which to rage doth goad
And eke, far sweeter than the comb
Fuming within the breast; as me
But leave we these things, past and gone,
And the fond fancies of our heart
Now must I sally, to o'ertake
Hector; but then shall I to Doom
Such is the will of Jupiter
For not the might of Héracles
In guise no other, (if on me
Fall shall I also. Now must I
So some of Troian dames thro' me,
May wipe from tender cheeks the tear
And learn that I did heretofore
Do thou, tho' loving, stay me not:
"All this is truly right, my child!
nor aught amiss I find it,
Long shall he vaunt it; sith alsó
But thou, refrain thyself, nor yet
for at the rise of morrow
This saying, from her bonny child
And to her sisters of the sea
The aged lord of waves to see
To him rehearse ye every thing;
It list him, to my son to give
plung'd beneath the wave of ocean.
But Thetis of the silver foot
Her to Olympus did her feet
Unto their galleys stretch'd the flight,
and to the sea of Helle.
then from the darts have rescued
For after him in new pursuit
like to a flame in fury.
Thrice by the gristle of the heel
Eager to drag him back; and loud
Thrice from the corpse the Ajaces twain,
Dash'd him away: but ever he,
At one time into riot thick
Stood firm with mighty shouts; but back
he not an inch withdrew him.
As shepherds, watching in the field,
from some slain beast avail not
From the slain hero to repel
Then surely had he dragg'd him off
But Iris, swift with feet of wind,
Secret from Jove and other gods,)
So, close in front of him she stood,
These to hold fast his dead remains,
To sever from the tender neck
the head, and high impale it.
But up! and lie no longer here.
Let shame and honour touch thee.
If aught of insult him befall,
to thee it were an outrage."
to me on errand sent thee?"
Knows it, of all who dwell around
Then her Achilles, fleet of foot,
"How may I hie me to the fray?
Nor doth my mother dear permit
for at the rise of morrow
From princely Vulcan hath she gag'd
Nor know I other man, of whom
the noble arms would suit me,
Unless I haply take the shield
But eke himself (I trow) with it
Dire ravage dealing with the spear
Then Iris swift with feet of wind
"Well also know we, that the foe
But, as thou art, hie to the moat,
and show thee to the armies,
Stand off from battle, and hereby
Gain respite of their toil:-and short
So utter'd Iris fleet of foot
Of golden cloud she set, and thence
As from an island riseth smoke,
Which from the city stretcheth far,
If for their homes the livelong day
But kindle at the setting sun
Then shooteth high aloft the blaze,
In hope, they will with galleys come,
So from Achilles' head the blaze
He on the margin of the moat
stood forth; nor with the Achaians
There standing, shouted he: the voice
So from the throat of Achileus
Were all in heart confounded: yea,
Dart radiant from off the head
Thrice from the margin of the moat
And on the spot twelve hero-chiefs
With their own chariots and spears.
and place him on a pallet.
Beheld upon the bier laid out,
Whom verily to war he sent
To plunge, unwilling, in his course
So he from mortals hid his light:
And, ere for supper heed they took,
All stood upright in mote, nor dar'd
One night gave him and Hector birth;
But one did in discourse excel,
in fight by far the other.
Who thus with kindly soul harangu'd,
and spake his word among them:
Unto the city now to haste,
Beside the galleys on the plain.
Far from our ramparts are we.
Now while this hero wrathful was
So long far easier to us
For truly joyful were the nights,
which by their fleet we tarried,
To me; while hope I had, to hend
But now from Peleus' mighty son
Not on the plain his haughty soul
will list to wait, where Ares
But he our city and our wives
will make his prize of battle.
But to the city hie we now :
Thus will it be:— ambrosial night
Pelides fleet of foot; but if,
He sally and o'erhend us here,
too well we all shall learn him.
Whoso may scape:— far from my
ear be such events! but many
Of Troians in the dust shall lie,
a joy to dogs and vultures.
But if my words compliance find,
Our elders nightly strength shall give
And lofty portals, and on these
But with the morning's early dawn
will make his prize of battle.
Stand we along the towers: then,
It list him for our wall to fight,
Back to the galleys wend will he,
Troth! will his spirit him forbid
Nor shall he storm the wall; but first
the sprightly dogs shall eat him."
To him with frowning glance replied
Who biddest us again to go
and in the city coop us.
Had ye not long ago your fill,
For, troth! a tale it was of yore
What stores of copper and of gold
But those fair treasures now, ye see,
great Jove with us was angry.
But, now as unto me the son
Glory hath granted, fast to hem
For none of Troians will obey;
nor troth! would I allow it.
But come! as I the word shall speak,
Along the host, by rank and rank,
take each man now his supper,
And hold remembrance of the watch,
And if a Troian for his gear
Then, lest the Achaians swallow it,
And yield his riches to the folk
But we, with morning's early dawn
(An so it list him,) by the ships,
for him will it be worser.
Not I will flee him: stand will I
bear off the meed of prowess.
So Hector counsell'd: to his word
For they to Hector gave assent,
Thereat, along the host, took they
To them did Peleus' son lead off
With thick-drawn moanings; as may moan
Whose cubs a hunter of the deer
Thro' many a glen then courseth he,
And overhend him: ay, for sharp
I on that day did utter,
And said, I safely back should bring
For here, in Troas both of us
Are doom'd; for never in his halls
Nor Thetis, my return shall greet;
but here the earth shall hold me.
Ere that thy funeral I make,
The arms and head of Priam's son,
And eke before thy pile will I,
Sever twelve heads of living men,
Meanwhile thou here shalt lie,-just so,-
And round thy body Troian dames
Shall night and day bewail; whom we
An ample tripod on the fire
Might wash the gory clots away
So on the wasting fire they set
Water for bathings in they pour'd
Around the bottom play'd the fire,
But when within the shining brass
Then wash'd they all the clots away,
and with rich oil did oint him,
And fill'd with unguent nine years old

div.note: Nine years old. This is interpreted, "brought with him from home nine years ago."

l.seg: Then on a pallet stretching him,
From head to foot, and over all
Thus round Achilles fleet of foot
The livelong night with tear and groan
But Jove to Juno made address,
For lo! Achilles fleet of foot
From thy own body erst were born
"O son of Saturn, grim and dire,
Man verily, I trow, for man,
is wont his will to complish.
Nor kenneth counsel like to me,
How then should I, who claim to be
for that I hold the title
Thy wedded queen to be; and thou
Not against Troians in my wrath
Brazen and starry, mid the abodes
Free from decay; which for himself
Him sweating at his work she found,
Earnest: for he just then would make
To stand in order round the wall
That mid the company of gods
And to their mansion hie them back;
So far completeness had they found;
but ears not yet were added,
While he, with soul of artifice,
The goddess of the silver foot,
Then closely did she press her hand,
thou dost not much frequent us.
But follow further; then will I
She seated her, where for the feet
And call'd to Vulcan, fam'd in art,
some need of thee hath Thetis."
Who rescued me, when far I fell,
did wreak her will against me,
Beside them, I for nine years forg'd
But there, the stream of Ocean
Neither of gods nor mortal men,
these twain, who sav'd me, knew it.
She to our mansion now is come:
To pay the salvage of my life
Do thou fair gifts before her place,
While I my bellows put away
and all my tools of workshop."
He spake, and from the stithy rose,
Limping and huge: yet under him
The bellows from the fire apart
he plac'd; and all the weapons,
Tools of his art, he gather'd up
Then with a sponge his face around
and both his hands he wiped
And sturdy neck and shaggy chest,
Took in his hand a sceptre stout,
Lame as he was, abroad. Beside
and round their lord, mov'd nimbly
Pages in fine-wrought gold, in form
Which have within their heart a mind,
And strength; and canny service know
These did their tasks fulfil, and watch'd
Sat him upon a shining chair,
near to the seat of Thetis.
Then closely did he press her hand,
thou dost not much frequent us.
Speak forth thy meaning: me my heart
If complish it I can, and if
Knowest thou one, who in her heart
As Jove the son of Saturn hath
Me from my sisters of the sea
Sorely against my will, in sooth.
and lo! new sorrows whelm me.
For-that, whereas he gave to me
to bear and rear an offspring,-
A chief of warriours; and he
grew tall, like to a sapling;
And him, as in the orchard's lap
And sent him forth to Ilium,
yet in the halls of Peleus
Ne'er shall my child returning meet
And while he liveth still for me
and sees the light of heaven,
Her from his arms with contumely
Grieving for her, his spirit pin'd
Were by the Troians to their poops
hemm'd up, nor dar'd to sally
Abroad beyond them. Him the chiefs
Besought, and many gifts to him
thereon refus'd he at the first;
Yet, after, in his proper arms
and unto battle sent him forth
Then they around the Skæan gates
And in the selfsame day would storm
Slew him amid the foremost ranks
Therefore before thy knees I come,
And corslet; sith, the arms he had,
his comrade dear hath lost them,
a prize to Troy: so on the ground
"Cheer thee; nor let such things as these
For, oh that I so easily,
As now shall beauteous arms to him
Shall, one and all, count marvellous,
This said, he left her on the spot,
and to the bellows hied him:
Against the fire he pointed them,
and bade them to be active.
In twenty censers coals were laid,
As might to Vulcan pleasant be,
Into the fire stiff brass and tin,
He cast but on the stithy next
In one hand grasp'd a hammer stout,
First did he take in hand to frame
five folds; but on the surface
He fram'd with soul of artifice
On it the Earth and on it Sea
he plac'd, and on it Heaven,
The Sun unweary, Moon at full;
and on it all the marvels,
Wherewith the sky is crown'd; and
Bear, which Wain is call'd by others;
Which turneth alway on his field,
On it did he two cities place
Splendid. In one of them were held
And thro' the city brides were led,
Dancers were reeling: flutes and harps
And from the threshold every wife
stept out to gaze and wonder.
The people in the public square
One vow'd, he had the whole
-no gear had yet been paid him:
So both an umpire from the folk
would get, to end the quarrel.
To each in turn acclaim was made,
But heralds did the people check;
And in their hands the sceptres held
Wherewith in turn each sallied forth,
when he for judge was chosen.
Full in the midst, -award of law,-
To yield to him, whose argument
The one would ravage and destroy,
The gear, whatever might be coop'd
would make, but arm'd for ambush.
Should man the rampart for defence;
In golden raiment; fair and tall,
And mark'd asunder to the eye:
the folk were small beside them.
But when they at the place arriv'd,
Beside a river, where a ford
Near it began they low to crouch,
from these, two scouts were sitting,
Watching to tell, if flocks be near,
Soon did the cattle forward come
The men in ambush notice gat,
Upspringing, forth they hied in chase,
and reach'd the ground of combat,
Amid them Riot, mid them Strife,
Dragg'd deadly Fate one man alive
Another dead man by the feet
she tugg'd from out the turmoil,
And on her shoulders wore a cloak
Such was their dealing, such their war,
And they on either side drew up
On it he plac'd a soft new field,
Where many ploughmen teams did drive
When to the limit of the field
they reach'd, and turn'd the cattle,
Then did a man, with cup in hand
Give them to drink: those in the rows
turn'd round to look, and hanker'd
To reach the limit: black it was,
And seem'd as newly plough'd: such work
On it he plac'd a cornfield deep,
Reaping, and wielded each in hand
Others, the binders of the sheaves
Three were the binders of the sheaves,
With heart of effort, dealt supply:
The king in silence near the row
And o'er a mighty bull, new slain,
White flour in plenty o'er it shed,
On it an orchard next he plac'd,
Laden with luscious crop of grapes:
dark were the clusters on it.
was propt on poles of silver.
On either side, a dark blue ditch;
Of tin: a single narrow path
led thro' the field to reach it,
By which the pickers came and went,
when they would crop the vineyard.
And tender maids, and striplings slim,
And in the midst of them a boy
The others to the tune beat time,
and humm'd and skirl'd and bounded.
On it he further plac'd a herd
Of gold and tin the kine were made:
With lowing came they from their yard,
Four golden herdsmen with the kine
were rang'd: dogs with them follow'd,
Nine, spry of foot: but in the front
two lions grim were mauling them.
A bull, that bellow'd loud, to whom
the hounds and youths would speed
The twain had rent the bull's vast side,
And vainly came the swains in chase,
These from the lions kept aloof,
nor dar'd to bite; but round them
Stood at safe distance, barking fierce,
In a fair copse, of snowy sheep,
with pens and huts and stables.
Next he achiev'd a reel complex,
Maidens, who dower earn of kine,
Therein were dancing, each the hand
the youths were clad in tunics
Of tissue featly spun, with oil
Each maiden bare upon her head
Each youth a golden cutlass wore
And these with canny feet one while
As when a potter tries his lathe,
At other while they both in ranks,
Stood gazing with delight: from them
came forth a pair of tumblers,
Last, in it, he the mighty strength
When thus he had achiev'd to make
The corslet after it he wrought,
than blaze of fire more shining;
And eke the solid weighty casque,
All radiant and full of art,
he brought and laid his present.
Bearing from Vulcan to her son

div.head: BOOK XIX

l.seg: rose from the streams of Ocean,
When Thetis to the galleys came,
And found her own beloved son
Wailing aloud; and at his side
Were weeping. Then amidst them stood
And closely did she press his hand,
"My child! him let us now endure,
So as he is, to lie; sith he
by will of God is fallen.
But thou, out of my hands receive
After such word, before her son
nor dar'd to eye the goddess.
But Achileus, the more he look'd,
the more did rage possess him,
And, neath his eyelids, direful shot,
like flame, his glance of eyen:
Yet joy'd he in his hands to hold
his eyes with joy had sated,
"My mother! arms, the god hath sent,
Gird me for fight forthwith will I,
in sooth: yet direly dread I,
Breed worms within my valiant friend,
(For all the life is gone from it:)
so would his flesh be rotted."
Then Thetis of the silver foot
"My child! let not such things as this
The flies, these savage tribes, that eat
My care it be, aloof to keep.
Here lie, his flesh shall alway be
But unto public mote do thou
And there to Atreus' royal son
Then instant for emprize of war
to keep his flesh in firmness.
Yea, those who formerly would stay
Who pilots only were, and kept
Or held the public bread in store, —
Now even these to mote came forth,
Leaning upon the spear; for still
These mid the foremost of the mote
did hie, and soon were seated.
He too disabled by a wound;
Thus did Achilles, fleet of foot,
This present work to do; when we,
Did for a damsel's sake flame out
Would that the shaft of Artemis
had slain her in the galleys,
Upon the selfsame day, when I
To Hector and the Troians that
Will long remembrance hold, I deem,
But leave we these things, past and gone,
And the fond fancies of the heart
Here then an end I make of wrath:
to boil; but come! and quickly
So I, with Troians once again
Whether beside the ships to sleep
That of their army more than one
his limbs will rest full gladly,
If safely from our spear he scape
So spake he, and with gladness
Address'd them, seated as he was,
For in the hurly of a crowd
tongues and ready hearts are crippled.
Now I to Peleus' son reply
Ye Argives! ponder well my word,
and each of you discern it.
Oft time the Achaians spake to me
Who am not guilty. Jove, and Fate,
Upon the selfsame day, when I
What else to do avail'd I? God
But trampeth heads of men, with
bale; and one or other tangleth.
Chiefest they call; yet him with guile
What time Alcmena's hour was ripe
For birth of Héracles: then he
That I the counsel may declare
A hero from my breed and blood
shall show to light of heaven
This day, for lordship far and wide
'Thou wilt the liar play, nor crown
Swear thou, that he shall lordship hold
Who, from thy breed and blood, this day
But sware the dreadful oath: therefrom
But Juno, hasting on her path,
knew) in hope of tender offspring.
Her did the goddess forward bring,
a word, O Jove! I bring thee.
Now is a noble hero born,
will o'er the Argives lord it.'
So spake she; but his soul profound
And raging sware a hardy oath,
And starry heaven back should wend she,
Whirling her with his hand; and quick
she reach'd the works of mortals.
And her he alway curs'd, whene'er
he saw his own dear offspring
Nor yet the more was I, what
Dire ravage of the Argives made
who once had wrought me mischief.
But tho' enfrenzied I have been,
and Jove my wits hath emptied,
Again to win thee fain am I,
But up! for enterprize of war,
And pledg'd I hold myself to pay,
Or come! an so will please thee, stay,
And hither shall forthwith my squires,
Bring thee the gifts; so mayest thou
But him Achilles, fleet of foot,
The gifts (an so thou wilt,) to give,
as seemly is, or keep them,—

div.note: The goddesses are here plural, as in 11, 270.

l.seg: With thee it resteth. Instant now
Nor toy, nor shuffle: still is left
As each may Peleus' son behold
Wide wasting with the brazen spear
So eke let every man give heed
For combat with the Troians; sith
Shall greet us, when for bargain once
But bid the Achaians food to taste,
Of pleasant bread and wine: for this
— For, man may nowise all the day
For tho' his spirit eager be
Yet heaviness by stealth his limbs
o'erhend him, and his knees beneath
But whoso with the foe may fight,
Even the livelong day, his soul
Nor faint his limbs, ere every man
But come! disperse the people; bid,
that they to dinner gird them;
shall bring his gifts before us:
So with their eyes the Achaians all
shall see, and thou be gladden'd.
And let him swear the mighty oath,
That never did he touch her bed
As is the ordinance, O prince!
Thereafter shall the prince of men
By a rich banquet, leaving naught
Thou, son of Atreus! wilt alsó
In other men's esteem: for none
can blame it, that a monarch,
Who did in anger take the lead,
to hear the word thou sayest;
For duly hast thou every thing
The oath thou namest, this I wish
Nor shall I perjur'd be: my soul
Must here that little while await,
And stay, assembled, ye the rest,
May from my tent arrive, and I
by faithful oaths may bind me.
But to thyself this deed in charge
the gifts from out my galley,
and lead with them the women.
And, for the Sun and Jove, amid
But him Achilles, fleet of foot,
Shall after flag, when in my breast
the fire may burn less fiercely.
But now our comrades prostrate lie
when Jove to glory rais'd him.
But ye to feeding urge us. Troth!
to war, and then with sunset
To meet in solemn supper, when
But neither food nor drink to me
Adown my tender throat to pass,
now as my friend is perish'd,
Who prostrate lieth in my tent,
Turn'd to the threshold. Round him weep
Not food, but carnage now and gore
Thou with the spear (I wis) than me
might I in turn surpass thee
By far; sith I am elderborn,
bear the pressure of my counsel.
too small, but straw in plenty,
When Jove, high arbiter of war,
But nowise may the Achaians mourn
the dead man with their belly;
Of sorrow be? Whoso is slain,
Steeling our hearts, but weeping still;
From hateful warfare, him the care
So may we more incessant fight
Be at the Argive galleys left.
So spake he, and beside him took
Who hied to speed them to the tent
There, scarcely spoken was the word,
and lo! the deed was ended.
They from the tent the tripods brought,
The coursers twelve for racing fam'd,
Then women quickly led they forth,
Seven; but after them the eighth, —
Then by the people's shepherd stood
Whose voice with voice of god might vie,
and brought the boar before them.
But Atreus' son with ready hand
Which by his sword's great scabbard hung,
Clipt the first bristles of the boar,
And prayed. All the Argives sat
So he invoking spake his pray'r,
"Of this let Jove be witness first,
of gods the Best and Highest,
And Earth, and Sun, and Furies all,
who in the world beneath us
That never did I lay my hand
Neither with purpose of the couch,
But in my tents she aye abode
If aught hereof be falsely sworn,
then may the gods bestray me
With whatsoe'er of woe they send,
He spake, and thro' the victim's
throat deep drove the heartless weapon.
The corpse, as fodder to the fish,
Never in sooth would Atreus' son
The spirit in my bosom, nor
Was minded, bale and death to send
But now to dinner hasten ye;
Such words he utter'd, and at once
And hied to set them on the ship
Meanwhile, some in the tents they plac'd,
The racers, these his stately squires
right shrill, and rent with gashes
Her bosom and her tender neck
when from the tent they led me:
But now, returning, here I find,
Thy corpse unburied. How doth aye
The man, into whose arms my sire
Before our city him I saw
My tender kinsmen, all of them
And when swift Achileus had slain
The town of godlike Mynes, thou
Trusting to make me wife of youth
To Phthia, and with all the folk
Then measureless thy death I wail,
O thou who aye wast gentle!"
So spake she weeping; after her
but he with moans refus'd them:
With food and drinking bid me not
Grim sorrows pierce me: any gait
I stay and dure till sunset."
But still the two Atridæ stay'd, —
Beguiled be, ere that he face
But he incessant heav'd, and spake,
how quick and earnest wast thou
A dainty dinner in my tent
Now, thus thou liest mangled; but,
In emptiness of thee, my heart
Naught can I suffer worse; not if
Who, as I ween, in Phthia now
Widow'd of such a son; (who here
Or, if I heard the death of him,
For formerly my heart this hope
That only I should perish, far
Here on the spot, in Troy; but thou
lead on the sharp dusky galley
My child unto his father's home,
and shouldest all things show him,
My servants and my wide estate
For, as to Peleus, he, I bode,
Or, with a little life mayhap,
Aye waiting for my mournful tale,
Wailing he spake, and to his moans
Each one remembering all, that he
Lo! there he sitteth, fixt before
For care of dinner: he alone
But come! lest famine him distress,
He by such charge Athene spurr'd,
Who plung'd in semblance of a bird,
Forthwith were arming in the host.
Of her much-mighty sire: but they
far from the ships were pouring.
Thick as from Jupiter may fly
Chill'd by the gust of Boreas,
So from the galleys thickly pour'd
The blaze to heaven reach'd; the earth
from feet of men was rising
Dire rumble; and in midst of them
His teeth did gnash; his eyen shone,
Against the Troians, donn'd the gifts,
First on his shins the dapper greaves,
He fasten'd; then, to guard his chest,
About his shoulders next he slung
Brazen; but after it he took
As when to sailors out at sea
Far from their friends, despite their will,

div.note: Much-mighty. An Englishman expects Almighty; but Jupiter was not almighty in Homer's conception.
Compare 15, 530, on the corslet.

l.seg: The flare to heaven shot: and he,
From the thick horsetail shone the
casque; for, hairs of gold around it
would prove him in the armour,
Whether his glossy limbs had ease
But it, as wings, did lift from earth
Then from the flutings in his tent
An ash of Pelion the shaft,
For his dear father Cheiron cut,
to be the death of heroes.
Already round their breasts was plac'd
Thro' their compliant jaws they pass'd,
and strung the reins behind them
In other guise now ponder ye
Back to the troop of Danaï,
when we have done with battle:
Nor leave him dead upon the field,
And droop'd his auburn head aside
His full mane falling by the yoke
now with voice of man endowed:)
will save, and more than save thee,
Not ours the guilt; but mighty God
Not by the slowness of our feet
The Troians did thy armour strip
But the exalted god, for whom
Slew him amid the foremost ranks,
Now we, in scudding, pace would keep
Which speediest they say to be:
By hand of hero and of god
So much he said: thereon, his voice
To him Achilles fleet of foot,
from thee this was not needed.
Apart from mother and from father dear,
Pause will I make of war, until
He spake, and yelling, held afront

div.head: BOOK XX

l.seg: Thus at thy side, O Peleus' son,
Along a swelling of the plain,
But Jove commanded, from the head
That Themis should to council call
From all sides bade them in the halls
None of the Rivers absent were,
None of the Nymphs, who make their haunts
But all, attentive to the word,
So in the courts of Jupiter
Disdain the goddess' word, but came
from the deep brine to join them.
He in the middle took his seat,
and ask'd what Jove was planning:
The gods dost summon? doubtest aught
For in sore nearness now to them
Now in a dell myself will here
Forth gazing as may please my soul,
Go ye, until ye reach the field
For if with none but human foes
Alway, I trow, of former days
But now, when for his comrade's
loss his soul is grimly wrathful,
I dread, lest, in despite of Fate,
he storm and spoil the fortress."
And into war forth went the gods,
Gainful, and with them Vulcan too,
Lame tho' he was; yet under him
But to the aid of Troïans
And the Bright prince of locks unshorn,
Now ere that with the troop of men
So long the Achaians vaunted high
Smitten with trembling, when they saw
But when amid the crowd of men
But Ares on the other side,
Thus blessed gods, on either side
Mingled the fight, and caus'd the burst
Aloft, the Sire of men and gods
Earth and the steep heads of mountains.
Then all a-quaking were the feet
And the hill-tops, and Priam's walls,
And leapt in terror from his throne,
And to the eyes of gods and men
So dire the hurly of the shock,
when gods were match'd in quarrel.
They fronted one to one; against
But, Vulcan to resist, stood forth
Thus, for the battle, god to god
In throng of heroes chiefly long'd
To close in combat: his the life,
To glut with gore the warriour
But by his voice he seem'd to be
Apollo, son of Jove, I say,
The threats, which o'er the winecup oft
the shock of war to venture?"
In adverse struggle me to clash
not now for erst I find me:

div.note: See Note on 1, 385.

l.seg: For, coming on our kine of yore,
With the same spear, which Pedasus
But Jove for rescue granted me
Else on that day I verily
And by Athene; who in front
held forth a light before him,
Bidding with brazen spear to slay
Wherefore no mortal man, I trow,
For alway at his side some god
Yea, and without the god his dart
Ere through the skin of man it pass:
but if the god would yield us
Me would he conquer then; not if
To him the prince, the son of Jove,
"Come, hero! eke do thou then pray
Ay! for in truth to thee, they say,
But right against this hero bear
Thus saying, vigour great he breath'd
Who thro' the foremost ranks advanc'd,
When he amid the troop of men
Then she together call'd the gods,
and spake a word before them:
To ponder in your bosom, how
On us it resteth, either back
this god to turn from combat,
nor let him fail in spirit,
But mighty prowess grant to him:
to teach him that the noblest
But he in after day, I wis,
Upon his thread of birth the Fate
span, when his mother bare him.
But if Achilles shall not hear
Then, when some god in ranks of
war with adverse force may front him,
are gods in sight and presence."
too far: nor is it needful.
For me; troth, never could I wish
To mingle us the other gods;
But from the path departing now
and men shall care for battle.
And if the combat be begun
Or should they hold Achilles back,
or scare him from the struggle,
Even by us: but they, I ween,
Will to Olympus hie them back,
straight unto the rampart led them
And maid Athene rais'd for him,
When the sea-monster from the shore
unto the plain would chase him.
Thereon did Neptune sit him down
plain was fill'd with men and horses,
And by the general rush of feet
But in the midst two champions
in front of both the armies,
Held the fierce buckler, and with threats
Whom all the country long to slay
He first despiseth them; but when
Some dart may pierce him, then his heart
Valiant; then gathers he his limbs,
Over his tusks; with mighty tail
His flank and buttock, spurring on
With grey eye glaring on his mark
When they to shorter distance came,
Standest? art eager me to meet
By Priam's favour, lord to be
Yet, if thou strip me, not therefore
For he hath sons, and sound of mind
Shall then the Troïans in sooth
An if thou slay me? That emprize
is hard, I deem, to compass.
Or haply dost forget the day,
when down the fells of Ida
Thee, left alone amid the kine,
Nor didst thou look behind in flight,
Diddest thy knees repose: but I,
by aid of Jove the Father
And of Athene, turn'd on it
Thence led I many a dame away,
reft of her day of freedom,
Spoil of my spear; but thee did Jove
But not again, I reckon, they,
as in thy mind thou castest,
Will save thee. I my counsel give,
To hide thee in retreat, (and not
— Too late the fool is prudent."
To him in words responsive spake
as silly child to fright me.

div.note: To omit 203-255 would be an improvement to modern taste: yet Homer wanted to introduce the pedigree of Eneas, which excuses 203-245. The last passage, 246-255, seems to have no excuse.

l.seg: To each the other's breed is known,
Hearing the tales of old renown,
But, face to face, nor thou nor I
Thou, as the rumour telleth, art
And a seanymph thy mother was,
I likewise from a noble sire
Vaunt to be sprung: my mother is
Of these shall one or other weep
By Ares torn away; for not,
But if it please thee this to learn,
then straightway will I tell thee
Who o'er Dardania built: — not yet
But still along the slopes they dwelt
And he of mortals richest was:
Were pastur'd on a marsh, all mares,
Eke Boreas, who saw them graze,
thereby twelve foals they bare him.
This progeny, when bound it would
Scudded across the stalks of corn
at top, and did not break them:
when it in turn would gallop,
It scudded in the hoary brine
Fairest of mortals: him the gods
To bear the cup for Jupiter
Such is the parentage, and such
the blood in which I glory.
As listeth him; for he mid all
But come, no longer let us stand
Both may, to surfeit, jibes retort:
and rich the tongue of mortals,
rife crops of tales and stories.
Words, as it list thee, utter thou;
But wherefore should we twain with strife
Full in the street advancing, rail
But not by railing mayest thou
Ere that, confronting thee, I try
hard brass: but come, and quickly
Each shall with pointed weapon taste
Thus speaking, in the mighty shield
Far from his body; deeming sure
Would hurtle thro' its folds with
ease the spear with lengthy shadow;
That hard it is for mortal men
by dint of will to vanquish
So neither then the weighty spear
Might pierce the buckler; for, the
gold, gift of the god, repell'd it.
Yet thro' two brazen outer plates
For in the central plate of gold
And yet two plates of tin remain'd,
which equal was on all sides,
And thinnest lay the hide of ox:
with clank and crash of leather.
Æneias crouch'd, and far from him
Over his back the spear to earth
but he, so near to danger,
Shudder'd, and o'er his eyen swam
But Peleus' son his sharp sword drew,
which not two men might carry,
but he alone with ease did swing it.
Then had Æneias with the stone
On helmet or on buckler, which
And with the sword in closing fight
But that the shaker of the Earth,
And instant to the immortal gods
Who soon by Peleus' son subdued
would seek the house of Pluto,
Silly: for nowise will the god
But wherefore should a guiltless man
Vainly, for woes of other men,
tho' to the gods, who habit
But come ye! from the approaching death
For eke will Saturn's child be wroth
Lest that the breed of Dardanus
lov'd most of all the children,
Who of his body gotten were,
But Saturn's child hath long ago
or leave him to be vanquish'd,
Noble and pious tho' he be,
But unto all the immortal gods,
We twain, — in many oaths have sworn,
Will we the evil day avert;
not when all Troy shall smoulder
In wasting flames, which (soon or late)
He sped to hie him thro' the fight
and thro' the mell of weapons,
And came, where with Æneias stood
Over the eyes of Peleus' son
before his feet he cast it,
And rais'd Æneias from the earth,
Then many ranks of men and steeds,
Were arming them: then to his side
Who both is mightier than thee,
But alway henceforth draw thee back,
Lest to the house of Aïdes,
despite of Fate, he send thee.
But when by death and destiny
Nor ever dread to be a spoil
He spake, and left him on the spot,
He saw; and sore indignant spake
Here lieth on the ground my spear,
nor do I find the foeman,
At whom I hurl'd it, hankering
will he to try my prowess
Have courage; who hath even now
But come, and let me stir to fight
seek some one to confront me."
He spake, and leapt among the ranks,
But come, let man with man confront,
To deal with such a troop of men
Not Ares, tho' a deathless god,
and feet and strength, may compass,
No want of enterprize, I say,
shall stay me, e'en a moment:
Nay, but across their rank will I
who near my spear may venture."
So spake he urging them: meanwhile,
With loud upbraidment call'd, and vow'd
to hear the son of Peleus.
I too in battle of the tongue
But, with the spear, that harder were;
If one he complish, yet will he
And tho' to fire his hands be like,
Tho' unto fire his hands were like,
So spake he urging them, and they
But mix'd in tumult of the war
and in the crowd await him,
Lest with the sword or flying dart
So spake he: Hector then again
In tremor, when the heav'nly voice
Him, as he eager onward rush'd,
Struck, full in middle of the head;
So with a crash he dropt. Thereat
Here dying, here thou long shalt lie:
So spake the victor: but the slain
His corpse, the Achaian horses tare
With spear upon the temple, thro'
Nor might the helmet's brass resist
the ashen weight; but thro' it
Mid bone and brain the weapon splash'd,
And sped in front of him to flee,
with the same spear was wounded.
He gasp'd and bellow'd, as the bull,
So him, as there he bellowed,
Then went Achilles with the spear
The godlike son of Priam: him
For-that in tale of years he was
of all his sons the youngest,
And dearest to the father was,
and none could match his fleetness.
Then in his folly, to display
how swift his feet could bear him,
He thro' the foremost rag'd, until
Him with the dart divine Achilles
Full in the back, as past he rush'd;
Where golden buckles join'd, and where
Unto the other side the point
So, groaning, on his knees he dropt;
Gather'd: and bent aside in death,
But Hector, when he thus espied
Clutching his bowels in his hand,
and on the earth cast sideways,
Thick mist around his eyes was shed,
nor did his heart allow him
Longer aloof to stay; but he
Like to a flame; and brandish'd fierce
Saw him, and instant sprang on high,
and spake a word of vaunting:
"Behold the man at hand, who most
but now, I ween, no longer
Will each from other skulk away
He spake; then this with scowling glance
But, naught dismay'd, to him replied
as silly child to fright me.
I know, that doughty is thy force,
and that my own is weaker;
But in the lap of destiny,
Whether, tho' weaker far than thee,
I yet may haply slay thee,
some men have found it pointed."
He spake, and poising, forward threw
Blowing with gentle breath, turn'd off
And back before the feet again
Thrice rush'd Achilles with the spear,
But when the fourth time on he sped,
In vain, he then with menace dire
Came near to thee: but now anew
To whom, I ween, in din of darts
But when, next time, on thee I light,
If eke to me among the gods
I hend, shall be my onset."
Thus speaking, with the dart he
pierc'd the midmost neck of Dryops,
Who fell before him: him he left,
His knee the spear had crippled;
The mighty sword then smote him
down, and reft away his spirit.
Next from their chariot to earth
he struck two sons of Bias,
Then Tros, Alastor's son, came up,
O simpleton! who knew not this,
nor sweet of temper was he;
But fierce and earnest. While with hand
Touching his knees, he plung'd the sword
and stabb'd him in the liver.
Forth gush'd the liver: dusky blood
His lap with dire pollution fill'd:
As fail'd the living force. But next,
Pierced from ear to ear: then he
Smote on the head with hilted sword,
and warm'd with blood the weapon:
There crimson Death his eyen press'd
Piercing the tender wrist: but he
And viewing death in front; until
Lopt off the head and cast afar
Shot from the spine on high: so hé
Rhigmus, who came from loamy Thrace.
And dash'd him from the chariot.
but the same spear subdued him,
Fix'd in his back: thereat the steeds
When huffling wind the flames doth
roll and whisk their wreaths on all sides:
So with the spear on every side,
and dark earth reek'd with carnage.
As when one yokes broad-fronted steers
On a hard floor, the lowing brutes
quick with their feet do crush it;
with gore of men were pelted,
Splash'd from the tires and horses' hoofs:

div.note: It is agreed that "the lord of Helicon" means Neptune.

div.head: BOOK XXI

l.seg: But when unto the ford they reach'd
Then were the Troians split in twain:
Fled in disorder, scatter'd o'er the plain,
Were on the yester scar'd to flight,
And in their faces Juno pour'd
thick mist, to stay their hurry.
Then in, with mighty dash, they plung'd;
The banks beside it echoed high;
As when, the force of flame to shun,
The Joveborn hero then his spear
Leapt in, with sword alone in hand,
Slashing them right and left: but they,
with blood the stream was redden'd.
So on the dreadful river's flood
The Troians shelter'd them. But he,
Seiz'd him, from out the river pick'd
These from the water led he forth,
Like fawns, and with the wellcut thongs,
Upon their twisted vests, with these
he bound their hands behind them
And bade his comrades them conduct
Then to the river back he sped,
And there did he Lycaon meet,
From out the river fleeing; whom
Who from a figtree wild would hew
with the sharp axe new branches
For chariot-rim; but on him came
Carried on shipboard; but the son
A stranger, ransom'd him, and sent
Whence he by stealthy journeys came
but on the twelfth did Fortune
To send him on unwilling feet
Nor helm nor buckler guarded him:
he on the ground had cast them,
As eke his spear: for-that the toil
Distress'd him, and his knees subdued.
Saw him, and sore indignant spake
Will surely rise again to life
As this man now return'd I see,
Whom I at heav'nly Lemnos sold;
Come! of our spear then shall he taste
May learn, if back he still will come
the stalwart, safe shall keep him."
So, as he waited, boil'd his heart;
the lengthy spear to pierce him,
He stoop'd and eager ran beneath,
Over his back the spear was fix'd,
Then he with one hand caught the
spear, and held it; with the other
For I beside thee, days agone,
When, in the wellwall'd orchard caught,
a hundred beeves I fetch'd thee.
Three times as much my ransom cost;
and now twelve times the morning
But to thy hands hath deadly Doom
I ween, must hate me: soon to die,
His daughter, her did Priam wed,
From her we twain were born;
and thou wilt both her children slaughter.
On me now cometh woe likewise;
Again to scape thy hands, to which
some heav'nly Fate hath brought me.
But one thing will I say; and thou
Spare me, sith as I was not born
of the same womb as Hector,
The man who slew thy comrade dear,
So then did Priam's gallant son
"No ransom now to me set forth,
For once, — ere that his day of doom
So long, my heart more pleasure had
to spare the lives of Troians,
Nor lov'd their carnage: many then
I took alive, and sold them.
But now in front of Ilium
Into my hands, no longer one
Of all the Troians; most of all,
But come, my friend! die also thou.
Behold me, what a man am I,
Me did a noble sire beget;
Yet even upon me will Death
When some one shall from mé alsó
Whether the spearthrow strike me down,
Loosing the spear, he sat him down,
his two-edg'd sword, and plung'd it
Deep in the bottom of the neck:
So headlong dropt he, stretch'd on
earth, which stream'd with gory dark-
Him by the foot Achilles seiz'd,
and toss'd him to the current, [ness.
And vaunting o'er him in harangue,
they shall from out thy gashes
ne'er thy mother shall compose thee
Shall bear thee on his waters tost
some fish shall seize as fodder,
Who, fretted by the bristling gloom,
Perish ye all, till, — ye in flight,
nor shall your flood avail you,
tho' many bulls ye yield him,
Victims, and in his current drown
Deal as ye may, you evil fate
Which, in my absence, late ye made,
When thus he spake, the River-god
And ponder'd in his boiling soul,
how might he quell from effort
On him Achilles rush'd; but he
Stood forth, with double spear in
hand; for, Xanthus in his bosom
Put courage; sith he wrathful was
When they to nearer distance came,

div.note: Son of Jason: see 7,468: also 23, 741-7.

l.seg: thou, who darest to confront me?
And troth! unhappy are the sires,
to him then spake an answer:
From Axius, whose stream of all
his son, they call my father.
So much of this: but now in turn,
but both his spears the hero
