So with the one he frontwise hit
Nor thro' might pierce it: for, the gold,
gift of the god, repell'd him:
By the right elbow, grazing pass'd
Out of the wound: but it in earth
Against his foeman hurl'd, but miss'd:
Full half its length imbedded was.
Leapt eager on him: he meanwhile
From the o'erhanging bank to tug
Thrice did he hurtle it in vain;
the fourth time, sought to break it:
Too late: for first Achilles' sword
had stabb'd him in the navel.
The foe then, tramping on his breast,
"Lie as thou art. 'Tis hard for thee
A man, who o'er the Myrmidons
Rivers, that trickle to the sea,
So, than the progeny of Jove,
Yea, if he aught avail'd to help,
Beside thee here: but none can fight
with Jove, the child of Saturn.
with him may play the equal,
Tho' from his fulness every Sea
Yet at the lightning-bolt of Jove
when from the sky it crasheth."
He spake, and from the bank upright
And left the vanquish'd on the sands,
There eels and fishes busy were,
By hands and sword of Peleus' son
These next Achilles chas'd, and slew
By swift Achilles; but in wrath
Like to a man, with human voice
As man to men: for aye to thee
If Saturn's child hath granted thee
Out of my channels on the plain
For lo! with carcases are chok'd
Nor able am I, by their mass
fill'd up, to pour my current
Enough! in sooth aghast am I,
But him Achilles, fleet of foot,
shall be, as eke thou biddest.
Only, I may not cease to slay
Ere that I hem them in their walls,
and force to force with Hector
Try, whether of us twain shall be
He spake, and on the Troians rush'd,
"Ye Spirits! oh thou child of Jove,
Little hast thou the counsel kept
Charg'd thee the Troïans to aid
Late spreading in the Western sky,
He spake: but, rushing from the brink,
Leapt in the midst: and after him
Pursued, and all his streams bestirr'd,
Which in thick numbers round about
These cast he forth, hoarse bellowing,
And, dashing on his shield, the stream
His feet to steady. He thereon
Grasp'd, and upwrench'd it, stem and root,
and all the bank around it.
Across with branches thick it fell,
Then from the depth the hero rush'd,
Over the plain to fly, in fear.
Yet cease; but rose with darkling
To quell his effort, and from bale
But he a spearthrow onward shot,
That hunter, who of feather'd wights
With such a rush Pelides sped,
Of brass upon his bosom. He,
from some dark source of waters
Doth with his spade a rill conduct
Clearing the gutter of the clods;
Trickleth adown the slope, and e'en
So did the surges of the stream
Albeit swift was he: but gods
Oft as, reliant on his feet,
If all immortals be his foes,
So oft above his shoulders dash'd
With flying feet aloft; for still
Out-tir'd his knees, and from his feet
lick'd up the dust beneath him.
Then, gazing to the vasty sky,
"Oh father Jove! of all the gods
doth none in pity save me
let aught, that may, befal me.
so much to me is guilty,
I should beneath the gliding shafts
Might Hector slay me, who of all
Caught by the river's might, as tho'
a brook may sweep to ruin."
He spake: then quickly at his side
In mortal semblance: hand to hand
And Neptune, shaker of the Earth,
nor yield too much to terror.
Lo! what reserves of war we twain,
Come from Olympus to thy side,
Nor, to be vanquish'd by a Stream,
But quickly will his power cease,
and thou thyself shalt know it.
And if to us thou listen, we
Not earlier to stay thy hands
Till thou the Troian people coop,
And to the ships return: ourselves,
to earn this glory, grant thee."
But he, (for so the charge divine
On to the plain: and all of it
And corpses floated. High in air
To stay him: for Athene's self
But eke Scamander ceas'd not yet
"Let us at least by joint attack
Dear brother! else o'erthrow will he
Full soon; nor will the Troïans
and from thy springs of water
Fill well thy streams, and stir for aid
And lift a mighty billow high,
Of floating trunks and rocks upwrench'd,
But neither mighty force, I trow,
Nor yet his splendid armour: it,
Full deep within our pools shall lie:
Countless, will I enwrap in sand,
His bones to gather: such a slush
shall hand of man be needed."
So spake he, and with raging might
Curling his waves aloft, with foam
And straight with shout of terror call'd
we deem to be in combat
A match for swirling Xanthus: haste,
full girt with flame, in succour.
I to the briny deep will go,
Of the West wind and sprightly South,
Consume the Troians, — men and arms:
but by the banks of Xanthus
Burn thou the trees, and wrap in fire
himself; nor let him stay thee
Nor sooner lay thy force aside,
save when by skirl of notice
I give the signal; then do thou
When thus she charg'd him, Vulcan aim'd
First in the plain he kindled it,
Which in thick numbers round about
So parched he the plain entire,
Quickly is drièd up; and he,
So drièd then was all the plain,
and burn'd were all the corpses.
Which by the river's dainty streams
The eels and fishes were bestraught,
Hither and thither, from the blast
The River felt his might burnt up,
"O Vulcan! none of all the gods
to thee may play the equal;
Nor I against thy flaming fire
Desist from quarrel: from their walls
Drive out the Troians: what am I
So spake he, parching in the flame,
And as, by fire abundant urg'd,
When under it dry faggots lie,
but in it fat of bacon
So then his dainty streams with fire
And halted, nor would onward flow:
for sore the blast distress'd it
Beyond the rest? not unto me
As to all other gods, by whom
And eke let him withhold: and I
by oath alsó will bind me,
That never will I ward away
the day of woe for Troians,
Not even when in wasting fire
In sake of mortals, with distress
She spake; and quickly did he quench
And back returning rush'd the waves
So both the combatants were stay'd;
Set limit on her wrath, so soon
But on the other heav'nly gods
Noisome; and diverse ways the heart
With mighty turmoil on they came:
his heart was fill'd with laughter,
Rejoicing, when the gods he saw
Thereat, no length of time stood they
Holding his brazen lance; and spake
Or haply dost forget the day
when thou to wound me sentest
so my tender flesh was mangled.
Now then I think to pay thee back
for all thy deeds of evil."
After such words, he stabb'd against
On this, with lengthy thrust of spear,
But she, retiring on the plain,
A rock, which there behind her lay,
Which for a stone of boundary
Herewith she struck upon the neck
Helpless: o'er seven roods of land
And taunting o'er the fallen god
I claim to be than thee, whene'er
Who angry planneth ill to thee,
But him, as thick and deep he sobb'd,
Led by the hand away, and scarce
She, as she hurried, drew regards
Lo! the Flyvixen in the mell
From out of foeman's battle lead: —
She spake: then off Athene rush'd,
and in her heart was joyful.
she smote her on the bosom,
And from her body beat the breath:
And she, with vaunting over them,
Often as they to battle march
We, in that gait, had long ago
Athene spake, and drew a smile
"Why, Shining one! stand off we twain
When others have begun the war.
Palace of Jove. But feud to me
Holdest at all, how much of yore
when, sent by Jove from heaven,
Alone of gods, a year complete
Then round the Troian city I
Broad and right noble; so their town
And thou, upon the woody slopes
Bright god! didst tend the clumsyfooted
brought round the end of labour
Troth! threaten'd he with wellcut thongs
both hand and foot to bind us,
And ship us for the mart of slaves
And vow'd, the ears of both of us
So we, returning, on our path
Enraged for the hire, which he
Dost thou for this achievement bear
Nor on our side contendest, how
With children and with decent wives
If against thee I enter war
Who like to leaves do onewhile bud,
But leave their quarrel to themselves;
and us, from fight withdraw we."
After such speech, away he turn'd;
Much it abash'd him, hand to hand,
Queen of all venison, did scoff,
Thy voice no longer let me hear
That thou in battle wouldest stand
She spake; but no reply came back
Hard is to thee my adverse force,
Hath made thee, and hath granted
might to slay, whomso thou willest.
Better in sooth it is, to kill
But if thou willest war to learn;
then come, and let me teach thee,
How greatly I surpass thee, when
She spake; and both the goddess' wrists
She with the right hand stripp'd away
Laughing; and with it o'er the ears
slapt her, as off she turned.
Out of the quiver fell the shafts:
Fled the encounter, as a dove,
Into the cavern of a rock
hath flown, and there is rescued.
So fled she tearful from the war,
and left her darts behind her. —

div.note: Clumsy-footed. In deference to Lexicographers, I have everywhere thus rendered the rather obscure epithet eilipous: yet I cannot get rid of the suspicion that Homer meant "cloven-footed."

l.seg: by cuff of hand to bargain.
But, pri'thee, with a forward zeal
Vaunt, that by might and main thou hast
So spake he: she from off the ground
And, soon as she recover'd had
That daughter hasted on her path
Palace of Jove; there at the knees
She wept, and her ambrosial veil
Drew her to him, and, laughing sweet,
such ill hath wrought upon thee,
All wantonly, as tho' thou wert
Upon that day, despite of fate,
One part embitter'd by the fray,
Did ravage men of Troy alike
From out a burning city; it
the wrath of gods hath kindled,
And toil on many hath it laid
laid many a toil and sorrow.
At once the Troians in alarm
Aught they retain'd. Then groaning he
And, passing by the wall, bestirr'd
"Hold in your hands the leaved gates
Reach to the city, scar'd in flight:
Here hieth routing them and hence,
But when they respite again at length,
I tremble, lest this ghastly man
So spake he: they the bolts push'd back,
Leapt out in front, from baleful doom
Straight for the town and lofty wall,
parch'd up with thirst and dusty,
Out of the plain they fled: but he
keen with the spear pursued them.
Dire fury did his soul possess,
and in his heart put courage.
He stood by him, to ward away
Stood still; and much his heart within,
"Woe to my fortunes! if I flee
Whither the rest are scar'd in rout,
he even so will catch me,
And butcher me all helpless: but,
if these I leave behind me
Routed by him, and on my feet
make for the plain of Ilus,
Until to Ida's slopes I reach
and in the bushes hide me,
I bathe me in the river,
And, cool'd from sweat, to Ilium
But, O fond heart! why holdest thou
For if, as to the plain I swerve,
With rapid foot outstrip my speed;
from Death and Fate no longer
For troth! the body eke of him
may by sharp brass be pierced;
One life is in him, and they say
As from a thicket deep may come
For e'en if earlier a stab
or shot of dart may wound him,
Not, tho' the weapon pierce him
thro', doth he his valour lessen,
But that he grapple with the foe,
To flee approv'd not, ere he try
Forthwith, he held in front his shield,
which equal was on all sides,
And with the spear took aim at him,
That thou, forsooth, this day wilt storm
O simpleton! for many a woe
must still be borne beside it.
Who in the sake of parents dear,
He spake, and from his heavy hand
Nor miss'd but struck him on the shank:
Gift of the god; which turn'd aside
But bright Apollo foil'd his hope,
Shrouding the foe in copious mist,
Him sent he, quiet to return,
Then he by guile asunder drew
While o'er the wheatful plain he
that soon his feet shall conquer;
Gladly had refuge in the walls
and fill'd with crowds the city.
Nor longer ventur'd they to wait
One for another, and to learn, —
But all, whom feet and knees bare
off, pour'd gladly to the city.

div.head: BOOK XXII

l.seg: THUS scar'd and routed from the plain,
They cool'd themselves from sweat, and drank,
and heal'd the thirst of water,
Me, an immortal son of Jove?
nor yet dost thou beknow me;
Whom thou didst late affray: all these
But me thou wilt not slay; for I
To him Achilles, fleet of foot,
Who from the rampart me hast turn'd:
Ere they might reach to Ilium,
But me of glory great thou now
Right easily; for free art thou
Troth, would I vengeance take on thee,
if but I had the power."
Thus saying, he with haughty soul
Who spanking broad along the lea
So Achileus his feet and knees
But him did aged Priam's eyes
first ken, as o'er the champaign
Swift rush'd he, flashing like the star,
Whose rays in gloom of night appear
Which was alsó by ancient men
For he by far most brilliant is,
and sign of woe is counted,
So brilliant was the brass upon
Then groan'd the aged man, and struck
And call'd, imploring his dear son,
Before the gates for battle stood,
Him the old man, with arms outstretch'd,
Apart from others, lest that thou
Vanquish'd by Peleus' son: for he
than thee by far is stronger.
Ah cruel! would that from the gods
Such as from me: then quickly should
Prostrate: so would my inmost heart
A man, who hath bereaved me
Slaying, or selling in the mart
Now too, my eyes Lycaon miss,
Who stand not with the fugitives,
Now if amid the foeman's host
they live, we might redeem them
For brass and gold is still within:
But if already they be dead
and in the house of Pluto,
This to their parents grief will be,
to me and to their mother;
Yet to the other folk nathless
be screen'd, my child! to rescue
The Troian dames and men of Troy,
To Peleus' son and be thyself
who still have thought and feeling,
Ill-fated; whom on steps of Eld
By noisome doom shall waste, when I
on many a woe have gazed,—
And infants hurl'd upon the ground
And my sons' partners rudely dragg'd
Me last of all before my gates
When stab or shot of dart shall take
the life from out my members.
Those dogs, whom in my palace halls
I feed from off my table,
The guardians of my doors, shall lie
Swilling my lifeblood. Naught for youth
For to the warriour, in death
But when an aged man is slain,
Despite of hoary head and chin,
This do I deem most piteous
He spake; and many a hoary hair
pluck'd from his head: but Hector
With loosen'd robe and open breast
If e'er the soothing teat to thee
I held; dear child! obey me.
Ward from within the wall the foe,
nor stand in front against him,
Cruel! for if he vanquish thee,
But far from us the nimble dogs
by Argive ships shall eat thee."
Thus weeping did the parents twain
to move the mind of Hector:
But still he waited the approach
As at his den a mountain-snake,
With direful anger in his heart
And horrid is his glance of eye,
as round his den he twineth:
So Hector stirr'd not from his post,
But on the tower's shelving ledge
During the deadly night, which first
But I his counsel follow'd not,
I blush before the men of Troy
Lest haply some one, than myself
So will they say: thereat to me
Now, if aside my bossy shield
And prop against the wall my spear,
And with her all the gear, whatso
That I both this to Atreus' sons
will yield, and give beside it,
All of the city's hidden store,
Naught to conceal, but forward bring
The gear, whatever may be coop'd
But oh fond heart! why holdest thou
Should I draw near and supplicate,
yet will he know no pity,
Nor will revere my coming; but
Thus helpless as a woman, when
I shall of armour strip me.
Nowise from oak or rock may we,
Better it is, in instant strife
May learn, to which the Olympian
So heaving in his heart, he stood;
And pois'd the ash of Pelion
Dreadful; and brass upon his frame
In semblance as a blazing fire
But Hector, when he saw, was seiz'd
There to abide; but left the gates
As o'er the mountain-side a kite,
Which flees beneath him; he behind
Souseth right oft upon the game,
with heart intent to gripe it:
So he, all eager for revenge,
Plying his supple knees, to course

div.note: From oak or rock: i. e. from a respectful distance (?).
Enyalius; another name of Ares, as in 13, 520.

l.seg: And they, within the watchman's highth,
Then to the dainty streaming wells
Gush forth, that feed the silver depths
The one with water alway warm
As tho' from blazing fire, aloft
Or as the cold of melting snow,
or ice from out the water.
Where wives and daughters fair of Troy
In former days, while peace was yet,
Hereby ran they, in flight the one,
Brave was in sooth the man who fled,
And fierce their hurry; sith, I wis,
to them the prize of running
No victim was nor hide of ox,
Nay, but they scudded for the life
As fleetly round the goal may race
So round and round three times the twain
In rapid running. All the gods
look'd down to see the contest.
Thereat the Sire of gods and men
Chas'd round his rampart; and my heart
Who never fail'd to burn to me
But come, bethink you, all ye gods!
Whether we now from coming death
shall save him, or shall quickly
A man, who mortal is of birth,

div.note: Watchman's highth; see 2, 793. Wild figtree; 6, 433.

l.seg: Do it: but we, the other gods,
not all shall praise thy doing."
not with a soul so earnest
Have I harangu'd: and fain would I
to thee, my child! be gentle.
Do, as thy bosom prompteth thee,
and start not from thy purpose."
He by such charge Athene spurr'd,
And, speedy darting, down she came
As o'er the mountains may a hound
A fawn, awaken'd from its lair;
It hide, yet tracking, on he scuds,
Oft as toward the Dardan gates
He sped, in hope they might by darts
toward the plain he turn'd him.
As one who dreaming tries to run,
So nor could Hector flee away,
Nor troth! had Hector then so long
But that Apollo first and last
And breathed vigour in his heart
Then to the peoples with his head
the fourth time at the fountains,
two fates he cast within it,
That for the son of Peleus;
He rais'd the scales; and tilting fell
To Pluto fleeted then his years,
And standing near beside him, she
now shall we twain, I reckon,
By Hector's slaughter; tho' he be
Sith as no longer now from us
Falling around the knees of Jove
But stand thou now and breath regain;
By counsel will persuade his heart
with adverse might to front thee."
and in his soul was joyful,
And leaning on the ashen shaft
Him then she quitted, and was found
There, near before his face, she stood,
too hard doth press upon thee,
But let us stand and hold our ground,
To him with word responsive spake
wast thou to me far dearest
Of all my kin, whom Hecuba
Now, more than ever, in my heart
Who, when thy eyes took note of me,
Out of the rampart; but the rest
There to abide: so great a dread
But still my heart was inwardly
fight we: no stint is needed
Of javelins: so shall we know,
Havoc shall make, and bear away
or by thy spear be vanquish'd."
Thro' such pretences of the lip,
When they to nearer distance came
Thrice fled I round the mighty wall
Thy coming to await: but now
To stand against thee: so will I
But come, and gart we by the gods:
these, who of all are highest,
Are eke the best of witnesses
No éxtreme outrage I on thee
will lay, if Jove may haply
and if I rieve thy spirit:
But soon as I have stript away
I to thy friends thy corpse will yield:"
to this be thou too plighted."
To him Achilles, fleet of foot,
As not to lions and to men
And as thro' heart of wolf and lamb
Will I engage; but first, I ween,
Glut with his gore the warriour,
Call to remembrance all thy skill
No more evasion hast thou here:
Cast thee beneath my spear, and thou
shalt in one sum repay me
The sorrows of my comrades, whom
He spake, and poising forward threw
and stoop'd to shun its fury;
So o'er him flew the brazen point,
and in the earth was fasten'd.
But maid Athene caught it up,
not yet my fate thou knewest
From Jupiter; but me thy spear
Thou art, methinketh, apt of speech,
In hope, so might I thro' alarm
Not in my fleeing back shalt thou
(If so the god have granted thee)
thy dart implant; but drive it
Shun now my brazen spear in turn.
O might thy flesh receive it
Entire! so should the war become
By thy destruction; sith to them
He spake, and poising forward threw
Nor miss'd; but in the middle hit
But from the buckler glanc'd the dart
No other ashen shaft had he;
And loudly summon'd to his aid
Asking of him a lengthy spear:
but nowhere near he saw him.
Then Hector in his inward heart
the gods to death have call'd me.
But him the wall doth hide, and me
And now an evil death to me
Escape is none: for thus of old,
To Jove and Jove's far-darting son,
Zealous to rescue me; but now
in turn doth Fate o'erhend me.
for future age to talk of."
He spake, and drew his shining sword,
which sharp and great and stalwart
Hung at his hip, then on he rush'd
for hairs of gold around it
As in the sky at dusk of night
The fairest stud in heaven's host;
which in his right hand Achilles
Brandish'd, with thoughts of deadly bale
And Iraqi ponder'd, where the tender skin
Had stript, did elsewhere screen his flesh:
save twixt the neck and shoulder,
And thro' the tender neck its point
So should his throat avail to speak
Then dropt he in the dust; whereat
"Hector! thy thought it was, I ween,
Secure to be, nor heldest count
Was left, beside the hollow ships;
Now thee foul dogs and birds shall rend,
To him, with puny vigour left
"I by thy life and by thy knees
Give me not up, to dogs a prey,
But from my father's hands accept,
Copper in many kinds, and gold,
And give my lifeless body back
From Troian men and wives of Troy
in rightful flames may shrive me."
To him Achilles fleet of foot
"Seek not, O dog, my knees to touch,
and plead not by my parents.
Oh that, for all thy outrages,
To slice raw pieces of thy flesh
and down my throat devour them!
So is there none, who from thy head
may fray the dogs and vultures:
thy flesh and bones to balance
In gifts of gold, yet even so
Place on the pallet and bewail
Then spake with dying breath to him
But ponder, lest from wrath of gods
What time, before the Skæan gates,
The archer Paris thee shall slay,
While thus he spake, the end of death
Out of his members flew his soul
and reach'd the house of Pluto,
"Die thou: but I shall then to Doom
Such is the will of Jupiter
He spake; and from his prostrate foe
But, placing it aside, he stript
All bloody. Then around him ran
Of Hector; nor came any near,
but left some stab upon him.
And thus spake one, with eye that glanc'd
for touch of man to handle
Is Hector, than when late he wrapt
Thus each man spake, and standing near
But when Achilles, fleet of foot,
"O friends, who of the Argive folk
Sithence the gods have granted us
Who hath more evils wrought alone,
And learn what temper doth the foe
Whether, on such a loss, they think
But, O fond heart! why holdest thou
and while my knees support me.
Ay, if in house of Aïdes
Yet even yonder still will I
But now, the Pæan shouting high,
and bear this carcase with us.
Great glory have we earn'd; for we
To whom the Troians in their town,
as to a god, made prayer."
So spake he, and unseemly deeds
He bor'd the gristle of his feet
And thongs of bullhide thro' them strung:
so to the car he bound him,
Leaving his head to drag: aloft
not loth in flying course to speed them.
As on they dragg'd him, clouds of dust
Abroad were scatter'd; all his head
Thus in the dust was all his head
Her child beholding, rent her hair,
Afar, and wail'd aloud: so eke
was fill'd with shriek and moaning:
Such was the general face of woe,
as tho' from foot to summit
And hardly might the people then
Their king, who thro' the Dardan gates
And vilely rolling in the dirt,
And singly call'd on every man,
"O friends, refrain: and from the walls
Alone to issue, and to seek
With prayer let me meet this man
If haply he my hoary hair,
For he too such a father hath,—
Peleus, who gat and rear'd him,
A woe to Troians, and to me
his force from me hath rieved.
But tho' I grieve, yet not so great
for all of them my anguish,
As is for one, sharp grief for whom
shall drag me down to Pluto,—
Hector!—Oh would that in my hands
With tears and wailing o'er his corpse
would take our fill of sorrow."
So spake he weeping: after him
"My child! and wherefore live I still,
By thy destruction? thou that wast
Within the city, day and night,
Who, as a god, did welcome thee;
for thou to them great glory
Wast, while alive; but now, alas!
do Death and Fate o'erhend thee."
So spake she weeping: but his wife
not yet had heard the tiding
Too true; how that without the gates
But she, withdrawn in a recess
of the tall house, was working
An ample tripod on the fire
O simple! nor discern'd, that far
And dropt upon the ground her mesh;
Then did she once again address
I go to learn this matter:
The voice of our revered queen
I heard, and in my bosom
My heart upleapeth to my mouth;
With terror: troth, a mighty woe
Far from my ear be such a tale!
but grim alarm doth seize me,
Cut off bold Hector all alone,
and o'er the plain do chase him;
Might direful end be set: for ne'er
in the thick troop abode he,
But far in front was wont to rush,
Thus saying, from the chamber forth
With beating heart, and after her
But when unto the troop of men
she reach'd, and to the tower,
Upon the rampart there she stood
A swoon of horror then her eyes
Gasping she fell to earth, and dropt
The braids and ribbons of her tire,
But round, her female kindred throng'd,
She deeply heav'd a moan, and spake
Forth brought us both; thee here in Troy,
And me in sacred Thebe's wall,
would never had he got me!
To hateful mourning: and thy child
To whom, my Hector! birth we gave,
Mayest thou be to him, nor he
to thee; sith Death hath riev'd thee.
For even if his life escape
never will fail to hunt him;
For, all thy acres broad and fair
His eyes dejected close their lids;
his cheeks with tears are flooded;
One then he pulleth by the cloak,
Then one, from those who pity him,
The boy with double parent blest
With blows of fist bemauling him,
'Off to ill luck with this! for not
Then weeping, back the boy doth wend
on knees of his own father
And when thro' weariness of sport
Then he upon a couch would sleep,
Or downy pillows, when his heart
But henceforth will he suffer much,
He, whom the Troïans have call'd
Now from thy parents far apart,
When that the dogs have ta'en their fill,
the crooked worms shall eat thee,
For, sav'd, they useless are to thee,
sith not in them thou liest;
But, burn'd, they honour thee, in sight
So spake she weeping: after her

div.head: BOOK XXIII

l.seg: So they with moan incessant fill'd
When to their galleys they had reach'd
and to the sea of Helle,
Partly were scatter'd on the strand,
And from his bosom spake a word
Loose we not yet beneath the cars
But with our chariots and steeds
For slain Patroclus pour the tear,
which is the dead man's honour.
But after, when with deadly wail
Then let our steeds unharness'd be,
and meet ye here for supper."
He spake; and they in company did moan:
Three times around the corpse they drave
And wept amain; for Thetis rous'd
the love of wail within them.
With tears the very sands were wet,
Sith they for such a hero mourn'd,
To them did Peleus' son lead off
which erst to thee I plighted,—
Hither to drag, and raw to dogs
deal forth, the corpse of Hector;
And that before thy pile would I,
Sever twelve heads of living men,
So spake he, and unseemly deeds
Stretching him prostrate in the dust
The others, each did lay aside
dealt forth to all his comrades.
Then sprawled many a sprightly bull

div.note: Iron. Elsewhere the Homeric knives are brass. Possibly it should rather be translated, "around the spit of iron."

l.seg: In slaughter; many a bleating goat,
The blood from all sides, caught in cups,
Meanwhile, the master of the feast,
An ample tripod on the fire
to set, in hope that quickly
Pelides from his skin would wash
and sware an oath upon it:
of gods the Best and Highest:
Water of washing, none to come
in fire, and raise a barrow,
And shear my tresses: sith again
Into my heart shall pierce, while I
Firewood to bring; and all things else
For a dead man to carry down
Out of our sight, and eke the folk
to their own works betake them."
He spake; and they all zealously
Thereat, when each with busy skill
They banqueted; nor did their soul
of food and drink was ended,
The others to their proper tents,
But Peleus' son upon the strand
Lay mid a troop of Myrmidons,
On a clear space, whereat the waves
Shed balmy round him: for in sooth,
Like to the man in everything,
And stature; and around its form
And right above his head it stood,
No longer live I, but am dead,—
To pass the gates of Aïdes,
There, souls of corpses duly shriv'd,
Repel me, nor beyond the stream
to mix with them, allow me:
Give me thy hand, with tears I pray:
when once to flames ye give me.
Nor surely shall we sit alive,
For canny counsel: but on me,
Yea, and thyself, beneath the wall
Another word and charge I speak,
When burn'd I am, place not my bones
But, as together we were rear'd
When to your country and your house
Carried me, still a boy, in flight
Then, in his home receiving me,
So let a single coffer hold
But him Achilles, fleet of foot,
Why are such charges needed now?
But come, and nearer draw to me:
And deadly wailings let us now
This having said, with loving hands
But fail'd to catch him: for the soul,
His hands together clapt, and straight
A soul and image, yet within
For o'er me stood with tearful wail
All night, and special biddings gave,
So spake he, and in all of them
And they still pitiful did weep,
Shone over them, around the dead:
both men and mules, to carry
And forth they wended: but their hands
And chains firm-plaited; and in front
Up steep and down, sidewise and slant,
thro' many a path they hied them.
But when the buttresses they reach'd
Straightway with lengthy edge of brass,
The stems of lofty leaf-hair'd oaks;
And bound upon the mules. But these
Plodding thro' many a coppice thick,
to reach the flat dale eager. [sur'd,
And there in order on the shore
Sitting they on the spot abode
should yoke his steeds, and gird him
In brazen armour. Quick they rose,
And on the cars the charioteers
Mounted: in front the horsemen went;
Then sheared they their locks of hair,
and on the carcase strew'd them
Moaning; for he to Pluto's realm
But, at the spot arriv'd, whereof
Resting the corpse, they quickly heap'd
a pile to soothe his spirit.
Then did the moody chieftain's heart
For, standing from the pile aloof,
Then, gazing on the purple deep,
That I, when thither safe return'd
and shear to thee my tresses,
Whereat a choice domain thou hast
So vow'd the aged man: but thou
But now, sith homeward wend not I
Grant me, my locks to yield as gift
He spake, and plac'd into the hand
The tresses; and in all the troop
Then on their wailing and lament
had come the shades of sunset,
Doth hearken,) whilom may there be
Now from the burning pile aloof
To care for dinner: we, to whom
Will busy us hereon: with us
But the chief mourners there abode behind,
The pile, this way and that, they fram'd,
And on the very summit plac'd
Took out from all of them the fat,
From head to ankle, piling round
On them he rested bulging jars
He earnest added to the pile,
Nine dogs as favourites were fed
Eke two of these into the flames
And after them, twelve gallant sons
Whom he by brazen edge laid low,
So to the iron heart of fire
which erst to thee I plighted.
Lo! in the fire twelve gallant sons
Not for the fire's devouring jaws,
but for the dogs reserve I."
So threaten'd he: yet not the dogs
But, watching near him day and night,
Frayed the dogs away, and him
that any drag and rend him;
From heaven to the plain below,
Yet still did not the flames consume
Then did the moody chieftain's heart
For, standing from the pile aloof,
to the two Winds he prayed,
And from a golden goblet eke
Imploring, that in speed they come,
the corpse with fire to kindle,
And that the fuel haste to catch.
Hied to the Winds as messenger
But they, assembled in the halls
And on the marble threshold stood.
They, when their eyes beheld her,
All started on their feet, and each
But to be seated she refus'd,
and spake her word before them:
"No seat for me: hence wend I forth
To the far land of Æthiops,
Unto the immortals: eke would I
If that ye hurry to consume
Over the deep free course they found
to blare, and heav'd the surges
Falling upon the pile: and dire
Then fiercely puffing all night long
And swift Achilles, all that night,
Out of a golden bowl did teem
The earth around him, calling aye
As, when a bridegroom's sudden fate
The father, who his bones must burn,
So then Achilles, who the bones
But when, announcing light on earth,
Then droop'd the blazing of the pile
The Winds upon their courses hied
Over the sea of Thrace: but it
But Peleus' son on other side
Meanwhile the chieftains were in troop
And as toward him these advanc'd,
their clank and hubbub woke him.
So, raising him, upright he sat
and spake his word before them:
"O son of Atreus, and the rest,
First, wheresoe'er along the pile the flame
With sparkling wine its force, throughout;
in the mid pile we plac'd him:
were burnt, both men and horses.)
Them in two folds of fat will I
Keep, until I myself in turn
hide in the courts of Pluto.
But suited; such the height: and ye,
A broad and lofty barrow rear,
So spake Pelides fleet of foot,
First, wheresoe'er along the pile the flame
With sparkling wine its force, throughout;
Then into double folds of fat
The white bones of the kindly chief
And stor'd them in Achilles' tents,
And fram'd a circle for the tomb,
And when the tomb was all embank'd,
And from his galleys bare to view
Cauldrons and tripods, steeds and mules,
In blameless work, and tripod ear'd,
The next, an untam'd six-year mare,
For the third driver, in the midst
Four measures holding, new to fire,
For him whose coursers fourth might be,
But for the fifth a double urn,
new to the fire, he destin'd.
Upright then stood he forth, and spake
"O son of Atreus, and the rest
Now if the Achaians contest held
Myself the chiefest prize, I trow,
For surely know ye, how by far
For they immortal are: of yore
in turn to me vouchsaf'd them,
But doubtless I aloof shall stay
whose loss they yet are mourning,
A lord so kindly, who full oft
Their heav'nly manes: so stand they there,
But ye, the others of the host,
Trustet the goodness of his steeds
So spake Pelides: at the word
Of them Eumelus, prince of men,
After him rose the son of Tydeus,
Who led beneath the yoke the steeds
He late had taken, tho' their lord
Stept forward: he beneath the yoke led
Spryfoot and Blazer, horse and mare:
that there he might disport him,
Great riches held from Jove, and dwelt
Her, keen persistent in the course,
At Pylos nurtur'd, drew the car.
Wisely advis'd to good the youth,
Albeit young thou art, and thee
Well knowest thou around the goal
Are slowest in the race: whence eke,
But of the rest the steeds in sooth
Know not more fully than thyself
But come, dear fellow! in thy heart cast
Right crafty, lest from out thy hand
Far more by counsel than by strength
By counsel and by might of mind
But whoso, on his nimble steeds
are spent, nor aught prevails he.
But whoso worser steeds may drive,
With eyen fasten'd on the goal
the thong of ox should tighten;
A mark, right easy to discern,
behold: thou shalt not miss it.
A fathom high above the earth
Standeth,a trunk of oak or pine,
And near it, two white slabs of stone
Just in the narrows of the road;
but smooth the course is round them.
Such was it eke of yore, or mark'd
where some dead man was buried.
Right close to this approaching, drive
And to the right steed yield the reins,
and freely lash and urge him.
But let the courser on the left
That, troth! the centre of thy wheel
may doubt to graze the surface
Of the rough stone. Shun thou to prove
But keen, my son! and wary be:
Thou round the goal, not one of them
Not even if behind thy heels
which was a brood of heaven,
Or the proud steeds of Tros, which here
After such words, back to his seat
When charges to his proper son
gain'd the first lot for starting.
After him, third, was Atreus' son,
Himself by far the chiefest, came,
When thus in row drawn up they stood,
In the wide level seen afar;
the breadth of plain travers'd they,
Far from the galleys; but the dust
Stood as a cloud, or like a squall;
Along the breezes wav'd the manes:
the cars, at one while vanish'd,
Up stood the drivers from their seats,
Each shouted to his steeds, and they
Back to the hoary brine would come,
And worth of each; for hard was stretch'd
The rapid mares of Pheres' stud
So near, they alway seem'd as tho'
they on the car would mount them.
For aye against him lay their heads,
And now they haply would have pass'd,
But bright Apollo, wroth of old
Struck from his hands the shining scourge:
then tears from out his eyen
Fell in his anger, when the mares
He saw; while, of the lash bereft,
Nor did Athene fail to mark
And gave him back the scourge,
and breath'd into his horses vigour;
His yoke: down fell the pole:
the steeds this way and that were parted.
On to his elbow: nostrils, mouth,
His eyen were with tears brimful,
his throat was drain'd of juices.
Then past him Tydeus' son held on
After him kept the son of Atreus,
"Step on, ye also; strain to speed.
I claim not, that the coursers
to them hath swiftness granted now,
And be not left behind them,
Quickly; lest in disgrace ye sink,
Outstrip you in the race. And why
For roundly will I say, and this
No longer shall ye twain receive
By the sharp brass will slay you,
If by your heartlessness a prize
But keep ye pace and follow hard
If in the narrows of the road
I find a place to pass him."
So spake he earnest: they beneath
Ran swifter on, awhile; but soon
Saw, where the road was rent away
Whose pent-up waters sapp'd the ground
While narrow is the road; and soon,
Where it is broader, pass me;
Lest on the chariot thou strike
Urging the horses with the scourge,
Alike as tho' he heard not.
Then, far as may a youthful man,
Over his shoulder fling the quoit,
But Atreus' son in mid career
Would in the dust themselves be cast,
None is than thee more deadly.
But I will challenge thee to oath,
And of the prize despoil thee."
After such words, with cheery voice
"Stay not behind, nor wait to think,
To them far earlier, I trow,
Will feet and knees be weary,
Than unto you: for from them both
The strength of youth is parted."
So spake he earnest: they beneath
The coursers, who across the plain
And saw a signal horse in front,
Who else was bay of colour,
But bare a white spot, like a moon,
"O friends, who of the Argive folk
Do I alone the coursers view,
Or eke do ye descry them?
Another pair of steeds than erst,
I ween, met damage on the plain,
Surely ahead I saw the mares
But them I nowhere now may ken,
Turn, as I gaze, on every side
Hath dropt, or round the pillar
Could not his coursers duly hold
There (bode I) forth he fell, and brake
And from the road his mares, possest
My eyen may beknow: yet here,
Among the Argives thou, I ween,
Art not so much the youngest,
Nor peer thy eyen from thy head
But thou with words of bluster aye
Dost prate: and wherefore need we
Still the same coursers hold the lead,
Which at the turn were foremost,
Eumelus' mares, and in the car
To him with anger, front to front,
In all beside thou fallest, sith
Come! for a wager stake we now
And let us both as umpire take
Which steeds are foremost: so shalt
So spake he: but Oïleus' son,
And now had he haply farther gone
And spake a word before them:
Yea, with another were ye wroth,
Soon will they hither come themselves,
Then each the coursers shall beknow,
Which first, and which are second."
He spake: but Tydeus' son drew near,
He alway lash'd the steeds: but they
With daubs of dust was powder'd;
And scarce a track behind them
In the thin dust the wheel had left;
So flew they o'er the champaign.
He in mid company drew up;
Abundant sweat down to the ground,
From crests and flanks and bosom.
Hanged upon the yoke his scourge.
And to his comrades high of heart
And eared tripod, for their lord:
Yet even so with nimble steeds
As wheel is parted from the horse,
Draweth his master with the car;
So far by Nestor's spotless son
So far alone: yet was he once
Yet quickly won he back the loss;
For more and more the vigour
And if for both the chariots
Who a spearthrow was left behind
For slowest were of all the field
Latest of all, with long delay
And to the Argives standing forth
But come ye; upon him a prize
In second rank: but Tydeus' son
Must with the first be honour'd."
So spake he: then the chieftains
And now had he the mare on him
This word: for of my proper prize
In pity, that his speedy mares
And that himself is worthy: yet
Vows to th' immortals; never then
Had he been thrown to rearmost.
Thy tents have gold in plenty,
And brass and sheep and servant-maids
Take greater prize to give him,
Or even now before us: so
But I to none will yield the mare:
To her adventure, must with me
So vaunted he, and drew a smile
Fresh prize from out my stores to give,
A corslet I on him bestow,
I took: of brass the substance is,
Are streams of shining tin; and this
A goodly price would yield him."
To bear it from his tent: but he
And plac'd it in Eumelus' hands;
And he with joy receiv'd it.
Into his hand a sceptre plac'd,
To silence: then the godlike man
Spake out his heart before them:
Wast sage; what now hath ail'd thee?
Loss to my coursers hast thou wrought,
Thrusting thy horses to the front,
Which were by far the worser.
But come now! ye who leaders are
Pass ye a sentence in the midst
And carried off the mare, altho'
His steeds were far the worser,
Only that he himself in might
Or else, come! I myself will judge;
Nor do I ween that any
For fair shall be my trial.
Touch the steeds, and swear by Neptune
"No more of this; for, troth, am I
Younger by far, and thou than me
Well knowest thou, how youthful men
Therefore thy heart will bear with me.
The mare, which I had won: and eke
If more thou shouldest ask me
From my own stores to bring, this too
At once would I present thee
Right willing, rather than with thee,
All days, and an offender be
Son, and led the mare and plac'd her
As in a growing crop of wheat,
The ears doth warm and soften,
Youth hath thy mind o'ercome: henceforth
Shun thou to trick thy betters.
Had not so lightly calm'd me.
But troth, much thou endured hast
For me, and much hast toiled,
As thy good sire and brother too;
Will I comply: yea, and the mare,
So shall these learn, that ne'er my heart
He spake; and to Noëmon's hands,
Gave up the mare, and took himself
And Merion, who fourth drave in,
But the fifth prize, which still was left,
Bare it, and spake beside him:
"Here now! let this to thee alsó,
Old friend! be made a keepsake
And hark! this prize I give thee
Gratis! for neither race of foot
Wrestling, nor boxing: for on thee
He spake, and plac'd it in his hands;
And he with joy receiv'd it,
Hath fail'd my limbs: no longer
The feet rush agile, nor the hands
O that I still were só in youth,
As when the Epeians did of yore
Then no one might with me compare,
Then Iphiclus, tho' swift he was,
Yet did my feet outrun him,
And with the spear I overpitch'd
Only the sons of Actor then
Who two to one against me were,
On fire with zeal of conquest,
Sith as the noblest prizes yet
Twin bretheren were these: and one
Such once was I: but now in turn
To Eld unkind must bow him:
'Tis fitting: but in days of old
Do thou by games of funeral
And willing I this gift accept;
Yea, and my heart is joyful,
That alway thou my kindly soul
What honour seemly is for me
For this, on thee may heav'nly gods
So spake he: and Pelides hied
Thro' the thick crowd of Argives,
When he unto the whole address
Then of the painful boxing match
— six years its age, which hardest
Is to be tam'd, this led he in,
Next, to the vanquish'd forth he set
Upright he stood, and spake his word
"O sons of Atreus, and the rest
Raising aloft the first, to box:
In sight of all the Achaians,
His the toilbearing mule shall be,
Unto his tent to lead it:
But to the vanquish'd I behight
So spake he: then forthwith arose
A man both huge and comely
Of old well skill'd in boxing.
And thus spake forth his challenge:
"Near let him come, who means to get
But none, I trow, by strength of fist
From me the mule shall carry
Of all Achaians; sith I boast
In battle of the spear and sword
I yield: doth that content you?

div.note: Pheres was father of Admetus and grandfather of Eumelus.
I express what I suppose to be the poet's meaning.
Clumsy-footed. In deference to Lexicographers, I have everywhere thus rendered the rather obscure epithet eilipous: yet I cannot get rid of the suspicion that Homer meant "cloven-footed."

l.seg: Never may man in every work
For roundly will I warning give
of that which I will complish:
His flesh asunder will I rip,
and crush the bones within him.
Here let his kinsmen wait around,
And bear his shatter'd frame away,
they all were dumb in silence.
a godlike man, to meet him,
Whose sire the prince Mekisteus was,
Who, when in former days at Thebes
Came to the games, and conquer'd there
And hearten'd him with words: for much
A girdle fitted to his loins
So then the two wellgirded came
Dire was the crackle of their chaps;
sweat dripp'd from all their members.
And rising, smote upon his cheek:
nor long the blow endur'd he;
For on the spot his gallant limbs
As on the weedy beach a fish,
So with the blow upleaped he:
Rais'd him with both his hands.
Who led him thro' the gazing ring,
Spitting thick blood between his teeth,
his head on one side drooping,
And hasted, to his tent to fetch
A tripod, on the fire to stand,
The vanquish'd to repay, he set
four beeves her price they counted.

div.note: The epithet given to Edipus shows that Homer followed a totally different tale from that of the Attic tragedians.

l.seg: Upright he stood, and spake his word
eke in this game to try them."
And next, a man of cunning deep,
Fronting; and clasp'd with sturdy hands
As when, within a lofty house,
Their backs in hardy tustle crack'd;
from head to foot sweat trickled,
And many a wale, blood-purple, ran
Yet alway eager still they strain'd,
But when at length their struggle
Let one or other lift his man;
then Jove the rest shall settle."
He spake, and lifted him: with guile
Struck him within the knee, behind,
and tripp'd and cast him backward,
And fell upon his breast: thereat
But barely stirr'd him from the ground;
And both together fell to earth,
a third time would they wrestle;
and by his word withheld them:
The victory to both is due:
Retire ye: so may eke the rest
And wiping off the dust, sat down,
Then for the swift of foot anew
A bowl of silver featly wrought
was first: it held six measures;
And for its beauty, all the earth
no peer of it could furnish,—
But o'er the hazy breadth of sea
And lod'd it safe on Lemnos' shore,
Offspring of Jason, yield it up
And now Achilles set it forth,
— a prize, his friend to honour,
For him, whoso with rapid feet
might of the host be nimblest.
Next, for the second brought he out
But half a talent's weight of gold
for the last prize he destin'd.
Upright he stood, and spake his word
eke in this game to try them."
So spake he: then Oileus' son,
mid all the youths was signal.
At very start full speed they made,
Darted; but close upon his heels
The spindle, whence she draweth out
So near Ulysses kept, and trod
Ere dust could fill them; o'er his head,
And, eager to outstrip, press'd on,
while all the Achaians cheer'd him.
But when at length the last career
and to my feet show favour."
So utter'd he the word of pray'r,
His limbs, both feet and hands above
But when they quickly were about
Where lay the offal of the bulls
Slew for Patroclus: nose and mouth
And lifted up the bowl: the ox
Who, blowing from his lips the filth,
Of the field-roaming ox, and spake

div.note: Thoas, king of Lemnos; the father of Hypsipyle, mother of Eunëus: see 7, 469, and 14, 230.
I do not certainly understand the description of the Greek, but I think spinning, not weaving, is intended.

l.seg: Like to a mother, stands beside
So spake he moanful: him they all
Who, smiling jocund, spake a word
"My friends! well know ye, — yet will I
The immortals on more ancient men
For Ajax is in years to me
But this one is of elder birth,
A raw old man, they say, he is:
with him, of all the Achaians
None easily in speed may vie,
So to Pelides fleet of foot
he by his words gave glory.
shall not in vain be utter'd:
But a full talent now of gold
I give, for half the talent."
He spake, and plac'd it in his hands:
and he with joy receiv'd it.
Which, late, Patroclus stript from him.
And upright stood, and spake his word
Clad in gold panoply, to fight
And each of other trial make
And reach to graze the cover'd parts,
Him with this falchion of Thrace,
Will I adorn; the which I took
But let the twain in common bear
And in our tents will we beside
with a rich banquet grace them."
And after him rose Tydeus' son,
But soon as, from the throng apart,
In middle of the folk they both
But when, advancing each on each,
Thrice sally did they make, and thrice
Then Ajax struck upon the shield
which equal was on all sides,
Nor reach'd the flesh behind; but it
But Tydeus' son at Ajax' neck
Thereat the Achaïans, smit with dread
lest aught go wrong with Ajax,
Bade them their rivalry to stay
Bearing it, with the thongs well-carv'd,
But him in sooth divine Achilles
And on his galleys carried it
Upright he stood, and spake his word
eke in this game to try them.
If one, in country far remote,
of iron this shall yield him
For rustic needs: from his estate
shall none thro' lack of iron,
Shepherd or ploughman, seek the town;
Uprose also that match for gods,
And whirling flung it: all the crowd
did naught but mock his effort.
Then a third throw with sturdy hand
But when the rugged mass was hurl'd
So far as may a herdsman fling
Amid the kine, so far he shot
Then for the archers forth he set
Ten double were, and single ten:
and on the sands at distance
He plac'd a blue-prow'd galley's mast,
whence with thin cord he fasten'd
A trembler pigeon by the foot,
and bade them aim toward it.
Whoso might hit it, to his tent
But if one miss'd the bird, yet shot
He should the single axes take,
sith as his skill was meaner.
And the first lot to Teucer fell.
Right forcibly: nor yet did he
The bird he miss'd, sith as the god
Yet went his arrow thro' the cord,
The cord was sever'd by the shot;
And pierc'd her thro' beneath the wing:
Stuck in the ground before his feet;
but quick from out her members
Fleeted the life: she droop'd the neck,
So fell she far away: thereat
And Teucer to the hollow ships
And eke a cauldron new to fire
Then up did Atreus' son arise,
then spake a word before them:
"O son of Atreus, well we know
Thou shalt my chiefer prize possess
Escort it: but the spear we give
If pleasant to thy heart it be:
for I do greatly urge it."

div.head: BOOK XXIV

l.seg: So was the concourse of the games
And for delight of slumber sweet
Wept, at remembrance of his friend
Rememb'ring all the toils and schemes,
So, tender tears he dripp'd, reclin'd
on side, on face, or backward.
Then rising upright off he reel'd
Along the strand, where early Dawn
And Hector fasten to the car
tomb he three times thus had dragg'd him,
Again within the tent would rest,
With face cast forward in the dust:
Warded all outrage of the flesh,
Even tho' dead, and all around
Lest that the skin be stript away
So he in mood of fury wrought
Of whom the blessed gods aloft
had pity, as they view'd him;
And much they urg'd the Argicide,
keenest of spies, to steal him.
To all the others pleasing was
such deed; but not to Juno,
And Priam's self and all his folk,
Then bright Apollo spake his thought
But now that he is hent in death,
heart ye have not, to save him
For wife and mother to behold,
And for the people; who would quick
ye gods are bent on succour,
Who neither rightful is in mind
nor may his breast be melted,
But brutal as a lion is;
Sallies with mighty force to clutch
the flocks and glut his hunger.
Yet after many a tear and wail
For in the breast of men the Fates
But this man, day by day sithence
But danger; lest, tho' brave he is,
For on a heap of senseless clay
To him in angry mood replied
"Lord of the silver bow! thy word
If equal honour ye shall put
Yet Hector mortal was of birth
and suck'd the breast of woman;
and gave her as a consort
To Peleus, whom of mortal men
And in their wedding all ye gods
Comrade of rascals! harp in hand
Diverse the honour of the twain
Of men who dwelt in Ilium,
As eke to me: for-that he ne'er
For never did my altar lack
And fragrant fat and streams of wine;
To steal bold Hector, pass we o'er:
It may not be: for day and night
Therefore, if some one of the gods
So might I speak a canny word
So spake he: then to bear his word
Who in mid course from Samos' isle
Leapt in the black expanse of the sea;
She to the bottom sank forthwith,
Which, in the horn of roaming ox,
In a smooth hollow grot she found
Nymphs of the brine, assembled sat
Was weeping for her spotless son,
Upon the loamy clods of Troy
far from his home to perish.
Then Iris fleet of foot drew near,
and stood, and spake her message:
I fear to mingle; for my heart
Yet go will I; nor shall the word
After such words, her form divine
she wrapt, and hied to speed her.
Then in a veil of dusky blue,
than which no garb is darker,
the wave of sea around them
Shrunk; and emerging on the beach
Eke all the other blessed gods
So she by father Jove sat down;
Yielded the seat: with cheery words
Hold a fair golden cup: but she
Thereat the Sire of men and gods
oh Thetis: I too know it:
Still will I utter, even so,
and o'er the corpse of Hector.

div.note: The lead, with the end of the cord attached to it, was enclosed in a cow-horn (they say) that the fish might not bite it off, with the hook.

l.seg: And some would urge the Argicide,
keenest of spies, to steal him:
In reverence of thee, and eke
Quick to the army hie, and give
Say, that the gods are wroth with him,
Chiefest am I in anger; sith
nor gave him back for ransom:
So haply may he fear from me,
and yield the corpse of Hector.
But I will Iris send alsó
Bidding him, for his son's release,
And costly presents bear, the which
And, speedy darting, down she leapt
Hasting to reach her proper son.
Within his tent she found him
They a huge shaggy ram, within
But she, his queenly mother, close
And soothing him with hand and voice
"My child! how long in tears and wail,
Eatest thy heart away, nor aught
Nor woman's love? yet good it is
Somewhile: for short to me thy life
Doth crimson Death stand near to thee,
from Jove I bring a message,
To say, the gods are wroth with thee,
Chiefest is he in anger; sith
But come; release the carcase dead,
Then her Achilles, fleet of foot,
"So be it! whoso gifts may bring,
eke let him take the carcase,
Thus did the mother and the son
With winged accents, each to each,
Our word, that for his son's release
And costly presents bear, the which
But let him lonely wend, nor take
The mules and wheeled car to guide,
To bear the hero's body, whom
For, such a comrade of his road
Who by his side will stay, until
The hero will no outrage do,
And with much sweetness will he spare
He spake; then on her message sped
And to the house of Priam came:
there din and wailing found she.
The sons around the father sat
With tears their garments: in the midst
Wrapt in a cloak, his shape was mark'd;
Was dung, which plenteous his hands
His daughters in the chambers wide
and his sons' wives were mourning,
Pierc'd with remembrance of the brave,
Then by the side of Priam stood
and let not dread possess thee.
But fill'd with thoughts of kindliness:
from Jove I bring a message,
Who, far asunder, yet for thee
And costly presents bear, the which
But lonely thither wend, nor take
The mules and wheeled car to guide,
To bear the hero's body, whom
For, such a comrade of thy road
Who by his side will stay, until
The hero will no outrage do,
And with much sweetness will he spare
So utter'd Iris fleet of foot,
Then to his sons the king gave charge,
The mules to harness, and on it
Bidding me, for my son's release
And costly presents bear, the which
But come: thy counsel speak to me,
how to thy mind it seemeth.
For of myself my heart and soul
Unto the ships to hie, within
So spake he: then his consort wail'd,
for which in lands of strangers
What! meanest thou alone to seek
The presence of a man, by whom
In deadly strife were slain? in sooth
shall set on thee his eyen
And catch thee, he no reverence
will show to thee, nor pity.
Let us now pour the tear apart
when to the light I bare him,
Spun on his thread of birth, that he,
On whom oh might I fasten me,
So for my child were vengeance ta'en;

div.note: O lady. The Greek word is that which I have elsewhere rendered elf-possessed: it here has its degenerate Attic use. Nearly the same remark holds of sorely (for greatly) in v, 198.

l.seg: In front, to rescue men of Troy
Then aged Priam, peer of gods,
did thus in turn address her:
For if of men who tread on earth
Now, sith myself have heard the god
and front to front have seen him,
I go; nor shall his word be vain.
my doom it be to perish,
This wish I. Soon as I in arms
my son have clasp'd, and vented
Thence twelve of every sort he took,
Of gold ten talents duly weigh'd
Two brought he out, and cauldrons four;
A grand possession. Men of Thrace
on him of yore bestow'd it,
When he on ambassy went forth.
But now, in zeal to ransom
His much-lov'd son, the aged man
And from the porch with railing words
At home ye have, that therefore now
ye hither come to plague me?
Tearing from me my noblest son?
but ye yourselves will learn it:
For by his death the Achaïans
To slay you. As for me, before
Betoss'd and spoiled, oh may I
He spake, and with his staff dispers'd
Before the old man's ire: but he
And eke Polites good at need,
Unto these nine the aged man
Would that ye all before the ships
Alas my hapless fate! for I
Of all in widespread Troy: of whom
And Hector, who mid men below
was as a god, nor seemed
A child of mortal parents, but
These Ares hath destroy'd for me,
Men who to plunder lambs and kids
Will ye not stir you speedily
And stow all these my wares on it,
So spake he earnest: they, beneath
Dapper and newly fram'd; and bound
Then from the peg aloft they took
Boxen, which rear'd a boss on high,
Together with the yoke, a strap
This to the polish'd pole they join'd,
And o'er the staple hook'd the ring:
thrice on each side they girded
Around the boss: then bound it fast,
Thereafter brought they neath the yoke
These, Priam and the herald old,
Themselves would harness to the car
Before the horses, there she stood,
That back from foemen ye may come
To Ida's monarch, who the whole
Which dearest is of fowls to him
On the right hand: so shall thyself,
On this reliant, seek the ships
Then would I surely urge on thee
Not to the Argive ships to go,
Then aged Priam, peer of gods,
did thus in turn address her:
Hands unto Jove to raise, is good,
Then to the keeper of his house
That she upon his hands should pour
And she beside him quickly stood,
With hands thus cleansed, he receiv'd
Then, standing in the midmost court,
With eyes to heaven upward cast,
Grant me, Achilles' tents to reach
Which dearest is of fowls to thee
On the right hand: so shall myself,
On this reliant, seek the ships
So he his prayer spake; and Jove,
Straightway an eagle sent he forth,
which eke they call the Dusky.
Widely as spread the doors, amid
In mansion of some wealthy man,
So far his wings apart were spread;
Rightwise he seem'd to them to sheer:
but they, to see the omen,
Were comforted; thus to them all
the aged man did mount him,
Earnest: so drave he from the porch
On the four-wheeled car in front
Guided the mules with costly load;
Which down the city with the whip
the old man plied behind him
With many a wail his dear ones mov'd,
as tho' to death he wended.
When thro' the city they were come
The train, returning on their path,
Notic'd the pair, upon the plain
The aged man, and thus address'd
"O Hermes! sith to thee in chief
and thou, to whom thou willest,
Dost hearken: hie thee! and untó
King Priam so conduct, that none
He spake, nor found his minister
But he straightway beneath his feet
And o'er the boundless reach of Earth,
So took he eke the rod, wherewith
the eyes of men he witcheth,
This in his hand engrasping, flew
Quickly to Troas' land he came
and to the flood of Helle,
And hied to speed him, like in guise
Of age most graceful, when the down
But they, when past the mighty mound
Halted the horses and the mules,
Over the earth just then the dusk
and spake a word to Priam:
Near us a man I see, and think
But let us with the coursers flee;
Touching his knees, his pity seek,
if haply he may spare us."
He spake, and with appalling dread
Approaching, seiz'd the old man's hand,
Who nigh thee round about are spread,
And if, thro' night's swift-rushing gloom,
of these should one espy thee,
what then would be thy counsel?
Neither thyself art young of years,
The arms of foemen to repel,
But I no mischief will on thee
Will I thy safety guard: for thee
to my dear sire I liken."
Then aged Priam, peer of gods,
did thus in turn address him:
"In truth so standeth all the case,
dear child, as now thou sayest:
But over me some god, I wis,
To him in turn the minister,
But come, explain thou this to me,
Dost haply treasures rich and large
In foreign land; that of thy stores
so much may safe be hoarded?
do all of you in terror
Hurry to flee, now as thy child,
Signal; who never fail'd to match
Then aged Priam, peer of gods,
That hast so truly named the doom
To him in turn the minister,
thou askest but to try me:
Oft have these eyes thy son beheld
Warring; and when against the ships
For to Achilles squire am I:
And I a Myrmidon am call'd.
Who doth in varied wealth abound,
and, like to thee, is aged.
Six sons around him still abide,
and I to him am seventh:
And I, when lots we cast, was ta'en
Now from the galleys o'er the plain
I come: for in the morning
Ye round the city must confront
they pine; nor can the chieftains
Then aged Priam, peer of gods,
Come, all the truth recount to me;
Him to the dogs already cast,
To him in turn the minister,
"Him, aged sire, not yet the dogs
But so he lieth in the tents
Still, as at first. Already now
Sithence he there is stretch'd: nor yet
at all his flesh is rotted,
Nor eaten is by worms, which feed
Yet outrage none upon his flesh
may rest. A sight of marvel
Thyself would judge it; how the gore
from all his skin is washen;
How fresh he lieth, not impure;
but clos'd are all the gashes,
Tho' plentiful: for many a man
So do the blessed gods on high
Even tho' life is fled: for, him
So spake he: but the aged man
"Oh! good it is, my child, to give
while as my son was living,
Forgat he in his halls the gods,
But come! this cup so fairly wrought,
out of my hand receive thou;
And for my rescue and defence,
with aid of gods, escort me,
Till I within the tent arrive
To him in turn the minister,
"Younger am I, old man, than thee:
yet vainly dost thou tempt me:
Who biddest me from thee accept
and in my heart do shudder
To plunder him; lest aught of ill
But unto thee as escort, I
No spoiler shall on thee be lur'd,
then on the car and horses
he caught the scourge and bridles,
And in the horses and the mules
But when unto the moat they came
Slumber; and backward push'd the bolts,
So with the wain and brilliant gifts
But when the tent of Peleus' son they reach'd,
Which for their lord the Myrmidons had built
And from the meadow heap'd aloft
But round, with closely planted stakes
Whose door a single beam of larch
(Three of the common sort) would lift
And for Pelides fleet of foot
And, from the car on to the earth
Hight Hermeas; sith as my sire
to thee as convoy sent me.
But truly now upon my path
for troth! it were a scandal,
If, face to face, immortal gods
But enter thou and clasp his knees,
And sire and child, entreaty make:
so shalt thou move his bosom."
for he the mules and horses
Stay'd to make fast; but the old man straight hied him
Where sat Achilles, dear to Jove;
and duly there he found him.
Apart his comrades had their seats;
A meal of food and drink;
and still a tray remain'd beside him.
Tall Priam pass'd them both unseen,
And stooping clasp'd the hero's knees,
and kiss'd the hands so dreadful,
Hands that were many a time distain'd
As when within his proper folk
He fleeth, smit with thick remorse,
Who on the deadly steps of Eld
far on, like me, is carried.
Nor standeth any by his side
Yet doth he verily, I wis,
Joy in his soul, and every day
Mine is a direr fate; for I
Of all in wide-spread Troy: of whom
Fifty I had, when first arriv'd
Of these a score complete, save one,
My proper queen: the rest were born
Beneath fierce Ares, most of them
But him who was my only guard
to kin and folk and city,
Him, fighting for his native land,
Hector. And therefore now I seek
From thee his body to redeem,
and for my years have pity,
Who have endur'd, what none beside
of men on earth would venture,
Unto my lips to raise the hand
He spake, and in the hero's heart
For his own sire: he with his hand
Then thickly gush'd the tear from both:
Wept for Patroclus: from the twain
And love of wailing left his frame,
from off his seat he started,
The aged man, and with address
"Ha, wretched sire! troth, many a woe
How hast thou dar'd, alone to seek
The presence of a man, by whom
In deadly strife were slain? in sooth,
But come, repose thee on a seat;
and we, tho' stung with anguish,
For, fruit is none of chilling wail;
The gods a life of grief have spun:
For in the hall of Jupiter
With twofold charge of gifts are fill'd,
So, such a man of fair and foul
But if unmingled ill he give,
And with vile famine driveth him
Thereon to wander, not by gods
So eke to Peleus from his birth
Tho' mortal, from the gods he won
Yet upon him alsó an ill
In-that no ruling race of sons
One only son begat he; — me
Comfort his failing years: for I,
Here sit in Troas' land, a curse
to thee and all thy children.
Thou too, old man, of yore (we hear)
Who didst in sons and wealth excel,
As far as Helle's endless stream
But now, sithence the Heav'nly ones
Bear up; nor droop within thy mind
By grieving for thy bonny son
Nor wilt upraise him, ere thyself
Then aged Priam, peer of gods,
did thus in turn address him:
place me, so long as Hector
but in all speed release him,
And let me see him with my eyes:
Ample, which unto thee we bring.
Safe to thy native land return'd;
and see the light of heaven."
To him Achilles, fleet of foot,
"Old man! no more my temper fret.
Myself am minded: so, to me
The daughter of the Ocean Sire,
Yea, Priam! in my heart I know,
some god from heaven led thee.
For thro' the army mortal none,
Nor lightly of our proper doors
Therefore, no further stir thou up
Lest, tho' within my tents, old man,
Still I endure thee not, but sin,
Before his word the old man quail'd
When death had ta'en Patroclus, most
These from the chariot and wain
And to the tents the herald led,
And on a settle seated him:
They brought the costly ransom in,
Two robes alone behind they left,
So might he duly shroud in them
Then handmaids call'd he forth, and bade
to wash the corpse and oint it,
The sight beholding, vent his ire,
And slay him on the spot, and sin,
And round it cast a mantle fair,
And with his comrades lifted all
if in the halls of Pluto
The tiding come to thee, that I
To his dear father; who in sooth
So spake he: then into his tent
And on a chair of crafty work,
Whence he had risen, seated him;
and spake a word to Priam:
"Even as was thy will, old man,
Upon the pallet is he laid:
and with the dawn of morning
ThSelf shalt see and take him:
now to supper pay we duty.
Who by a doom disastrous lost
Six of them, sons of manly age,
The sons were by Apollo slain
For-that she deem'd herself a peer
Who but to twain gave birth:
but she a mother was to many.
Nine days in carnage lay they,
nor was any left to bury:
For Saturn's child to stones had turn'd
the folk: but on the tenth day
The gods of heaven buried them.
Of food bethought her. Now, I ween,
There she, albeit turn'd by gods
Then, godlike Elder! eke let us
And if to Ilium thou bear
After bewail him: many a tear,
I wis, from thee he claimeth."
So spake Achilles, fleet of foot;
A white fleec'd sheep; and straight his squires
did skin and duly dress it,
And many a canny cutlet slash'd,
And broil'd them all right cleverly
On the good cheer before them laid
of drink and food was ended,
What was his stature and his mien;
how like to gods his presence.
But when, with looking each on each,
Then royal Priam, peer of gods,
do now in speed dismiss me,
That we our spirit may assuage,
At last: for never yet my eyes
Sithence by thy assault my son
Within the cloisters of my yard
But now, behold! I tasted have
the which afore I had not tasted."
So spake he: then Achilles bade
For the old man a bed to lay
Forth issued, and with busy zeal
But if, thro' night's swift-rushing gloom,
should one of these behold thee,
And bear the word to Agamemnon,
Then, to the ransom of the dead
But come, explain thou this to me,
That I so long myself may stay,
Then aged Priam, peer of gods,
did thus in turn address him:
The fuel on the mountains lies afar;
How we within the walls are hemm'd:
Nine days would we my son bewail
But on the tenth would bear him forth,
and give the folk their banquet.
The next day o'er him would we raise
But on the twelfth, if Fate and Need
To him in turn divine Achilles,
"All things shall even so be done,
For, as is thy petition now,
so long the war withhold I."
to calm the old man's terror,
He by the wrist his right hand clasp'd.
Slept Priam and the herald old,
But in the chamber closely pight,
Slumber'd; and by his side was laid
Now all beside, both gods above,
and men with crests of horsetail,
Kept thro' the livelong night repose,
how should he from the galleys
Send forth king Priam, and deceive
So right above his head he stood,
and spake a word of counsel;
"Old man! of ill thou thinkest not,
I ween; to see thee sleeping
Amid the beds of foemen, sith
Now thy dear son hast thou redeem'd,
But, for thyself, while still alive,
The son of Atreus know thee here,
and all the Achaians know it."
Hearing his word, the aged man
And thro' the army guided them
But when unto the ford they reach'd
o'er all the earth was scatter'd,
They to the city drave the car
The mules, behind, the corpse convey'd:
Learn of their coming; neither men

div.note: Thoas, king of Lemnos; the father of Hypsipyle, mother of Eunëus: see 7, 469, and 14, 230.
I do not certainly understand the description of the Greek, but I think spinning, not weaving, is intended.

l.seg: But first Cassandra, like in mien
Standing within his car, and eke
But Hector saw she on the mules,
Thereat she cried aloud with grief,
and wail'd to all the city:
come ye, and look on Hector,
If ye in him alive rejoic'd,
to see him come from battle:
For, troth, a mighty joy was he
to all the folk and city."
So spake she; then not any man
Nor woman: for on all of them
So, near before the gates they met
the wain that bare the carcase.
For Hector, first his consort dear
from off their head the tresses.
And now the wail for Hector
Before the gates the livelong day
till set of sun had lasted,
But from his car the aged man
"Open! and yield my mules a way:
I have my dead brought back, then ye
your hearts shall glut with weeping."
But when within his noble halls
they reach'd, then lodg'd they Hector
Leaders of dirges, who with chant
Fram'd the lament, whilst in accord
led off the dirge of sorrow,
Clasping within her hands the head
"Husband! thou in thy youth of days
art perish'd; and hast left me
A widow in thy halls, and eke
Whom thou and I illfated gave
to light of life: nor deem I
That he to manhood will attain;
Be from the summit riev'd: for thou,
By whom of yore its pleasant wives
Who soon upon the galleys' backs
shall ride, and I among them.
But thou, my child, shalt follow me,
Toiling to serve some foreign lord:
To sad destruction thee shall give,
Full many a man by Hector's skill
Wherefore for him the peoples all
So on thy parents, Hector! thou
Hast brought; but unto me in chief
Nor diddest, dying, from the bed
reach out thy hand to touch me,
Might every day and every night
So spake she weeping: after her,
"Hector, of all my sons wast thou
far to my heart the dearest.
Thou even to the gods wast dear,
And therefore had they care of thee,
Them was he wont for gear to sell
To Samos, or to Imbros; else
But when from thee with lengthy spear
Ruthless he trail'd thee many a time
Nor even so to life restor'd
Now in thy chambers liest thou
from silver bow, had slain thee."
So spake she weeping, and arous'd
Then Helen, after them, the third
led off the dirge of sorrow:
far to my heart the dearest!
Lord now to me is verily
Sithence my native land I left,
Yet never did I hear from thee
But if some other might perchance
Or e'en thy mother; (but thy sire
Alway;) then wouldest thou with words
Therefore lament I both for thee,
in all the breadth of Troas
but all that meet me shudder."
So spake she weeping: after her
Then aged Priam to the folk
When from the dusky galleys back
On us to wreak no noyance, till
So spake he: then beneath their wains
they yok'd the mules and oxen,
But when the Morn a tenth time rose
Then they with weeping bare abroad
And lodg'd upon the topmost pile
his corpse, for flames to kindle.
And soon as they assembling came
With streams of sparkling wine throughout
All plaintive, and adown their cheek
These, wrapt in purple raiment soft,
They lodg'd, and to a hollow pit
And on it rais'd a barrow-mound,
Then, when the tomb was rais'd, again
